---
title: "Ubuntu cannot compete with OS X or Windows 7"
date: 2009-11-29
forum: Recurring Discussions
---

### Post by arrow.69 on 2009-11-29
I'm gonna get incredibly bashed for posting this, but here it goes.

I've been running various Linux distributions on a bunch of computers since I was 13.  Windows would piss me off, Vista got released, or sometimes I just felt like playing around.  I've been through MEPIS, OpenSuse, Kubuntu, Ubuntu,amongst others.  It eventually became clear to me that Ubuntu was the overall best desktop distribution out there, if only for the tremendous amount of support there is out there for it.

I love Ubuntu.  It's a very capable OS and I love the idea of running a completely free and open operating system.  I've probably run some variation of Ubuntu on and off for the past four years.  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

However, I have to admit that Ubuntu simply can't compete anymore.  Windows 7 is a huge leap for Windows, and OS X has always been user-friendly and functional.  I know that under the hood, Linux is more stable, quicker, more secure than Windows.  But the truth is, to the end-user on the desktop front, the experience you get running Ubuntu doesn't measure up.  The problem isn't a lack of applications of features.  Ubuntu just lacks polish and integration.  Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users.  I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability.  Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer.  They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system.  They want it to just work for them.  Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now.

What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

---

I've been through MEPIS, OpenSuse, Kubuntu, Ubuntu,amongst others.  It eventually became clear to me that Ubuntu was the overall best desktop distribution out there, if only for the tremendous amount of support there is out there for it.

I love Ubuntu.  It's a very capable OS and I love the idea of running a completely free and open operating system.  I've probably run some variation of Ubuntu on and off for the past four years.  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

However, I have to admit that Ubuntu simply can't compete anymore.  Windows 7 is a huge leap for Windows, and OS X has always been user-friendly and functional.  I know that under the hood, Linux is more stable, quicker, more secure than Windows.  But the truth is, to the end-user on the desktop front, the experience you get running Ubuntu doesn't measure up.  The problem isn't a lack of applications of features.  Ubuntu just lacks polish and integration.  Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users.  I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability.  Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer.  They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system.  They want it to just work for them.  Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now.

What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

I agree with your ideas, but disagree that it cannot compete.  While I appreciate your thoughts and your candor, ultimately it's just an operating system; just software, no life-altering decisions went into which OS you use.  

If you like Windows or OSX, by all means use them.  The truth is, Linux *is* buggy...but so are the other ones.  No software is perfect, and any attempt to find one is nothing but an exercise in futility.  Rather, try to find (and then use) the OS that is perfect *for you*.  You can't lose if you do that.  :)

Good luck with whatever you decide.  :biggrin:

Boy, we are a long way past that now and in my experience Ubuntu can be set up to run without tinkering if that is what you want.

### Post by PariahVayne on 2009-11-29
Fragmentation will always hold back Linux. Choice is great, but too many choices and it gets confusing, especially for a new user.

---

### Post by Alex Libman on 2009-11-29
Ubuntu can definitely compete on the server, and in "the third-world market" where people can't afford the price of a Microsoft OS (including its ever-growing hardware requirements).  Ubuntu also has the (unfair) advantage of being able to include as much free software as it wants with its OS, while Microsoft is legally restricted by antitrust regulation.  A lot of student / job functions only require a finite set of software packages, and that's the market where Ubuntu could compete very well, and I also predict a bright future for simplified distros like Google's "Chrome OS".

And then you also have off-the-grid Anarcho-Capitalist tax resisters like me, but most of us prefer OpenBSD...  ;)

---

### Post by SunnyRabbiera on 2009-11-29
> **PariahVayne said:**
> Fragmentation will always hold back Linux. Choice is great, but too many choices and it gets confusing, especially for a new user.

You should tell this one to Microsoft though, both Vista and 7 both have one too many choices as well...
XP initially had two versions, Home edition and Professional
Stuff like Media edition and starter edition came later.
Both Vista and 7 have basic, home, home premium, business, enterprise and ultimate.
Cripes thats more then the primary versions of Linux one needs to know about:
Debian/Ubuntu, Redhat/SUSE, Slackware and Gentoo. (though one could equalize it with Arch)

Most of the other spin offs are based on those distros, or a varient of...
Like Mandriva, it is a varient of redhat but is still based on RPM.

Look the OP is only focusing on looks it seems, and popularity.
For me Linux is a very good competitor to both OSX and 7.
And Ubuntu can be a good competitor too, the last two buntus have flaws but hey all OS's have their down periods, including Windows and OSX

### Post by RobotChubby on 2009-11-29
It can compete with Windows, but in some ways it can't match it.  I'm building a gaming rig at the moment, and I'm pretty sure the only way I'm going to be able to play Arkham Asylum et al (in best quality) is by going the Windows route.

But the mere existence of Ubuntu 9.10 and the size of the community proves that it can compete and will continue to do so.

It all depends on the user and their applications.  The ends, in this case, justify the means.

In some ways, that renders the argument irrelevant.

So, er, can't we all just get along?(!)

There is some truth to that but they all have one thing in common;

_**WINDOWS**_ 7 Home Basic

_**WINDOWS**_ 7 Home Premium

_**WINDOWS**_ 7 Ultimate

Windows is far more unified than Linux is almost every aspect. It's strange how many Linux users slate Windows but refuse to acknowledge any of the lingering problems with their chosen O/S.

Ubuntu is great in a lot ways, but it has some very painful flaws. Xserver/PulseAudio/upgrading for example, and it's not quite the 'minority' that have these issues as some people like to pretend. Personally I think ubuntu missed its chance of not capitalizing more when Mircosoft was wounded with Vista. Now it looks like ChromeOS will push it even further back into the shadows.

### Post by craig10x on 2009-11-29
Arrow.69 after reading your post i would have to agree with some of the points you made...i use Ubuntu 9.04...everything works...and it's stable, fast and secure....and has lots of excellent programs on it....And i like it MUCH better then Windows...even in it's current form......It's also quite a relief to get away from the constant problems one can have with Windows...

though it still does lack some "polish" that coming from windows, one would like to see on it.....i'd especially like to see easier ways to downlload files that aren't in the package manager...it's not always so easy to get a deb file for a program (which is the easiest to install on ubuntu)...

and i'd like to have easier ways to upgrade to newer versions of programs in Ubuntu that i am using....

Actually, if you haven't already tried it...have you considered Linux Mint? (version 8 is out now i believe)...They take Ubuntu and polish it up and make it a more familar experience for those coming from windows....and even include all the codecs, flash player, java, etc..already installed...with a nice "minty green" look (lol)...check their website...

Try it...you may like it ;)

### Post by arashiko28 on 2009-11-29
Let's measure the competition this way:

a 34 years enterprise that has manipulated the market, an OS that forces the hardware factories to adapt to them instead of being reverse or at least common agreement, this is just windows...

OS X, they just sell you the new computer adjusted to the needs of the software...

Now, Ubuntu is a 5 years old OS that has run a great distance and won many battles on the market field and broke record... Not to mention being open source which I completely support...

In my case the toddler has beaten the adult. Ubuntu is up to the everyday needs of the average user, it's easy to use, even kids can have their ways on it!
A few months ago there was this poll among Linux users and gamers about if they were willing to pay for a game that can be played in linux even though you could get the game for free, most users didn't supported the idea, they want it all for free, it's not an utopia, development takes money, and older OS's have it, so in some areas they have the lead.

I'd rather to have a computer that works for me than me working for a computer.

### Post by Methuselah on 2009-11-29
Funny, for me windows cannot compete.
Whatever issues I might have, running windows is not really a solution; It is more trouble than it is worth.
The amount of calls I get from friends to fix machines that run an OS I don't even use anymore suggests that using windows isn't even a buy it and forget it proposition.

But on a certain level I agree that linux is designed by hackers for hackers (note: not crackers!).
Free software aims to remove boundaries and restrictions that the average user might not even perceive.
So, for many of them the advantage of running linux might not be so obvious.

IMO, the best candidates for running linux are the people who love to tinker and the people who have very limited requirements.
The former will obviously be in paradise and the latter will find a very stable OS that won't break on them while they browse the web or check email.
Possibly the worst migration candidates are the windows power users who are used to everything the way it is and like it that way.
They are also the quickest to think they can master another OS easily and be bitterly disappointed, even angry, when they realise they have much to learn.

---

### Post by User3k on 2009-11-30
OS x - Not sure, never tried it.

Windows 7 - Had it, the evaluation, and deleted it after several months of trying it. As far as drivers go out of the box, depends on what you mean. If it is bundled software, for example a Compaq with Windows 7 bundled together with Compaq's stuff, etc, then it will work. But get a new computer without anything installed on it. Then buy the normal Windows 7 Ultimate, or which ever one you choose, and let me know how much hardware works out of the box on your brand new computer. I did this with 98, XP and Vista. The longer they are out, the more hardware advances, then they start to lag behind. Also Flash, Java, etc never worked out of the box for me with Windows in general. I had to download them from their websites for XP and Vista, same goes for DvD players and such.

Besides I refuse to pay for yet another Windows OS. The prices keep going up and I am not impressed enough to save up  for it, yeah I am that broke where I would have to save up for it.

Ubuntu and my other favorite Suse are the ones I use only now, actually Ubuntu is the only one I have installed at the moment.

### Post by Riffer on 2009-11-30
Define "compete", if you mean marketing then of course Ubuntu can't.  Simple fact is that canonical doesn't have the cash for a 10's of millions dollar campaigns.  All Canoncial can do is provide the best OS that it can and know people will come over.  This strategy has worked well.

If you mean compete to mean usability and aesthetics then its in the eye of the beholder.  I find Windows to be a bit of a mess, the start menu is jumbled together, basic system tools have to be dug for and basic desktop configuration is some what limited.  

As for Macs, I find them counter intuitive, I just never got the "Mac" way of doing things.  Yes people will say that that if I gave Macs a longer chance I might of gotten it, but at the end of the day Macs never gave me the alternative that I was looking for.

With Ubuntu I have an OS that lets me tweak the desktop as much or as little as I want.  I have access to tons of great programs, and the commercial ones like autodesk or adobe that only run on the other 2 OSes I found I really don't need them.  Ubuntu delivers all that I want well.

### Post by RobotChubby on 2009-11-30
Is there any evidence of how *aware* general PC users are of Linux?  The people in Britain who were queuing up outside PC world to buy Windows 7 last month probably wouldn't have a clue what it is.  I feel that - certainly over here - it's only really the tech savvy or business users who know about Linux.

Also I feel that part of it's strength lies in it's small community appeal.  If you're big, like Windows, then you are clearly more of a target for Malware (although from what little I know about the code, I understand that in some aspects Linux is inherently more secure).

And yeah, polish does equate with eye-candy, but perhaps also with ease of use.  Those PC World shoppers want to stay on the consumer treadmill, but they want the tech to be really simple so that it works perfectly when they're showing it off to the Jones's.

### Post by arrow.69 on 2009-11-30
Wow.  Didn't realize my post would elicit such a reaction.

I'll try to reply to most of the ideas which were expressed by a bunch of you.

Define polish:  When I talk about polish, I do not mean eye candy or anything like that.  If anything I believe Compiz takes away from Gnome's polish.  What I'm trying to say is that other OSes look and feel more complete and unified.  You feel automatically at ease and comfortable.  And that's not just because one is used to Windows.  You get that instantly on OS X.  For me, a computer is a place where I study, work, socialize, live.  I love how I can personalize the Linux GUI and make it mine.  However, I'm no designer and not that great of a programmer.  I don't believe that Windows or OS X are making the perfect OS for me.  However they do a good enough job of making an okay OS for most people.  I think the problem most people have with Ubuntu is they don't have the skill or time to put in to make their Ubuntu configuration perfect.

Also, alot of comments out there about different bugs showing up randomly on different installs. That is a major annoyance.  In windows, when you're computer crashes and you click end now don't send end now don't send 300 times it's probably the stupidest and most annoying error ever.  However it's consistent and you know how to deal with it.  When I resize VLC and X crashes, I reboot and my xorg.conf file is broken.  That's not so fun.  When I boot up my computer and certain menu items are moving around.  Not so fun.  I don't mean for you guys to help me fix those bugs.  I can look around and figure them out.  But I just think the huge amount of bugs is what stops most users from enjoying Ubuntu to its fullest.

I read a few comments about Linux Mint.  That's actually the distro I'm running right now.  It's not too bad, although honestly to me it just feels like ubuntu with a few extra packages I could install on my own, and a theme I don't really like that much.  It's not bad, but it's not by any means revolutionary.  And it doesn't fix the issues I have with 'polish' in Ubuntu.  The thing is I'm really not talking about appearances and eye candy when I mention polish.  I mean feeling at ease and comfortable inside your OS.  My ubuntu install looks beautiful in a screen shot, but clunky when you actually have to use it for a while.

I'd like to conclude by saying that I don't hate Ubuntu.  In fact I keep coming back to it because it is making progress and I do love the idea of running open source software.  Also Windows tends to piss me off.  Linux has a wonderful database of free apps and an enthusiastic community.  As a person who likes to tinker with my computer and have it work exactly my way, there's no replacement for it.  However, I can't shake the feeling that Windows in my experience is so much easier to use, especially 7 which has really clever UI features.  When I say Ubuntu can't compete, I mean that 95% of users can't see a use for it.  Which is a shame because my mother loves the Ubuntu Netbook Remix, and it would be perfect for half the computer users I know.  Anyways.  I do like Ubuntu and Linux in general.  I'm mainly upset because I don't believe Karmic stacks up to any other OS out there any more.  It was viable back when Vista was the other option, but honestly 7 eclipses Ubuntu in many ways.  I do hope Ubuntu gets better over the years and I can keep dipping my toe into Linux waters until I completely make the switch.  In the mean time I've learnt a whole lot about Linux, and programming anyways, and I had a bunch of fun doing it.  So I'm not too worried about it.

---

### Post by tuxeee on 2009-11-30
I too agree that linux (ubuntu) needs that 'polish' and by that i do not mean turning towards windows.

All i mean by polishing is that its at a position now (stable enough) to start fitting into the lifestyle of people, and as you know we are all becoming more and more lazy! we want things to be done for us....so an example of polishing is:

- 1 click installation software (and afew more clicks and its done)

- The new ubuntu software is a charm! and should include more options in that (good examples is flash player, VLC and all other codecs etc.

- Appearance schemes; now finding an appearance off  gnome-look and getting it to install is way too hard for what your really achieving!

- Alot of fixes here and there: Compiz feature are not that easy to use without google being your friend, i dont think you can manage many things (being an average user) on your own.

Now if you dump hundreds of thousands of dosh into this OS, then it would have that 'polish' that people refer to...at one point windows should of stop trying to progress and actually fix everything they decided to skip cause they were trying to make a newer version and more money. thats what linux needs instead of 100s of distros being developed.

### Post by Glucklich on 2009-11-30
> **Aflack said:**
> What holds linux back for me is the extreme feeling of unpolished-ness... if that's a word.

I was talking about this the other day and about a very interesting phenomena that is... the most significant development image wise on Linux distro's, come from derivatives. I remember that Studio had a pretty polished look when comparing it to regular Ubuntu. The guys at Mint feels like they are trying to look for something. Expanding their horizons would certainly give interesting results. And finally, my personal favorite, despite not being for any user with the openbox menu and whatnot... but still, CrunchBang. I just love it. Though, when the user is looking for visual bling bling, I just show them Kubuntu. That thing is guaranteed to get anyone hooked.
Anyway, this phenomena is only possible because the derivatives don't have to worry about the working/not working problem. Therefore, I wouldn't expect much development on that area on the main distro, unless they get some guys to specifically work on the OS's interaction with the user in search of new grounds.

### Post by Glucklich on 2009-11-30
I agree that if the folks of CrunchBang worked with Ubuntu, it would definitely be more polished. But I don't know if what you are saying is feasible. Well, at least without having a full DVD image or without spending unnecessary network bandwidth. One thing is to have collaborations, another completely different is to have all distros in one (because we are talking about an absurd ammount of distros). They are based on Ubuntu but they follow a different philosophy because, in some cases, it's not only a theme thing. For example, CrunchBang uses OpenBox as the default Window Manager (instead of Gnome). That makes it considerably lighter on the resources but (in my opinion) relatively harder for new users. Basically, it just takes advantages of the Ubuntu's hardware recognition. And it certainly doesn't have the packages compromises Ubuntu has due to the newbie usability, but it's Linux and that is always customizable.

### Post by Tamlynmac on 2009-11-30
[> ]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=324859")[arashiko28]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=324859")
I'd rather to have a computer that works for me than me working for a computer.

Agreed.

The simple act of comparing Windows to Ubuntu/Linux implies that Ubuntu is seen as a competitive threat. Yet, some continue to post arguments informing us that Ubuntu poses "no threat". If Ubuntu is not seen as a threat, why waste time posting on this forum and arguing the benefits of Windows? Ask yourself, how much time and energy you would invest/waste going to a Windows forum and promoting Ubuntu/Linux?

I truly believe some hardcore Windows advocates do recognize Ubuntu as a threat and feel compelled to come here and defend/promote Windows. I laugh when reading threads of this type, for they re-enforce my beliefs. As more individuals recognize that Ubuntu/Linux is a free alternative to Windows for their home computing needs, the Windows advocates will become even more proactive. While constantly berating Ubuntu/Linux, they will maintain that it's not a threat. Seems some form of reverse logic. :confused:

The one argument that appears to stifle them, is the price tag. Keep in mind that not only does the number one OS charge for the use privilege, it also represents a substantial market of profitable applications required to accomplish most any task within it's environment.

I suggest, instead of providing these individuals with a platform to spew their spam, we take the high road. Ignoring their attempts to engage in unsubstantiated, opinion based dialog and simply laugh at them - sympathizing with they're plight. Knowing why they're here and asking ourselves if engaging with them is worth the effort. Last I checked, all our systems are stable and fully functional (as always). I feel no insecurity or need to defend what works. I actually take comfort in knowing that I no longer support a mega-monopoly.

Please, consider responding to these individuals and what benefit(s) the forum derives from said communications. Recognize that potentially these individuals may represent other interests and that said interests may have an agenda. Perhaps, if we stop providing them with feedback they may realize that they represent no threat to the Ubuntu/Linux community and are simply preaching to their own choir. I for one, find their actions humorous and pathetic. :(

Just my $0.02

---

And because, most people (including myself) migrated from Windows to Linux.

### Post by julianb on 2009-11-30
> What do you guys think? Am I missing something? Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

You're missing something, which is that open source software faces a critical-mass situation, and that is to our advantage.

The number of Ubuntu users and free software users is growing at a significant rate. The software keeps becoming easier for a total beginner to use, because many of us think that's a goal worth working on. 

A certain percentage of free software users will always be learning to be "power users". Among power users, a substantial percentage will always be finding an issue they want to fix, whether it's making something easier for beginners, or making it possible to accomplish a task faster as an advanced user. (or making a task doable at all.) 

As more and more people use open source software, it will continue to get easier and more useful. Which will mean more folks will use it.

### Post by scottuss on 2009-11-30
Quoted from OP:

"Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users. I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability. Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer. They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system. They want it to just work for them. Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now."

I could say exactly the same as above but substitute the word Ubuntu for Windows.

I support Windows, Macs and Linux and overall I have to say Windows needs the most tinkering. Just because it's usually done by an IT guy or support technician, not the owner of the computer doesn't mean it doesn't need doing. I can't remember the last time I had to defrag, edit my registry and clean up some malware on any of my Linux boxes... oh wait.

I'm not having a go at Windows or Mac OS X, they keep me in business and they have their uses for some people, but all I'm saying is that actually, your statements hold some truth from the other side.

### Post by dnvikram on 2009-11-30
One of the posters rightly said that,"if Canonical/Ubuntu had put a price tag for the OS like Microsoft,then it would have kicked Windows & OSX's teeth in."

Very aptly said.One has to take into consideration many factors influencing the development of an OS.Its not just White & Black (or) Good & Bad.I am not personally a Windows basher.There are things I like and hate about every OS out there.In my response I will only talk about Ubuntu Desktop Edition>There is no need for me to reinforce the capabilities and beauty of Ubuntu Server.

Does anyone remember how primitive Windows 3.x used to look when it first released?In today's standards,would you call Windows 3.x user friendly?Of course No!!!.It took almost 20 Years for Windows to evolve and Microsoft had to spend billions of $$$ to bring it to the stage at which Windows 7 is right now.Thousands of well paid developers,testers,integrators,sales,support & hardware vendors,etc worked round the clock all these years for MS to get to this point.And Microsoft also had to swallow the debacle of Vista to get Windows 7 right,with all its muscle,might and experience in its usability..Yes it is one of the most user friendly OSes out there and very polished in terms of its GUI & certainly has killer looks,can run awesome 3d games,directx10,etc etc.But at what cost?At the cost of its security and stability.Windows was built keeping a single user in mind and everyone knows its not as scalable or robust as Ubuntu or Linux per se.Linux on the contrary wasnt built keeping a single user in mind.It was invented solely for different reasons.But,even its evolving and growing the same way a baby or a child evolves mentally and physically.Its the only OS which came last in the OS market and is now almost leading the pack in any field or domain you name it.I agree,it has still lot of catching up to do with Windows 7.But,remember this Baby is growing fast and learning fast like a Prodigy.Remember those IBM Ads featuring a young boy???So apt.And coming to OSX,I wouldnt want to waste my time talking about that.Yes,its a very user friendly OS and got lot of eye candy,blah blah blah.But,remember buying a MAC PC means you are buying yourself into a vendor lock in and lose ,"The Freedom" to do get under the hood.Upon that Apple has a tight control over the hardware & software and charges a steep price for its PC.So,even in this case your so called,"Polished Look/Features" comes at a huge price.

But,with Ubuntu I agree one has to spend some time cutomizing the installation and tweaking around to get it to one's liking.Now,thats a perogative of an individual and his choice.You have to acknowledge the fact that in the last 5 years Ubuntu has become more and more ,"Out Of Box" and is trying to perfect that on every piece of hardware & not just the so called,"Certified Hardware" (or) "Windows 7 Certified" ones and its free of cost.So,yes there is a trade off and there are many people who can live with that and infact enjoy spending time customizing their Ubuntu they way they decorate or customize their new car or house.Once thats done,you can be rest assured that its in place and rock solid unless you fat finger something very stupidly.But,one cant assure the same about Windows.Can you definitively?I am sure you cannot.But,as a Ubuntu user,I can definitively say that ,"My Ubuntu will not break once its set".

Finally,all I would like to say is,every OS has its own merits & demerits.Being a hard core Unix/Linux devotee & a full time SysAdmin,even I use Windows and there are things I love about it and hate about it.It took almost 2 decades for Windows to get Polished.Just,give Ubuntu half of that time and I am sure your perception and opinion will change.If you see a Prodigy,you know he/she is destined to become a Genius...Just give it time and if really considerate,try donating some spare Greenbacks to the Ubuntu Community.Who knows,your $$ may end up in ,"Polishing" it.

All the best!!

Think rationally & Speak Ocassionally.

A Faithful Ubuntu'er

---

### Post by Windows Nerd on 2009-11-30
We are are talking about the desktop side of computing here, but what about server side? Servers are important too. Linux has _way_ more market share on the server side. And it is way more stable server side, too, from what my buddies that work with servers say, than compared to Windows server edition. Ubuntu (or any distro for that matter) can also maintain a much longer (almost infinite) uptime. 

Did I also mention that on various mobile phones and devices use Linux? Android, the iTouch/iPhone, and other smart phones that many people use nowadays are massively popular among the population and competing very well with the Palm Phones (which run a version of the Windows OS) or BlackBerrys.

So next time you say Ubuntu/Linux cannot compete with OS X or Windows, consider all aspects of computing, not just the desktop side.

Scott

### Post by hoppipolla on 2009-11-30
> **arrow.69 said:**
> I'm gonna get incredibly bashed for posting this, but here it goes.

I've been running various Linux distributions on a bunch of computers since I was 13.  Windows would piss me off, Vista got released, or sometimes I just felt like playing around.  I've been through MEPIS, OpenSuse, Kubuntu, Ubuntu,amongst others.  It eventually became clear to me that Ubuntu was the overall best desktop distribution out there, if only for the tremendous amount of support there is out there for it.

I love Ubuntu.  It's a very capable OS and I love the idea of running a completely free and open operating system.  I've probably run some variation of Ubuntu on and off for the past four years.  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

However, I have to admit that Ubuntu simply can't compete anymore.  Windows 7 is a huge leap for Windows, and OS X has always been user-friendly and functional.  I know that under the hood, Linux is more stable, quicker, more secure than Windows.  But the truth is, to the end-user on the desktop front, the experience you get running Ubuntu doesn't measure up.  The problem isn't a lack of applications of features.  Ubuntu just lacks polish and integration.  Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users.  I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability.  Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer.  They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system.  They want it to just work for them.  Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now.

What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

I dunno man, like I've said before I'm still waiting for Windows and Mac to catch up with what is being achieved by open source projects such as KDE.

I agree that Ubuntu with Gnome looks a little rough compared to 7 or OSX, but I think the branding and user-friendly approach should still win across enough people to make it viable as a project, and maybe even kick off the kind of "snowball" effect that we need.

However, unless Gnome 3 is something special, I personally believe KDE may prove to be the future of open source desktops, at least for a foreseeable period! :)

### Post by ElSlunko on 2009-11-30
The reason I like W7 so much now is because they took so many pages out of the book from Linux. I found it rad that it downloaded my video driver for me! Sweet. It also got me other drivers from the internet & codecs automagically! Sweet. No more having to visit a bunch of sites and download a bunch of separate files. The customization was a nice touch to the overall look! Sweet. Widget repo that didn't suck and was easy to access! Sweet. Lots of 3rd party programs that create a Repo like interface for many popular programs. Sweet.

These things are awesome, but I've had them for a couple of years. I'm not going to jump ship when a product gets as cool as linux ;P. Especially since there won't be any major changes beyond the "new shiny toy" phase.

---

### Post by ElSlunko on 2009-11-30
and one more thought before I go pick up dinner!

One thing I lament is the "support" issue. Windows has created a good product just like all the developers behind the Linux Distro's. Both OS's see eye to eye from base install to me & it's history & money that powers Microsoft. You can see their influence as either investment or strong-arming the market. But when it comes down to the base install either OS has its merits and faults. It's when you go into 3rd party interaction that Linux begins to dissolve.

This mindset that some godly power has made one OS better than the other is stupid. This idea that either is inherintly better than the other is stupid. There are market & historic forces at work & it's difficult for Linux to overcome, BUT IT HAS BEEN DOING IT WITH MUCH SUCCESS. I know this to be true because I have both, and spend most of my time on Ubuntu.

So before you begin to debate this in your head you have to take many factors into account because it doesn't come down to which is more 1337.

---

### Post by arrow.69 on 2009-12-01
I think I finally understand why I think Ubuntu will never be as good an OS as other proprietary OSes.

All Linux distros are designed, programmed, etc.  by engineers, programmers, with other engineers and programmers in mind.  That's fine, and I don't think that Linux necessarily needs to appeal to the same demographic of users as other OSes.

However, I'm sick of hearing thread after thread from Linux gurus explaining to someone contemplating a switch how Linux is the best OS for 100 reasons.  It's simply not true.  Hands down, Linux requires more work, is more buggy, and not necessarily as user-friendly.  You don't need to remind me of the 200 advantages Linux offers.  I'm there, I use Linux on a daily basis, I know.  I just think alot of people on here are missing the point entirely as to how most people see computers, what they want out of them, and what they consider a 'good OS'.  Let me give you an example.  I'm installing Kubuntu on my computer and my girlfriend who knows nothing about computers happens to be around.  I ask her what she thinks of the installer GUI, which I thought looks really sleek:  "I think it looks childish.  Like it was designed for 5 year olds or something."  I didn't get it.  But the point is, Linux just doesn't cut it for most users, and alot of users fail to see what's missing.  Since the users developing for Ubuntu do not have most computer users in mind, I don't think it will ever be competitive in the desktop area.  ( I know, Linux doesn't really 'compete'.  Whatever.)

---

tell her that she then doesnt have to worry about spyware, virus', a computer that after 6 months will take 2mins to load and another minute to get into facebook AND THEN! see what she'll say... :)

the average user flames windows cause it becomes time consuming, Slower and Slower! and they feel that they need to update hardware etc. which they dont really

### Post by ElSlunko on 2009-12-01
Since we've got all these opinions and experiences flying around I'd like to add that the two W7 installs I did both had their problems. W7 basically killed my NIC & I had to flash my bios to get it back. Luckily I found the DIY solution since many people with my mobo had to send their mobo back. I had JUST gotten it built & was really upset when my NIC's had dissapeared for no apparnet reason.

2nd install was on my friends lappy. Everything went great & he was very much in love with the new life given to his old laptop. Well he calls me up a week later and says that he installed an AV & now his PC won't shutdown completely. He's uninstalled it along with everything else he's installed since I gave it to him and no dice. His laptop goes black on shutdown but never powerse completely off save having to hold down the power button.

These aren't localized issues, these have occured to many folks. I'm sure these are just two of the many issues that new W7 installs have faced. A recent update from Windows even borked a lot of people's system so every OS has it's bugs.

It's ****ing ridiculous to say linux or windows is smooth. Both eat themselves alive every now and then. OSX does too! (just not as often since they have control over the hardware that their OS is installed on that would make MS jelous.)

### Post by Zoot7 on 2009-12-01
The only reason IMO Ubuntu or any other distro can't compete (for market share that is) against either Windows or OSX (mind you, one could argue that it's market share is not too far off OSX), is because of application support and support from hardware vendors. And neither the Software Vendors or Hardware Vendors are going to ever step up to the plate 100% as long as the Linux desktop is as fragmented as it is.
Sadly it's the nature of Open Source, in a capitalist world it ain't going to make the money fast enough.
Red Hat shared the same sentiments exactly when they said they'd never get into the Desktop market with Linux.

Granted though, Linux (in general) has a huge amount of advantages over Windows (not as much OSX), and it's getting better as time goes on. So I'll always be using some variant of it as my main OS from here on out.

My 0.02...

### Post by Frak on 2009-12-01
So, here are the main points I have read so far:

1. The GUI options are amazing
2. I can turn my computer into a web server at any time I want.
3. The source code is open.
4. Various stories about Windows that either have nothing to do with Windows or are entirely fabricated.
5. Dismissal of Windows because it comes in various versions catering to the specific need.
6. LINUX IS TEH WINZ!
7. Comparing today's release of Ubuntu to Windows XP, a nearly decade-old OS.
8. Berating Windows for viruses and spyware, which is not a product of Microsoft nor something they want, also something they actively defend against through Microsoft Security Essentials (nearly as good as commercial alternatives, but runs on much less resources) and Microsoft Update.
9. Berate Windows for consuming cheap resources to increase performance.
10. The kicker, claiming that Linux is gaining marketshare rapidly.

---

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2009-12-01
Ubuntu isn't intuitive? It's ridiculously simple. Kubuntu, maybe, but Ubuntu's interface is straightforward. I admit that the default theme could be better, but they include a really nice theme by default, Dust.

The desktop is very usable and easily changed, but for the basic user, I can guarantee you Ubuntu is easier to get around than any version of Windows or OS X for a beginning computer user.

I mean, I would agree with you if I didn't have friends and relatives who keep asking me to show it to them, then decide to use it long term. They seem to feel it has higher quality, and compatibility isn't a problem for them.

I think (just my opinion) sometimes, because of file formats or a job, you have to use a piece of Windows software that doesn't work in Wine yet. So then you're compelled to believe that Linux isn't "good enough."

However, it all depends on your experience. There are many Linux-only applications I just can't do without anymore. Linux applications are faster, smaller, and more reactive than Windows applications. They also have a consistent and integrated GUI for the most part, and get to the point.

Doesn't that make them high quality? Is Banshee "low-quality" just because it isn't Windows Media Player or iTunes? To me, iTunes is low quality because it can't sync oggs to an mp3-only player. It lacks basic media player features.

But, since you seem to like Ubuntu enough to keep trying it, let me make a suggestion. You don't have to choose just one or the other. If you have a newer computer, you can simply run Windows XP in seamless mode in a VirtualBox without even needing to dual-boot. Also, PortableUbuntu on a faster computer is also a viable option, or even Ubuntu in a VirtualBox on Windows. Linux is free, and there are many ways to live with it and Windows.

Another thing I think would be good to realize is that no one's forcing you to use it. Linux does the job, regardless of its market share. Unless you're a bug tester or developer, Linux doesn't NEED you to use it. So come back in a few years and maybe things will be even better for your needs, with application availability and Wine compatibility growing every day.

Sometimes I feel we take Linux for granted. Expecting too much of something just because it continually exceeds expectations.

### Post by beloved88 on 2009-12-01
Linux and other open source was assessed as Microsoft's #2 threat after a bad economy.  Competing with Windows 7 does not mean it has to have a huge market share or be equal in the eyes of the average user, because it's not.  But it's the fear factor that FOSS software strikes in the hearts of MS engineers that is one compelling factor for working hard and effectively.  

Also, we seem to be ignoring emerging markets.  FOSS is going to be huge in places like Cambodia where no-one can afford Gate's licenses, but older and cheaper technology is filtering down to them.  They're getting all our old MS boxes and cheap chinese MIPS machines and installing Linux (OpenSUSE is VERY popular in Cambodia).  But they just don't have the infrastucture to support wide usage of broadband so their activities go completely un-accounted for in market share statistics based off of web activity.

### Post by beloved88 on 2009-12-01
I lived in Cambodia for two years.  In a number of provinces, all schools and government offices use a localized version of OpenSUSE. They chose this because support for typing in Khmer Unicode &#6026;&#6076;&#6021;&#6023;&#6070;&#6050;&#6025;&#6098;&#6021;&#6073;&#6020; (just like that) was initially better supported under Linux.  And most people that now know how to type using that stardard layout, like me, highly dislike Vista's and 7's khmer keyboard layout, because it doesn't suit the need of the language very well, like the need for non-gap spaces and such.  go to [http://www.khmeros.info/drupal612/](http://www.khmeros.info/drupal612/) for more info

### Post by beloved88 on 2009-12-01
I actually tried for many days in total vain to install unicode support for my parents XP box, and it still won't render the vowel right (ie it would look like this &#6031;&#8203;&#6078; when it was supposed to look like this &#6031;&#6078;, vowels are often preceding, above, below, or following the vowel that they modify.  And when ever i would go to print, word would crash and burn in a furry of error messages, and i would have to reboot in some cases)  With Ubuntu, i didn't have to go searching the web and download anything, never touched the CLI, just used the GUI and picked Khmer as one of my layouts, and OpenOffice writer handles it great, and my HP printer is networked to my parent's computer and the wireless router, so i just hit print, and samba does a pretty good job of getting it to the printer from my netbook.  Life couldn't be better with Ubuntu.

### Post by arrow.69 on 2009-12-01
> **murderslastcrow said:**
> Ubuntu isn't intuitive? It's ridiculously simple. Kubuntu, maybe, but Ubuntu's interface is straightforward. I admit that the default theme could be better, but they include a really nice theme by default, Dust.

The desktop is very usable and easily changed, but for the basic user, I can guarantee you Ubuntu is easier to get around than any version of Windows or OS X for a beginning computer user.
.


No.  I'm sorry.  You make good points in the rest of your post but this I can`t accept.  Ubuntu is user-friendly at first, when you`re lucky.

Try to explain to a normal user how to set up dual-head (two monitors) in Ubuntu.  Just try.

Try to explain to a user why when he resizes a VLC window the computer completely crashes, and when it reboots, the resolution is wrong.

Try to explain to this same user why the 3D games he downloaded off Synpatics don`t run.

Try to explain to him that you can't edit some setting without going into the terminal and editing some scary configuration file.

Explain why resizing windows slows down your computer so much (Compiz)

Explain why the default theme is so ugly and why you have to jump through all these hoops to make it prettier.

Finally, after this user's been using Linux for a few months.  Explain to him how using Linux has made his life easier.


I don't expect Ubuntu to be perfect.  I understand that it's a young, evolving OS, I understand the limits of open source.  That's fine.  But I think that the Ubuntu community would benefit from some humility.  Your OS is far from perfect.  It is much more buggy and less usable than other options.  Yes, even for doing simple tasks, Ubuntu feels buggy and unpolished.  Even to the typical user.  Ubuntu has accomplished a lot in the last few years, and I'm confident it can do better.  But enough of this pride and smugness.  Ubuntu is broken on many important levels, and we need to work as a community to fix it.

P.S.  Whoever mentioned that the Ubuntu community doesn't need me, you're an idiot.  That's exactly the kind of comment that scares away users and gives Linux the reputation of being only for h4x0rs and pwn3rs.  We all know Ubuntu benefits from every user it picks up.  As Linux becomes more popular, support for the OS increases, the OS becomes more usable, everyone is happy.

### Post by entangled on 2009-12-02
Windows 7 is pretty much the same as Vista. It is being accepted now because people have had time to adjust to the Vista style desktop and because they want to confirm loyalty to MS (leader-follower relationship in the MS clan).

My job is to develop, administer and integrate Windows systems in networks. They mostly work, they have plenty of problems but there is always information around to fix them. My home Linux systems are functionally equivalent to Windows but are have less artistically designed desktops and less robust apps. The main gripe I have is that Linux documentation is very poor compared with Windows. The man pages are almost useless, usage info is very brief and out of date. This lack of documentation gives Linux a poor image to developers and administrators. The existence of Ubuntu web info is a major assistance to Linux.

I think the main reason Windows is 'competitive' is because it is bundled, apparently 'free', with almost all new systems. Other customer choices follow from this, such as using Internet Explorer, MS Office or Photoshop. My point is that Windows has the market through bundling deals. Linux  cannot improve market share until this practice stops.

---

### Post by scottuss on 2009-12-02
Reading all the posts on this thread is really reiterating one thing in my mind.

Use what's best for you. No one is right or wrong.

Ubuntu can't compete with OS X or Windows 7.
Ubuntu can compete with OS X or Windows 7.

Whatever. For me, personally, Ubuntu is a much better choice and easily beats Windows and Mac OS X, but for some people, this isn't going to be the case.


You can't convince someone who has had nothing but problems with Ubuntu that it's better than Windows and Mac OS X.

Likewise, you can't convince someone (myself included) that has had nothing but problems with Windows or Mac OS X that they're better than Ubuntu.

Just use what you need to!

Whatever your opinion, consider the opposite for a second, smile and get on with your life.

---

### Post by Jefferythewind on 2009-12-02
[PHP]Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer. They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system. They want it to just work for them.[/PHP]

  I think that once i get ubuntu installed, updated, and all set with the right programs, it is very stable and easy to use.  I have done that for a few friends and they haven't really had big problems with it.  For me, personally, the main reason ubuntu is difficult is when i want to run something designed for windows on linux.  And i can't really blame the ubuntu developers for that.  For example, there is no linux distro for itunes.  That just seems crazy to me.  Why does apple care if someone uses ubuntu, or windows to run their ipod? they aren't using apple hardware anyways.
  I think that the job for Ubuntu developers is much harder than for windows and Mac because they have to make ubuntu compatible to other systems, hopefully every system combination in the world.  that is a hard job.  Windows and mac developers know exactly what hardware they are running, and the hardware companies also work with windows to make it all compatible.  It is a much bigger job for ubuntu.  Given all of this i still think ubuntu works great for me.
  At the very end you have to look at the ubuntu guarantee:

"Ubuntu is and always will be free of charge. You do not pay any licensing fees. You can download, use and share Ubuntu with your friends, family, school or business for absolutely nothing."

That is amazing to me, and i love that ubuntu already works so well, i hope that as time goes by we can all add to its completeness and usability.

### Post by Roasted on 2009-12-02
I've used OSX extensively. I also have OSX on an Apple laptop. And even still when I use it, I feel extremely limited. I find myself having a hard time trying to get simple things done. 

Although I feel limited in Windows, at least I know I can get my work done.

But with Ubuntu, I have the ability to customize my operating system exactly to my needs. Not only that, but while providing stability, reliability, and ease of use, it's really a no brainer why all of us are still here as faithful Ubuntu users.

Even today after using Ubuntu for a few years, sometimes I just find myself sitting back at my computer desk. On the right, my spot for my Apple laptop. On the left, my spare computer running various Windows operating systems. And dead center, my main rig, with Kubuntu 9.04. And yet I still cannot fathom how something free can surpass (in my opinion) the other two paid options sitting right next to it.

Just my .02.

### Post by javadog on 2009-12-03
Hi

I am in two minds.

I have recently moved my work pc over to Linux 9.04 and now 9.10. An I am very impressed, I have the basic stuff I need and we work primarily with Lotus Notes that once all the font and work space issues have been resolved is a dream in terms of speed and stability. I had Vista on this machine and then upgraded to Win 7. Which initial was impressive and then just died and became very slow. So so far Ubuntu is king. 

However on my home pc which was a monster in its day and is still very powerful, I will not move off Vista Ultimate, that machine is a gaming machine and from what I have read in terms of Ubuntu games etc, I just do not see the benefit. 

I do believe that users want a easy to use interface and initially Ubuntu doesn't offer this, telling an end user to go into console type sudo apt-get etc... just won't happen when with Windows every thing 'works' yet I believe that the IT departments etc should set this all up. The way my machine is now, no user would complain until he wants to install games on his work machine. 

my 2 sents

Don't mistake familiarity with ease of use. In many aspects, Windows is actually harder to use, with lots of embeded interfaces and hidden option. Here's what one has to do after reinstalling Windows:

 - Install updates, reboot, install more updates, reboot, etc.
 - Download and install AV, anispiware, reboot, update
 - Download and install codecs and programs
 - Set up periferials if any
 - Defragment
 Dare I say, that will take most of the day.

Let's say you just reinstalled Ubuntu:

 - Update, install restricted drivers, reboot
 - install software, codecs (codecs are automated)
If you are on a fast connection, you are done in an hour. sudo apt-get is one way of doing things, but you could also go with Add/Remove or Synaptic.

### Post by Diluted on 2009-12-03

 - Download and install codecs and programs
 - Set up periferials if any
 - Defragment

### Post by scottuss on 2009-12-03
> **Frak said:**
> Trust me, if Ubuntu were as mainstream, it would have the same issues. Startup programs exist there as well.

No it wouldn't, at least not to the same extent. The way the two operating systems are built are significantly different and thus the same problems Windows has would not be found verbatim on Ubuntu (or other Linux distros)

This argument is true for the other myth that "if Ubuntu were mainstream there would be as many viruses for it as Windows" - This is false because although the number of attempts would increase (and there would be some success) the underlying architecture is much more resilient to malware.

Stop throwing around the "market share protects us" argument already!

(I agree that it does, but only to a certain extent) :p

---

They're not that different in how they approach solutions. Ubuntu uses startup programs too. You can throw around the "OMG DEY R SOOO DIFFRNT!" argument all day, but in the end, they REALLY aren't.

EDIT
Also, Open Source is not an automatic safety zone from malware. Let's say Ubuntu reaches the same level as Windows, it will have malware. There aren't enough people to patch the holes in time.

--


Yesterday I've noticed that I hadn't actually tried Ubuntu 9.10, so I read a few reviews and decided to take a shot at it. I boot in Ubuntu Live and decided to open Empathy. Error pops up about Something Telepathy crashing... "Close" it re opens... "Close"... again.. So I quit Empathy. Go on Firefox, download Opera 10.10, install it. Then I decide to go ahead and install Ubuntu. Everything works fine. Boot in Ubuntu, open Empathy - no errors. Okay, that's good. Download Opera 10.10 try to install it, get an error. Lyb3.3 >= (something like that). Really? So running Ubuntu from the CD had a newer version of Lyb (or whatever) than the installation? Okay. I'll just update and hope it updates. Where's the update notification? Okay, System > Administration > Update Manager (wow just as I'm typing, "Sorry the program "telepathy butterfly" closed unexpectedly). So I update, "time remaining: 30 minutes". Okay, I'll just put music and browse the web. Oh yeah, Rythmbox needs the MP3 codec, okay. "Only one Synaptec (or whatever) program can run at once" Really? We're in 2009 for f* sakes. I assume there's a very rational explanation for this, but I really don't want to have to wait to install a codec. So, I decide to go on YouTube... ahh yes, can't even install flash while my system updates. So by the time this is done, I get the mp3 codec and listen to music on Rythmbox. Then, another song starts playing, I get a notification. Wow, this is sweet, and it even looks sweet, but why is it in the middle right of the screen? So I look around to change this, 20 minutes later of searching options and Google, nothing. Well F* this. I install Opera, everything works. Decide to make the panels transparent. Everything works fine, but the "Show Desktop" icon is not transparent. For an icon that is there on default, I would have expected it to be. A few more items to the panel, half of them have transparent backgrounds, half of them don't. Whatever. Let's install Gnome Do. So I go to Ubuntu Software Center (which is really nice by the way). Try to install Gnome Do. Oh it's there "Not available", okay so I go on the Gnome Do website. They tell me to use Synaptec. So I go on Synaptec, try to install Gnome Do. It tells me it can't. Okay... close Synaptec, re open it. Install Gnome Do. It works. I open Gnome Do, go to "About" window pops up. Press "close" it won't close. Press it a few more times until I xkill Gnome Do in the terminal. Re open Gnome Do, go to preferences, the Gnome Do box disappear, and nothing pops up. Try to re open Gnome Do, program is already opened.

Well you know what? F* this. I'm going back to Windows.

I'm not a Windows advocate either. Windows has its share of problems. I mean, have you ever closed Windows without an error? Is it even possible? And how many times have Windows decided to hide those precious .dll files? I mean no system is perfect, and I had used Ubuntu between Windows XP and Vista. But Windows 7 just comes together. However, I'm still waiting for a blue screen of death which is bound to happen.


We can't really compare OS X because Apple uses its own hardware. But OS X just works and is user friendly. It's too bad for their huge monopoly (although, no bigger than Digidesign's) because sometimes I would love to have the freedom that Ubuntu offers. But there's a price to pay if you want everything to just work. But by no means as this come to be a problem.

But I may be biased because I'm more of a designer than a programmer.



However, one thing I've noticed a lot of you don't take in consideration is that the OP is referring to the average person. Would an average have gone through all the trouble of finding how to update? Install Opera? Maybe, maybe not. But in my case, I'm sure the average person would have said f* this way before I did.

And here's a small analogy for the sake of it. Take an average person, give them a Ubuntu and a Windows 7 (or OS X) based computers and give them two tasks: 
(1) Set up the computer to use dual monitors and (2) Get an Office Suite.

Windows person: Google Office Suite, the first website: Open Office > Download now > Clicks "next" a few times.
Connects a screen to the computer, and voila!

Linux person: Applications > Office, everything is there! Connects another screen to the computer, doesn't work. Google the problem, finds tones of different solution, downloads and changes tones of settings through the terminal, nothing works. Posts a thread on the Ubuntu forums. First few people bash him and tell him to use the "Search" function. Others give him the solutions he already tried. Someone suggests installing a video card driver. He doesn't know what a driver is, or what kind of video card he has... etc.

Yes, once Linux has been set up, it is much more stable than Windows, but all the trouble one has to go through isn't worth it IMO.

---

### Post by tuxeee on 2009-12-03
> **KiwiNZ said:**

thanks for clearing that up.

according to wiki *"Its sixth release Mac OS X v10.5 "Leopard" gained UNIX 03 certification while running on Intel processors."*

---

### Post by scottuss on 2009-12-03
> **tuxeee said:**
> thanks for clearing that up.

according to wiki *"Its sixth release Mac OS X v10.5 "Leopard" gained UNIX 03 certification while running on Intel processors."*

Does that mean that Linux and OS-X are similar if you strip them down to the bare bone?

No, not massively. There are some similarities, (one obvious one is the directory structure being similar)

Apple took some FreeBSD code and some other code from other places and threw it all together in a nice pretty box. It's now quite distant from FreeBSD which in itself is fairly distant from Linux.

---

### Post by Frak on 2009-12-03
> **tuxeee said:**
> thanks for clearing that up.

according to wiki *"Its sixth release Mac OS X v10.5 "Leopard" gained UNIX 03 certification while running on Intel processors."*

Does that mean that Linux and OS-X are similar if you strip them down to the bare bone?

Linux doesn't pass SUS, and couldn't if it tried. Linux is a monolithic kernel, and instantly fails the test.

At the same time, OS X doesn't have a microkernel either (it really doesn't, though in the same respect, it isn't monolithic either). Just goes to show that you can buy anything for the right price.

### Post by AllRadioisDead on 2009-12-03
> **arrow.69 said:**
> No. I'm sorry. You make good points in the rest of your post but this I can`t accept. Ubuntu is user-friendly at first, when you`re lucky.
 
Try to explain to a normal user how to set up dual-head (two monitors) in Ubuntu. Just try.
 
Try to explain to a user why when he resizes a VLC window the computer completely crashes, and when it reboots, the resolution is wrong.
 
Try to explain to this same user why the 3D games he downloaded off Synpatics don`t run.
 
Try to explain to him that you can't edit some setting without going into the terminal and editing some scary configuration file.
 
Explain why resizing windows slows down your computer so much (Compiz)
 
Explain why the default theme is so ugly and why you have to jump through all these hoops to make it prettier.
 
Finally, after this user's been using Linux for a few months. Explain to him how using Linux has made his life easier.
 
 
I don't expect Ubuntu to be perfect. I understand that it's a young, evolving OS, I understand the limits of open source. That's fine. But I think that the Ubuntu community would benefit from some humility. Your OS is far from perfect. It is much more buggy and less usable than other options. Yes, even for doing simple tasks, Ubuntu feels buggy and unpolished. Even to the typical user. Ubuntu has accomplished a lot in the last few years, and I'm confident it can do better. But enough of this pride and smugness. Ubuntu is broken on many important levels, and we need to work as a community to fix it.
 
P.S. Whoever mentioned that the Ubuntu community doesn't need me, you're an idiot. That's exactly the kind of comment that scares away users and gives Linux the reputation of being only for h4x0rs and pwn3rs. We all know Ubuntu benefits from every user it picks up. As Linux becomes more popular, support for the OS increases, the OS becomes more usable, everyone is happy.

### Post by scottuss on 2009-12-04
> **Icehuck said:**
> I'd say it's serious agreement and arrow.69 hit the nail right on the head.

OK, so when someone posts an opinion and it's obviously from experience, I have no issue with that. But when they act like the issue(s) are something common that everyone has, thus making Ubuntu look really bad, I get slightly annoyed. There's nothing wrong with stating what doesn't work for you, but that may not be true for everyone.

Quoted from arrow.69:

"Try to explain to a normal user how to set up dual-head (two monitors) in Ubuntu. Just try."

Plug them both in. Worked for me.


"Try to explain to a user why when he resizes a VLC window the computer completely crashes, and when it reboots, the resolution is wrong."

Been using VLC for years, on several different systems with different hardware, never happened once to me (or the people I know who use it)

"Try to explain to this same user why the 3D games he downloaded off Synpatics don`t run."

I don't tend to play games much, but the ones I have downloaded seem to work OK (Alien Arena etc)

"Try to explain to him that you can't edit some setting without going into the terminal and editing some scary configuration file."

Gconf-editor = similar to regedit on Windows
Terminal = similar to Command Line on Windows
Alt+F2 = similar to Run command on Windows.
Configuration files exist on Windows too.
Would you argue that Windows is REALLY so much easier?

"Explain why resizing windows slows down your computer so much (Compiz)"

Easy to explain: Lackluster hardware, drivers, or both. Easy to fix.

"Explain why the default theme is so ugly and why you have to jump through all these hoops to make it prettier."

I agree it ain't the prettiest thing I've ever seen, but it can be made much prettier, fairly easily. Click System > Preferences > Appearance just to start off. Some Operating Systems don't allow quite this level of customisation!


"Finally, after this user's been using Linux for a few months. Explain to him how using Linux has made his life easier."

Surely they will know if it has made their life easier or not? It's not my job to explain why. If it hasn't, give them back what they had before.


Just the other side of the coin :)

---

### Post by abhilashm86 on 2009-12-04
> **arrow.69 said:**
> I'm gonna get incredibly bashed for posting this, but here it goes.

I've been running various Linux distributions on a bunch of computers since I was 13.  Windows would piss me off, Vista got released, or sometimes I just felt like playing around.  I've been through MEPIS, OpenSuse, Kubuntu, Ubuntu,amongst others.  It eventually became clear to me that Ubuntu was the overall best desktop distribution out there, if only for the tremendous amount of support there is out there for it.

I love Ubuntu.  It's a very capable OS and I love the idea of running a completely free and open operating system.  I've probably run some variation of Ubuntu on and off for the past four years.  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

However, I have to admit that Ubuntu simply can't compete anymore.  Windows 7 is a huge leap for Windows, and OS X has always been user-friendly and functional.  I know that under the hood, Linux is more stable, quicker, more secure than Windows.  But the truth is, to the end-user on the desktop front, the experience you get running Ubuntu doesn't measure up.  The problem isn't a lack of applications of features.  Ubuntu just lacks polish and integration.  Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users.  I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability.  Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer.  They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system.  They want it to just work for them.  Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now.

What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

> **pookiebear said:**
> Only things holding back ubuntu or any linux distro is ATI, NVIDIA and Broadcom. 

Nvidia's Linux driver lacks a few features, but it's nearly as good as the Windows' version and sufficient even for commercial game playing (Quake Wars for example). For most uses and people there is virtually no difference in performance or usability.

ATI's direct Linux support is poor and the driver is not anywhere as good as the Windows' one, and they dropped support for "legacy" cards, which includes even some cards sold only a year ago. On the flipside, they did provide the specs so external developers can work on a FOSS driver. It's far more than most hardware manufacturers are willing to do.

Broadcom ... fair point. Older adapters are usually supported, but newer ones are troublesome or unsupported. The biggest issue here is that Ubuntu does not include the drivers and that you have to get them online, which, if you don't have another machine, is a problem.

The same applies for the video card drivers, DVD support and codecs. Easy enough to get if you know how to (there is no information about this within Ubuntu or during the installation), but unintuitive for someone used to a OS that comes with support out of the box. The unattractive brown theme aisde, this is one reason why I prefer to handout Mint's Live CD instead of Ubuntu's, for people who I think might like a Debian-based distro.

---

### Post by Digikid on 2009-12-04
> **arrow.69 said:**
> I think I finally understand why I think Ubuntu will never be as good an OS as other proprietary OSes.

All Linux distros are designed, programmed, etc.  by engineers, programmers, with other engineers and programmers in mind.  That's fine, and I don't think that Linux necessarily needs to appeal to the same demographic of users as other OSes.

However, I'm sick of hearing thread after thread from Linux gurus explaining to someone contemplating a switch how Linux is the best OS for 100 reasons.  It's simply not true.  Hands down, Linux requires more work, is more buggy, and not necessarily as user-friendly.  You don't need to remind me of the 200 advantages Linux offers.  I'm there, I use Linux on a daily basis, I know.  I just think alot of people on here are missing the point entirely as to how most people see computers, what they want out of them, and what they consider a 'good OS'.  Let me give you an example.  I'm installing Kubuntu on my computer and my girlfriend who knows nothing about computers happens to be around.  I ask her what she thinks of the installer GUI, which I thought looks really sleek:  "I think it looks childish.  Like it was designed for 5 year olds or something."  I didn't get it.  But the point is, Linux just doesn't cut it for most users, and alot of users fail to see what's missing.  Since the users developing for Ubuntu do not have most computer users in mind, I don't think it will ever be competitive in the desktop area.  ( I know, Linux doesn't really 'compete'.  Whatever.)

You just do not get it.  Sad.  Very Sad. :sad:

Try again.

### Post by arrow.69 on 2009-12-04
> **Digikid said:**
> You just do not get it.  Sad.  Very Sad. :sad:

Try again.


Thanks for that.

What I'm trying to say is that Linux guys don't seem to see what's lacking in all the Linux distros.  You remind me of Steve Ballmer dissing the iPhone when it launched: [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C5oGaZIKYvo](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=C5oGaZIKYvo)

See..  You can go through and compare all OSes feature for feature and tell me you can do everything on a Linux machine for free.  And you're completely right.  I can do mostly anything I do on Windows in Linux, although I lose access to some niche software.  For a day to day use though, it's fine, feature wise.

The problem I see with Linux is that both Gnome and KDE are very 'Me too! Me too!' in their approach to the desktop.  Although I'll agree some of the KDE 4 features are quite innovative, in general, the Linux desktop poorly imitates Windows and/or OS X.

Also, I'd like to comment on some of the arguments people have against Windows.

- Viruses/Spyware -->  To echo crispygoat's comments, how do you use the internet?  I probably haven't gotten a virus  since 2005
- Application Selection -->  Most useful Linux applications are available for Windows and are as easy, and often easier to install on Windows.
- Stabiliy -->  Windows 7 has made huge  leaps in this area.  Sure, applications crash, but they never take down the system or cause too much of a freakout.

I dunno.  Windows is not an amazing Operating System, but its huge market share makes it that much more usable.

I understand that most things holding back Desktop Linux are things such as market share and hardware support.  However, to the end user these debates don't matter.  No one cares why their Linux install sucks.  No hone cares that Windows isn't technically free, it comes bundled with every laptop you buy.  At the end of the day, people want their OS to work for them, not against them.  Of course, we forgive alot of Window's shortcomings because it's familiar.  This just means Linux has to try harder to attract users on the desktop front.  Back when Macs were less popular, people would see someone running OS X and say wow.  They'd get excited.  Girls want to play with the webcam effects.  Everything is shiny and eveything 'just works'.  That's polish.  Not eyecandy.  Polish.  Good, Intuitive design.  When I show someone my current Linux distro, they might be impressed by the Compiz Cube.  When I tell them it took me hours to get dual-head working with compiz and fglrx drivers, they get scared.  When they see me reboot my computer with a completely broken X configuration, they're completely turned off.  When they witness me scouring the internet and this forum for advice or help on fixes for this, entering scary looking code in the terminal, they know they want to stay away from Linux.

Show me a distro that not only has a nice default theme, also looks and behaves in a unified and intuitive fashion.  Then I'll swear off Windows and join the cult.  But for now, on both KDE and Gnome, I see a fully-featured, completely functional Desktop that does most of what I need it to do, except it's plagued with sporadic and sometimes unfixable bugs which make it frustrating to use.  I'm happy for you if Linux works for you.  Except for most user, it's just not worth their effort.  Those who fall into this category I'm sure don't check UbuntuForums.  But trust me, they're there.  Users who hear of Karmic, download the Live CD, run into problems during installation or configuration, and give up forever.

P.S.:  Written from OpenSuse 11.2.  Yes.  For all my bitching, I use Linux on my main machine.  I just think Linux gurus could use some humility.  How can we improve the Linux desktop without knowing what's wrong with it?

### Post by mivo on 2009-12-05
> **expxe said:**
> seriously? you need FOUR operating systems? what do you do on your computer? deep down a part of feel sorry for you, you must feel so deprived. write your own OS and programs and be happy.

No need to feel sorry for me, but thanks for the thought. :) (Even though your grammar is really off -- work on that and you'll flame much more efficiently!)

I'll explain it to you, though!

First, it's really just two operating systems. My main desktop runs Windows 7. I work in the gaming/entertainment industry, so I have to have a state-of-the-art computer with an OS that runs everything I need for work, including products from competitors (gaming, entertainment), so this means Windows 7. My netbook isn't well supported by Linux, and since I need suspend and hibernate to work, as I often open and close the device, XP is the logical choice. It works perfectly, so need to change it.

My laptop currently runs on Arch (Linux). It is well supported (except for suspend and hibernate) and functions as a quasi-desktop. It isn't very new and works better with a custom-configured Arch than it does with XP. It's faster in just about any way and offers all the tools I need. Key point here is that wireless works flawlessly. Then I have another desktop that plays a number of roles, ranging from file server to firewall to terminal (frequently have to SSH into my office's servers) and general "off"-desktop. I went with Ubuntu because it was fast to set-up and doesn't require maintenance (it'll stay with the next LTS).

So, you see, the right OS for the available tasks. I don't think I only had one computer for everything since about 1988, but I can appreciate that having multiple computers may seem alien to you. It's okay, though! I could merge the four machines into two, ideally with a dual head setup for the desktop, but there's no need to and anyone who ever had a machine die on them knows to value the availability of a backup computer (or several).
