### Post by MaraSkywhiner on 2009-12-05
I dual-boot WinXP and Ubuntu, though I've spent some time with OSX as well.  My only experience with Win7 so far is playing with it at MicroCenter.

I think OSX definitely wins out for people who want their computers to "just work."  My 80 year old grandparents are quite technically literate for their age.  They have three computers, all of which run OSX, but I would never suggest that they switch to either Windows or Linux.  OSX is incredibly user friendly.  My grandparents are fairly well protected from viruses (though not immune) running OSX, and they never need to open the Terminal.  

If they switched to Linux, they would be defeated the first time they needed to do anything involving the Terminal.  They don't care about customization, they just want to be able to turn on the computer, write books, plug in their iPod, and go online.  Apple has provided these services in a stable easy-to-use OS.  While you can do most of these things on Linux (I've never gotten an iPod to work as advertised on Linux, though others have), Apple's user experience is a lot cleaner.

Windows (at least in my experience) is not user-friendly enough for my grandparents.  They don't want to manage several antivirus and antispyware programs, or deal with the bloatware that most manufacturers install on Windows machines (not Microsoft's fault, but still irritating).  Also, in my experience, Apple's OSs are more stable than Windows.

Personally, I enjoy the challenge of getting things to work in Linux, and I like being able to customize an OS to my liking.  Sometimes it's INCREDIBLY frustrating (like when I needed to get online in Linux and could not authenticate on a network with WPA2 encryption), but overall I enjoy it.  That said, however, I can't see myself getting rid of Windows.  I need IE7 and Quickbooks for work, and I've never been able to get my iPod to work well with Linux.  Furthermore, I like that fact that I can see some software I need for business, and be able to buy it without worrying if I can get it to work on my computer.  I enjoy tinkering on my own time, but not on the company clock.

### Post by ns_477 on 2009-12-06
after having read most of the back forth arguments for and against windows or linux or osx to me the its very simple....people should be able to pick right from the beginning the operating system they want on their computer and thenbe happy with it.  when i saw that ubuntu was partnering with asus to produce netbooks with netbook remix on them i was shocked...i even went and tried to buy one only to find out the article i ah read was a month or so old and that they were apparently no longer availible....only to have to do what ive done so many times before, buy a computer with a system that i don't want , scrub the disk and put the system i want on it. in this case netbook remix. i think that if one really though about the idea of a software company exclusively contracting with multiple hardware manufactures to produce exclusivly only one type of system in a capitalist market that one would come to the realization that the very idea contradicts itself
and that the only real solution to the issue is to either A)un-licence all software and have it distributed under the terms of the original GNU Copy-Left agreement or B) require that all software be licenced
i don't see how it makes any differance whether one is better than another,it seems to be whether its fairly distributed and macintosh is the perfect example and microsoft the perfect counter example who has in their life ever seen a "Microsoft" brand pc?
not a compaq powerspec running win 98 not a dell inspiron with win 7 not a hp dv 1000 media center  with win xp media edition a true microsoft brand machine? i think the people at asus are cowards and just another example of weak people succumbing to the siren song of laziness. they had a great oppertunity to be the first to fight back along with mac and google and give us what is rightfully ours, the chance to buy the computer we want with the software we want not the big brother approved choice of windows that sucks and crashes or windows that looks pretty and crashes or we have better marketing so we have all the cool games on our system and it still crashes!
its time to stop bickering over whats better and start demanding choice in the computers we want! stop paying for windows if you wont use it fight the power!

### Post by mivo on 2009-12-06
> **ns_477 said:**
> people should be able to pick right from the beginning the operating system they want on their computer and thenbe happy with it.

A manufacturer or vendor has to support the OS that they include on the computers that they sell. This means extra costs, more personnel, more training, and so on. There is also the problem that many distros, including Ubuntu, change a lot between the releases, and suddenly that worked in the last release may suffer from issues in the next release just a few months later. From a commercial support perspective. this is a nightmare.

### Post by dzon65 on 2009-12-06
Due to medical costs and economical reasons I can't afford a new pc,let alone a mac.Besides,why should I.I've got this neanderthal pc which originally runs vista.Vista's been bugging since.....well,forever.I first started out with 8.04 but due to my lack of knowledge went back to windows.But,desperately in need of something that doesn't EAT ram or makes this machine feel like an oven,decided to give it another try,first with 8.10 and now 9.10 (both wubi installed).Exept for a few small issues (bluetooth and multisession burning),I think this is by far the best solution for me and most probably a lot of less fortunate people.I've installed quite a bit dual boots for people less fortunate with far lesser computerknowledge than most on the forums and guess what,they're very happy to be able to surf and email and...
Especially in these times of economical withdraw,for those who cannot afford windows7 or some fancy apple I think ubuntu makes a perfect example of what else can be done.
Or I must have been hybernating for years,no other os offers this.What are you going to install on something with 128mb Ram and 20Gb storage?Even xp light will be a challenge.
Os'es like windows and apple which are build around whatever they consider as being the best even have problems. So something "alien" like ubuntu can function perfectly within their homes AND free is in my opinion a nice thing.

### Post by lancest on 2009-12-08
I see what you mean, however these users typically have no need for the command line in Ubuntu.
I know this for a fact.
If they did it is very simple for them to cut and paste any command using a mouse into the Gnome terminal. Often easier than digging around for a application.

These are the same type of cut and paste skills that could be used in a word processing application.
 Also it's a myth that newbie users can't find simple uses for the CLI. It's what MS wants you to believe. 
I can rip a VCD with a simple command: vcdxrip -C . It will find my VCD without any hunting. 
How could it be any easier? Examples abound.  


If you are talking about seeking information in order to run a command it might be a hassle though especially if they can't Google.

### Post by wavery on 2009-12-08
> **lancest said:**
> I see what you mean, however these users typically have no need for the command line in Ubuntu.
I know this for a fact.
If they did it is very simple for them to cut and paste any command using a mouse into the Gnome terminal. Often easier than digging around for a application.

These are the same type of cut and paste skills that could be used in a word processing application.
 Also it's a myth that newbie users can't find simple uses for the CLI. It's what MS wants you to believe. 
I can rip a VCD with a simple command: vcdxrip -C . It will find my VCD without any hunting. 
How could it be any easier? Examples abound.  


If you are talking about seeking information in order to run a command it might be a hassle though especially if they can't Google.

I'm talking about something as simple as entering a sudo command.    For someone not used to entering commands then watching the results of that command go scrolling by, this can be intimidating.  Sure the same thing happens behind the scenes in a graphical interface, but I think that 'behind the scenes' aspect provides a certain comfort level for the user.  There is no chance of entering the wrong command in a point-and-click environment (excluding the possibility of pointing and clicking on the wrong thing).  

Maybe there is no need to leave the Ubuntu graphical interface, but the forum-suggested solution to most issues I've come across usually starts with going to the terminal window.  There may very well may be solutions without going to the terminal window, but they have not been as readily apparent for the issues I've had at least.  That's not a complaint; I can fully understand why experienced users would suggest command-line solutions over GUI ones.  Just my experience.

### Post by piedro on 2009-12-09
Hi! 


A typical personal computer I have to setup for customers looks like that: 


1. Buying cheap but suffcient hardware for their needs. 

Most of my customers are not willing to spend a lot of money on computers. No matter how cheap hardware gets they want the price on the lower end. I think that's some psycho thing: "By buying low price PCs I confirm that other things in my life are more important to me than computers." But they buy a fully automated coffee machine at least double that price ... "Can't be saving ip your money on those for life quality things, you know ...". 

Since I can pick the machine for hardware compatibility Ubuntu does a great job in this area. I know that it's probably running smoothly whilst looking great on a little cheaper hardware than Windows does. 

MacOS? Great! - but I never had to do a custom install. Cheap hardware? You're kidding ... 


2. Installing the OS including drivers 

As mentioned before, you rarely install Windows on a computer like the one I described. Well, I'd do it but it's just cheaper when it's done by the computer store. They do it for 20&#8364; - can't beat that price. But: Those 20&#8364; plus the 80&#8364; for the license is saved when using Ubuntu! I'm happy to install Ubuntu for 50&#8364;. And there's room for individual configuration and customization and polishing for another 50&#8364; ... 

Ubuntu installs are great: You can customize a lot of things very commfortably within the installer prior to installation. once you decide doing a custom install Ubuntu is much more convenient (and looks better ...) than windows ever was. If you install default settings both of them are doing their job. 

Once finished the basic install it's a completely different story: 
Windows needs a lot of drivers, like mainboard or chipsets. You find them on CDs, on hardware provider's homepages or the site of your motherboard manufacturer. It's just a nuisance: some will work better than Windows defaults, some won't. Some install useless software you want to remove afterwards, some require specific versions. Admittedly 90% of driver installations are hussle free but the remaining 10% easily make up for it. 

Ubuntu runs on the machines described above (cheap standard hardware, no bleeding edge technology) out of the box. In most cases one click on "Find hardware drivers" does the job and you're done. More room for individual customization due to the time saved in comparison.

MacOS? Sry, but I just don't know.    


3. Basic Setup, software installation 

An annoying part of Ubuntu setup is the multimedia setup. Installing nonfree codecs, DVD-Playback and ... well ... not too much more anymore, to be honest. (codecs coming in automagically through gstreamer is working - never worked on Windows Media Player!) 
Still it's bad the user has to fiddle with these things - "I don't care about patents, licenses, legal issues - I have a brandnew machine, so let's go!" 

Windows is something like half baked here. Yes most multimedia things work, but what about Quicktime, Realplayer and hmmm, a friend did send me those "ogg"-files, ah yes and Flashplayer missing, do I also need Shockwave, is it better to install JAVA now, since my customer shouldn't have to fiddle around with that one later, not knowing whether to deinstall the old versions or leave them to stay ... 
  
MacOS? In these respects the absolute killer machine! It just works. 
(To note: it can be done!) 
By the way: Not true for proprietary plugins, you still have to download them eventually. 

Now I get to Ubuntus biggest asset: Software installation. 
Can't be easier. But wait ... 

First look at the software people want (talking about the people like my customers, they want to be able to work, get social and informational access and still have some entertainment value, though they never admit beforehand that they use a computer for pleisure)
Sidenote: Google knows about it - that will be exactly the ChromeOS profile ...) 

Here's my standards: 

- office software, homebanking/tax & finance, adressbook 
- email, browsing, chatting, skype, syncing with mobile/camera 
- music/video/photo player & organization, some photo retuche, simple games (for obscure reason people I'd never expected to do this, play excessivly solitaire, hearts & mahjongg!) 

In my experience the above is the stuff everybody expects (not becessarily uses though ...)    

On Windows I need about 2 hours to install, setup and test all that stuff to work well. Don't forget some security suite. This may not be that much better than the windows builtin stuff, but people expect to have a security suite build by a security specialist, at least here in Europe. Noone really trusts Microsoft. That's why even the most unexperienced users ask me to install Firefox, or Itunes, or an application firewall, Nokia Phone Tools, the software that comes with their digicam and ... 

On a Mac I need about half half an hour to do the same. This is due to the fact that the on board software is so powerful. Safari, Itunes, Apple Mail, IPhoto, Quicktime - it's all there and does what you want. Additional Software like openoffice, NeoOffice or MS Office is pretty much the same easy stuff like everywhere else ... 

Now Ubuntu: Don't worry it's already there! Sorry but: No it's not. 
I mean it is more there than in either of the two commercial OSs. 
But to get the rest of this standards list is just not possible in my eyes. 

Take homebanking as example. There's no easy going homebanking software around. Gnucash? Doesn't even handle VAT out of the box. Kmymoney? Good one, just can't initiate online transactions and doesn't use the gnome looks and fonts out of the box. Hibiscus via Java, can't print, Grisbi doesn't connect online. Commercial german Starmoney uses wine (even more hussle for simple users), andthere is commercial Matrica Moneyplex, but this works just in german language, has a very unique look (though great), and needs two or three commandline adjustments with Ubuntu to make it work reliably. 
Welcome back to fiddling.

So it's not just about polish, it's about missing functionality in many areas. And malinformation: Ubuntu promises to much,so disappointment will come. 

Nice to show 5 different Homebanking Software packages within the Software center. That is fair and gives credit to the developers. 
But it disappoints the user. Starting out with one application, missing a feature, trying the next and eventually all of them. Installing all kinds of other stuff during the process, perhaps messing up some systemstandards, without the chance of ever getting there. 

Don't get me wrong: the softwares that do suffice for an average  user is so easyly maintained, every idiot out there get's the charme of the "keep my whole software bunch update via maintained repositories" idea! 

Custom software Updates with Windows or MacOs are ridiculous in comparison. Either do it manually or have all kinds of software update utilities running around in the background. 


4. customizing the look & feel 

All three OSs look good out of the box, personal taste let aside. 
Look & Feel may be a bluff in many areas of the commercial two. But it works. Microsoft spends a million dollars in the development of smoothing system sounds alone, Apple tweaks their looks to the state of the art edge of corporate design in a way, that everything non-apple becomes an irritation. A customer of mine got an installation of microsoft office on a apple powerbook running MacOs Tiger. The first time she looked at her desktop she asked immediatly: "what is this ugly Button here, can you remove it?", pointing out the MS office icon in the bottom dock. 

Ubuntu will not be able to compete with this kind of perfectionism. Too may contributors, too few money and the belief in individual freedom (designers included!) will lead to an only fairly consistent look with a little individualistic flair. That's not bad. 
And as far as this it can compete. Cause people like the small things that aren't always completely polished. 
Just, please, no complete inconsistencies like running Amarok within gnome! 

I still have the 50&#8364; worth for customizing, so I usally do something nice for that money. looking for a nice theme, tweaking the fonts a bit, look for usable but beautiful background, a nice screensaver (bluffing also, who needs that?) or a nice welcome sound, suitable for my customer. That way people get that emotional approach towards their cheap "my computer isn't important for my life"-machine. The same one people have towards that abandoned street dog they found at the beach and took home. It's not the most pretty but somehow special. They would never give it away again. And by this emotional approach some of these virgin Ubuntu users slowly get to understand the conceptional, technical and social aspects of their system compared to others. Suddenly the eyecandy competition isn't that important anymore.    

Still: 
Let's polish! To compete for look, feel and usability is simple, though not so easy to do. 

1. Have a standard desktop installation that incorporates only flawlessly working applications. Don't advertise the others to the new and unexperienced user. 

2. Have a help page for every single function within the standard applications. MacOs has a wonderful helpsystem. Gives you the feeling of not being left alone. MS tries the same but honestly never ever solved one of my Windows problems. Make this one consistent and binding: No software gets into the standard repositories without working help system.  
 
3. Reduce the customization options to the ones not threatening to destroy the usability in any area. For example, test the maximum font size before busting dialog boxes within the standard application set. Make it impossible to choose an icon set that's not complete. ... You get the idea. 

4. Have an official online documentation for every release ready for average joe's use. when searching the wiki, to find out about how to adjust microfon recording sound levels I don't wnat to find a hardy heron article explaing a bug of alsamixer and how to get around it! 

5. Decide for a system and make it work! Don't tell users: "At the moment gnome uses gstreamer as default, you can use xine with the following commands." When I'm new to Ubuntu I'm lost right here. I visited the Wiki to find out howto play those encrypted wmv files. I will probably think "nice let's try xine for a change, maybe wmv plays then" and run into a lot of trouble. 

6. Distinguish beetween the standard and the advanced (geek or expert) options. Mark everything potentially troublesome as clearly experimental and offer a "bring back the original" solution at every aspect of customization. 


I know that people don't like restrictions but on the other hand everyone likes expert suggestions. So I think there should be more pre-decision on look, usability $ documentation standards. I think this would also help to get applications ready for the desktop. I imagine that if the decision were to use gnucash as standard for the next release of Ubuntu a community call to support and codevelop the application, the documentation and the system integration would push the progress immensely. 

Still Ubuntu as a free system will not be able to compete with the MS or Apple world without more funding. But their is quite a lot of people out there that would love to dump their Windows box or save money on Apple in exchange for a husslefree and maybe not perfectly perfect but likeable sytem, that does what they expect. 


my two cents,
thx for reading, 
p.

---

### Post by hoppipolla on 2009-12-09
> **SeanBlader said:**
> [QUOTE=openuniverse;8437609]i've spent a few years getting more comfortable with ubuntu, and i did it because i care what ubuntu stands for.

I patiently read through 19 pages of this thread and came to this line that ultimately sums up in a nutshell why Ubuntu CAN compete with OS X or Windows 7.[/QUOTE]

heh, I don't know if that's important to everyone though :)

I would say that **most** people care about results. I mean think about it, it's a BIG business world out there.

However, I believe we have results and they will continue to improve with time. The first stage is making Linux accessible which Canonical are doing a phenomenal job of with Ubuntu, other than their relative lack of attention towards KDE (which tbh I can't blame them for due to KDE's weaker position during the earlier 4 releases) I couldn't have done a better job myself :)

I believe then the second stage is to just let it snowball. To not shy away from the better GUIs and set-ups (so, if KDE or Gnome proves superior we should USE it, if a piece of closed source or proprietary code does something well such as a driver we should use that too), and to let the open source "movement" gain pace.

The first tipping point IMO is making it as friendly as Windows, and accessibly branded. The second will be better driver support, the third better software support, and each one of these hurdles will increase the market share. Godspeed Canonical and good luck! :D

### Post by mivo on 2009-12-10
> There is a learning curve, but to that I say [My time has no value?]("http://www.psychocats.net/ubuntucat/my-time-has-no-value/")

Fair point and a good read.

The point I aimed at was this: I feel that stressing the fact that Linux is free (as in beer) does the OS a bit of a "disfavour" (too strong a word) by portraying the non-existent price tag as a chief reason to use Linux. Hardships or too many computers aside, I believe this is generally a poor reason and attracts "freeloaders" (too strong a word) rather than future active and contributing members of the Linux community. People who believe in the philosophy, or users who see the benefits of this flavour of OS. (I admit that this is partly an unreasonable expectation once the desktop market share grows.)

Granted, "free" (beer) is also an argument that speaks for Linux as a choice, especially if you build your own computer. But if that is the only or even the major reason, then I don't know if the time that is spent on learning and fixing is all that well spent.

All of that aside, the deciding key factor is probably the answer to a simple question: Is it fun and rewarding?  If yes, the time you invest is well spent. If no, and you are frustrated or annoyed, the price of "no price" is probably too high.

I didn't mean to make it sound as if I constantly evaluated my own time. I certainly don't use it efficiently in a monetary way. For example, this afternoon I spent several hours learning Xingqi (Chinese chess). It will never make me money. I probably won't even play it very often. However, it was fun, I learned something new and I enjoyed myself. But I would never recommend the game to someone and say, "Play it because unlike video games it is free!".

### Post by aysiu on 2009-12-10
That's a good point. I don't agree that the free monetary cost of Linux should not be the primary selling point. Still, for me, it was one less barrier to adoption (if Linux had cost money, I'd probably have just stuck with Windows or moved to Mac).

What drew me to Linux was the ease of customization. What kept me was the ease of use and the community support.

### Post by Ylon on 2009-12-10
> **arrow.69 said:**
> What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

Point one:
Microsoft and Apple do something that within Linux universe, was never do: push hardware manufacturers to *fix* their product, make driver and, more in general.. spend a lot of their resource to make Windows and MacOS ran smooth with their hardware.


The blackmail is simple: if your hardware don't work (or work bad) with our OS... your clients will go elsewhere.


Point two:
Read the first point... now, try to just forget for a second about Ubuntu/Linux/whateverelse. Isn't your first though: "*Geez, in this way the hardware (_ANY_ hardware) will work smooth only on Windows and MacOS!*"
Now you can remember anything else: Ubuntu do this work.. and with a HUGE database that both Windows (any version) and MacOSX (any version) couldn't even imagine. With very little to no efforts made from the legitimate manufacturers.


Point three (money&time to learn an OS):
Windows come with the PC: everyone know Windows... but anyone actually did _choice_ it? If you buy an IBM PC (with windows) and have some problem, do you call Microsoft support center?
No, that's because Microsoft isn't making any effort to help you: your money just jump in their pocket when you did buy a IBM PC (and you call IBM support center to have your Windows's problem solved)
On contrary, Ubuntu community is doing this (for free).. if you need more powered support, you can pay for Canonical support center: you know WHO you pay.. and you know that you are pay the right one to make your problem, their job.

Point four (money&time to learn an OS(2)):
Ask a Chinese: is more easy to talk/learn in English or Chinese?
The first time was placed in your hands a PC, you didn't ask for Windows Operanting System... you ask for a Dell/HP/Acer/Sony/whatever to browse internet... Windows was just *forced* in your hands.
You didn't choice how spend your money or your time to learn.. you were forced to learn everything Microsoft decided for you to.

Point Five (trust-ability and fairness of the product):
- What did people did say about Windows Me? Clients did stay back on Windows 98SE.
- What did people say about Windows XP? Still with 98SE.
- Vista? Step back on XP
These aren't choice, this is the usability of their old Dell/HP/Acer/Sony/whatever. Windows is (and was) always a disturb to update.. to keep use your old pc!
If people did make a choice, they should *realize* that someone is getting rid of them? pay again an update in order.. to allow your PC make the same things! (run new (low resource) software).
The Microsoft's %share is always there.. that's because Windows isn't a choice


Point Six (where all my moneys go!?):
How many PCs there's in the world? How many of them send money in the Microsoft bank?
Well, looks like these are REALLY BIG numbers so.. where are all the investment of this money in....the Windows Software?
Screw NASA resource: at this time Microsoft should be able to let us fly *physically* teleport on the moon with all the money spent on research.
Where are all investment made on Vista? A ZERO-PAIED OS like Ubuntu is way a lot better.


Just my six cents.

### Post by mivo on 2009-12-10
> **Ylon said:**
> Microsoft and Apple do something that within Linux universe, was never do: push hardware manufacturers to *fix* their product, make driver and, more in general.. spend a lot of their resource to make Windows and MacOS ran smooth with their hardware.

Have you considered the possibility that perhaps hardware manufacturers may **want** their products to be compatible and have better drivers than their competition? I see no reason for Microsoft or Apple to "blackmail" hardware manufacturers. It is a symbiosis, and both parties mutually benefit.

Often enough, it is the hardware makers that push. Take 64-bit for example. Before W7, and to a degree Vista, 64-bit Windows was not a good choice, yet 64-bit CPUs have been out for quite a while. Did you see Microsoft "blackmail" Intel or AMD to stop producing them? Or even slowing down their production? No, of course not. It was MS that had to struggle to come up with 64-bit compatibility, and they reached a satisfying level a long time after Linux got there.

To me, your statement makes no sense. Microsoft doesn't have the "power" to blackmail AMD or Intel, nor do they have any need for doing so. There is less Linux support because there is less money to be made from Linux users. There are only a few (compared to Windows users), they use their hardware longer (Linux doesn't push hardware sales -- Windows and games do), there are numerous distros that all do little things differently and use different kernels, etc.

Why would Nvidia or ATI spend a lot of time on getting their video card drivers on the same performance level as their Windows equivalents if there are hardly any Linux games? Let alone games that actually benefit from top driver performance. The Nvidia Linux drivers are good the way they are anyway.

---

### Post by Zoot7 on 2009-12-10
> **mivo said:**
> 

<snip>


Good point. One example of that you could take is Moblin. Intel were the driving force behind Moblin and you can bet Microsoft wasn't too happy about that. Blackmail didn't come into it.

The main issue from Hardware vendors point of view is market share. When you can support ~90% of the market with a driver for one platform - Windows, why expend as much effort and ultimately money into the other 10%?

### Post by Zoot7 on 2009-12-10
> **Frak said:**
> Very good point. Don't get mad when vendors decide not to support Linux or BSD, they just don't see the money in it.
Well like it or not we do live in a capitalist world after all.

IMO Linux in general will never rise to a prominent marketshare as long as it's as fragmented on the desktop as it is now, due to the nature of entirely open source software. 
Thus, Independent Software Vendors and Hardware Vendors will never expend the excess effort required (in comparison to Windows) because they, as you said, don't see money in it.
As a result, like it or not, as it stands now Ubuntu (or any other distro for that matter) isn't going to compete with Windows or OSX on a viable level, nor do I envisage it will do so in the near future.

That said, I really do like Linux a lot, it offers so much more than Windows could even dream of for someone like me, and there are areas it absolutely trounces Windows IMO - customizability for example. 
But just because I use it, enjoy using it, and have introduced friends and family to it with a very positive reception, doesn't mean it's ready for mass adoption across the entire Desktop sector, because of the nature of the way it is now.
Whatever you may say about Windows short comings, this is the grim reality.

Anyway that's my take on it.

### Post by Ivo Moelans on 2009-12-10
I've used Windows (from 3.1 to XP SP3) for about 14 years. I use Ubuntu now exclusively since about 2 years.

During my Windows-years not a day went by that I was deeply unhappy with some of the OS's behavior. During most of these years I suffered 3 to 4 crashes (BOSD) a day. I couldn't do a lot, a whole lot of things I wanted to do. Reinstalling was a nightmare and guaranteed two days work minimum to have the system I was used to back *more or less*. In most cases less.

I read in a lot of posts that people want a unified experience out of the box. Where is the scientific basis for that assertion? Did those people even conduct a serious poll? Of course not. Microsoft wants us to believe that *the Microsoft-way* is the only good way. What Windows can't do is simply not possible. What Ubuntu can't do is a serious flaw of the OS. The almost ridiculous lack of configurability of Windows is a feature: people want that (says who?). The configurability of Ubuntu is a *liability* and a sign that the OS isn't as polished as Windows.

I'd rather have some minor (and a few major) inconsistencies in my desktop experience than having to pay for half finished products (in 14 years: Windows 3.1, 3.11, 95, 98, 98SE, (Millennium, I skipped), Win2000, XP, XP SP2) that ***all*** frustrated and angered me on a daily basis and didn't at all give me any kind of desktop experience I wanted, liked or could live with.

I think Ubuntu is great. I love it every day more. Every new distribution brings more refinements. Compare 5.04 with 09.10 and the difference is huge. And I love how I can make it do things *my* way instead of having to do things the MS-way.

It is a capable OS. Otherwise the French police wouldn't be using it. :)

---

### Post by starcannon on 2009-12-10
> **arrow.69 said:**
> I'm gonna get incredibly bashed for posting this, but here it goes.

I've been running various Linux distributions on a bunch of computers since I was 13.  Windows would piss me off, Vista got released, or sometimes I just felt like playing around.  I've been through MEPIS, OpenSuse, Kubuntu, Ubuntu,amongst others.  It eventually became clear to me that Ubuntu was the overall best desktop distribution out there, if only for the tremendous amount of support there is out there for it.

I love Ubuntu.  It's a very capable OS and I love the idea of running a completely free and open operating system.  I've probably run some variation of Ubuntu on and off for the past four years.  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

However, I have to admit that Ubuntu simply can't compete anymore.  Windows 7 is a huge leap for Windows, and OS X has always been user-friendly and functional.  I know that under the hood, Linux is more stable, quicker, more secure than Windows.  But the truth is, to the end-user on the desktop front, the experience you get running Ubuntu doesn't measure up.  The problem isn't a lack of applications of features.  Ubuntu just lacks polish and integration.  Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users.  I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability.  Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer.  They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system.  They want it to just work for them.  Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now.

What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

Thanks for your opinion. My reality is, I refuse to pay the exorbitant extortion fees that MS and Apple feels are appropriate for me to use my hardware. Linux is what I run, MS and Apple can not compete for my computers. Use what you like, I know I do.

---

### Post by Chame_Wizard on 2009-12-11
> **piedro said:**
> Hi! 


A typical personal computer I have to setup for customers looks like that: 



1. Buying cheap but suffcient hardware for their needs. 

Most of my customers are not willing to spend a lot of money on computers. No matter how cheap hardware gets they want the price on the lower end. I think that's some psycho thing: "By buying low price PCs I confirm that other things in my life are more important to me than computers." But they buy a fully automated coffee machine at least double that price ... "Can't be saving ip your money on those for life quality things, you know ...". 

Since I can pick the machine for hardware compatibility Ubuntu does a great job in this area. I know that it's probably running smoothly whilst looking great on a little cheaper hardware than Windows does. 

MacOS? Great! - but I never had to do a custom install. Cheap hardware? You're kidding ... 


2. Installing the OS including drivers 

As mentioned before, you rarely install Windows on a computer like the one I described. Well, I'd do it but it's just cheaper when it's done by the computer store. They do it for 20&#8364; - can't beat that price. But: Those 20&#8364; plus the 80&#8364; for the license is saved when using Ubuntu! I'm happy to install Ubuntu for 50&#8364;. And there's room for individual configuration and customization and polishing for another 50&#8364; ... 

Ubuntu installs are great: You can customize a lot of things very commfortably within the installer prior to installation. once you decide doing a custom install Ubuntu is much more convenient (and looks better ...) than windows ever was. If you install default settings both of them are doing their job. 

Once finished the basic install it's a completely different story: 
Windows needs a lot of drivers, like mainboard or chipsets. You find them on CDs, on hardware provider's homepages or the site of your motherboard manufacturer. It's just a nuisance: some will work better than Windows defaults, some won't. Some install useless software you want to remove afterwards, some require specific versions. Admittedly 90% of driver installations are hussle free but the remaining 10% easily make up for it. 

Ubuntu runs on the machines described above (cheap standard hardware, no bleeding edge technology) out of the box. In most cases one click on "Find hardware drivers" does the job and you're done. More room for individual customization due to the time saved in comparison.

MacOS? Sry, but I just don't know.    


3. Basic Setup, software installation 

An annoying part of Ubuntu setup is the multimedia setup. Installing nonfree codecs, DVD-Playback and ... well ... not too much more anymore, to be honest. (codecs coming in automagically through gstreamer is working - never worked on Windows Media Player!) 
Still it's bad the user has to fiddle with these things - "I don't care about patents, licenses, legal issues - I have a brandnew machine, so let's go!" 

Windows is something like half baked here. Yes most multimedia things work, but what about Quicktime, Realplayer and hmmm, a friend did send me those "ogg"-files, ah yes and Flashplayer missing, do I also need Shockwave, is it better to install JAVA now, since my customer shouldn't have to fiddle around with that one later, not knowing whether to deinstall the old versions or leave them to stay ... 
  
MacOS? In these respects the absolute killer machine! It just works. 
(To note: it can be done!) 
By the way: Not true for proprietary plugins, you still have to download them eventually. 

Now I get to Ubuntus biggest asset: Software installation. 
Can't be easier. But wait ... 

First look at the software people want (talking about the people like my customers, they want to be able to work, get social and informational access and still have some entertainment value, though they never admit beforehand that they use a computer for pleisure)
Sidenote: Google knows about it - that will be exactly the ChromeOS profile ...) 

Here's my standards: 

- office software, homebanking/tax & finance, adressbook 
- email, browsing, chatting, skype, syncing with mobile/camera 
- music/video/photo player & organization, some photo retuche, simple games (for obscure reason people I'd never expected to do this, play excessivly solitaire, hearts & mahjongg!) 

In my experience the above is the stuff everybody expects (not becessarily uses though ...)    

On Windows I need about 2 hours to install, setup and test all that stuff to work well. Don't forget some security suite. This may not be that much better than the windows builtin stuff, but people expect to have a security suite build by a security specialist, at least here in Europe. Noone really trusts Microsoft. That's why even the most unexperienced users ask me to install Firefox, or Itunes, or an application firewall, Nokia Phone Tools, the software that comes with their digicam and ... 

On a Mac I need about half half an hour to do the same. This is due to the fact that the on board software is so powerful. Safari, Itunes, Apple Mail, IPhoto, Quicktime - it's all there and does what you want. Additional Software like openoffice, NeoOffice or MS Office is pretty much the same easy stuff like everywhere else ... 

Now Ubuntu: Don't worry it's already there! Sorry but: No it's not. 
I mean it is more there than in either of the two commercial OSs. 
But to get the rest of this standards list is just not possible in my eyes. 

Take homebanking as example. There's no easy going homebanking software around. Gnucash? Doesn't even handle VAT out of the box. Kmymoney? Good one, just can't initiate online transactions and doesn't use the gnome looks and fonts out of the box. Hibiscus via Java, can't print, Grisbi doesn't connect online. Commercial german Starmoney uses wine (even more hussle for simple users), andthere is commercial Matrica Moneyplex, but this works just in german language, has a very unique look (though great), and needs two or three commandline adjustments with Ubuntu to make it work reliably. 
Welcome back to fiddling.

So it's not just about polish, it's about missing functionality in many areas. And malinformation: Ubuntu promises to much,so disappointment will come. 

Nice to show 5 different Homebanking Software packages within the Software center. That is fair and gives credit to the developers. 
But it disappoints the user. Starting out with one application, missing a feature, trying the next and eventually all of them. Installing all kinds of other stuff during the process, perhaps messing up some systemstandards, without the chance of ever getting there. 

Don't get me wrong: the softwares that do suffice for an average  user is so easyly maintained, every idiot out there get's the charme of the "keep my whole software bunch update via maintained repositories" idea! 

Custom software Updates with Windows or MacOs are ridiculous in comparison. Either do it manually or have all kinds of software update utilities running around in the background. 


4. customizing the look & feel 

All three OSs look good out of the box, personal taste let aside. 
Look & Feel may be a bluff in many areas of the commercial two. But it works. Microsoft spends a million dollars in the development of smoothing system sounds alone, Apple tweaks their looks to the state of the art edge of corporate design in a way, that everything non-apple becomes an irritation. A customer of mine got an installation of microsoft office on a apple powerbook running MacOs Tiger. The first time she looked at her desktop she asked immediatly: "what is this ugly Button here, can you remove it?", pointing out the MS office icon in the bottom dock. 

Ubuntu will not be able to compete with this kind of perfectionism. Too may contributors, too few money and the belief in individual freedom (designers included!) will lead to an only fairly consistent look with a little individualistic flair. That's not bad. 
And as far as this it can compete. Cause people like the small things that aren't always completely polished. 
Just, please, no complete inconsistencies like running Amarok within gnome! 

I still have the 50&#8364; worth for customizing, so I usally do something nice for that money. looking for a nice theme, tweaking the fonts a bit, look for usable but beautiful background, a nice screensaver (bluffing also, who needs that?) or a nice welcome sound, suitable for my customer. That way people get that emotional approach towards their cheap "my computer isn't important for my life"-machine. The same one people have towards that abandoned street dog they found at the beach and took home. It's not the most pretty but somehow special. They would never give it away again. And by this emotional approach some of these virgin Ubuntu users slowly get to understand the conceptional, technical and social aspects of their system compared to others. Suddenly the eyecandy competition isn't that important anymore.    

Still: 
Let's polish! To compete for look, feel and usability is simple, though not so easy to do. 

1. Have a standard desktop installation that incorporates only flawlessly working applications. Don't advertise the others to the new and unexperienced user. 

2. Have a help page for every single function within the standard applications. MacOs has a wonderful helpsystem. Gives you the feeling of not being left alone. MS tries the same but honestly never ever solved one of my Windows problems. Make this one consistent and binding: No software gets into the standard repositories without working help system.  
 
3. Reduce the customization options to the ones not threatening to destroy the usability in any area. For example, test the maximum font size before busting dialog boxes within the standard application set. Make it impossible to choose an icon set that's not complete. ... You get the idea. 

4. Have an official online documentation for every release ready for average joe's use. when searching the wiki, to find out about how to adjust microfon recording sound levels I don't wnat to find a hardy heron article explaing a bug of alsamixer and how to get around it! 

5. Decide for a system and make it work! Don't tell users: "At the moment gnome uses gstreamer as default, you can use xine with the following commands." When I'm new to Ubuntu I'm lost right here. I visited the Wiki to find out howto play those encrypted wmv files. I will probably think "nice let's try xine for a change, maybe wmv plays then" and run into a lot of trouble. 

6. Distinguish beetween the standard and the advanced (geek or expert) options. Mark everything potentially troublesome as clearly experimental and offer a "bring back the original" solution at every aspect of customization. 


I know that people don't like restrictions but on the other hand everyone likes expert suggestions. So I think there should be more pre-decision on look, usability $ documentation standards. I think this would also help to get applications ready for the desktop. I imagine that if the decision were to use gnucash as standard for the next release of Ubuntu a community call to support and codevelop the application, the documentation and the system integration would push the progress immensely. 

Still Ubuntu as a free system will not be able to compete with the MS or Apple world without more funding. But their is quite a lot of people out there that would love to dump their Windows box or save money on Apple in exchange for a husslefree and maybe not perfectly perfect but likeable sytem, that does what they expect
 


my two cents,
thx for reading, 
p.


It's depend what people really want.

---

### Post by mivo on 2009-12-11
> **Ylon said:**
> Yes, I've consider that Windows OSes are actually "choices"... but then I had to consider *everything else* and... No, that isn't a choice. Choice is something that you can afford a refuse, hardware manufacturer simply _can't_ refuse Windows.

Why would they even want to? And how exactly could they "refuse" Windows? For what purpose, and by what means? Hardware doesn't have any built in "Linux blocks". If hardware doesn't work it is because there are no drivers, and there are no drivers because a manufacturer doesn't see the Linux market as significant enough to justify the expenses. There are usually also no drivers for BSD, or for some other minority OSes. It's a free market. 

> By say "Windows is 90% of market" you're only merely adding more proof that is true.

No one stops you, or anyone, from installing Linux on your hardware. It's free. If I were in the business of selling pre-built computers, I would include Windows, too. Why? Because it's easier to maintain and keep updated , there is plenty of support (and trained support personnel) available, it's more desktop-ready than the alternatives (except OSX), and, above all, it is what people seem to want.

How do you propose to change it? Force vendors to include or support Linux when they do not want to? Is that your idea of freedom and choice?

> First Asus Netbook was a success product, the Microsoft rule was to not enter in it.. they changed their rule and suddenly the (left for death) XP Zombie returned. That wasn't a new product.. that was the "MS-XP Hardware Rule" pushed where the Microsoft claimed will never been.

There are manufacturers who took Linux netbooks off the market because customers brought them back as defective and wanted a Windows version. When I bought my netbook, I noticed that my (large) hardware store only had Windows models, even though some of the models were offered by the manufacturer with Linux, too. So, I asked why they don't carry them. The answer was that they used to have them in the catalog, but they didn't sell, so they removed them from the selection and now only offered Windows netbooks. That is not Microsoft's doing. That is what the free market decided.

You are so focused on what Microsoft allegedly does that you don't seem to see that vendors respond to market trends. If anything, Asus/etc. pushed Microsoft to offer cheaper licenses for XP, not the other way around, and apparently Asus/etc preferred XP than Linux because now Asus focuses on XP an W7 netbooks, and they seem to sell quite well.

I'm not surprised, either. Look at the drama every six months when a new Ubuntu version comes out (8.10 worked on my netbook, 9.10 no longer does). Why, as a vendor, would you possibly want to deal with this? It's much easier to bundle XP (and now W7 Starter which works well on the standard netbook specs), pay a few bucks extra and save yourself a ton of support expenses and the trouble with returned devices. 

Linux, on the desktop, will succeed or fail because of its own merits or shortcomings. Not because of what Microsoft does or doesn't do. Vista was a flop, and how much did the Linux marketshare increase during that time? A quarter of a percent? It seems to have stagnated in the year before Windows 7 came out.

What holds back Linux on the desktop, if anything does hold it back, is the state desktop Linux distros are in. There are a million of them, each doing their own thing, various packaging systems that are not directly compatible with each other, huge quality, stability and functionality differences between releases, a lack of unification (which is good for us power users, but a disadvantage for wide-spread success), etc.

A lot of people here are so focused on being "against" things (Windows, MS, Apple, OSX, commercial applications, patents, vendors, etc.) and so busy making excuses and conjuring up wild theories to explain why "outside forces" prevent Linux's breakthrough on the desktop, that they never stop to think about how Linux could improve.

Stop pushing against this or that and start focusing on improvement and progress. Intrinsic improvement and progress that is independent on what other companies, OS developers or communities do. Negativity does not produce positive results.

---

### Post by Roasted on 2009-12-11
> **Zoot7 said:**
> Well like it or not we do live in a capitalist world after all.

IMO Linux in general will never rise to a prominent marketshare as long as it's as fragmented on the desktop as it is now, due to the nature of entirely open source software. 
Thus, Independent Software Vendors and Hardware Vendors will never expend the excess effort required (in comparison to Windows) because they, as you said, don't see money in it.
As a result, like it or not, as it stands now Ubuntu (or any other distro for that matter) isn't going to compete with Windows or OSX on a viable level, nor do I envisage it will do so in the near future.

That said, I really do like Linux a lot, it offers so much more than Windows could even dream of for someone like me, and there are areas it absolutely trounces Windows IMO - customizability for example. 
But just because I use it, enjoy using it, and have introduced friends and family to it with a very positive reception, doesn't mean it's ready for mass adoption across the entire Desktop sector, because of the nature of the way it is now.
Whatever you may say about Windows short comings, this is the grim reality.

Anyway that's my take on it.

I think competing with OSX and Windows can be taken two different ways.

In my opinion, and I cannot tell you how firmly I believe this, Linux absolutely positively can compete against OSX and Windows. But I speak this from a functionality standpoint, user friendliness standpoint, etc. The nature of open source software ensures that Linux has limitless capabilities, and in my opinion, already far exceeded the standards that OSX and Windows can only ever dream of achieving.

On the flip side, you can talk about market share too. Yeah, you might have it there. *buntu might not be top dog when it comes to market share, or even close to it, but you also have to consider what the aim is for the operating system itself. They don't advertise. There's no commercials. There's not even very cheap CDs in stores for people to pick up based on curiosity standpoint. It's by word of mouth. People talking. Oh, I can just burn it and install it? Okay, I'll check it out. Afterwards, addiction sets in. :P 

You have to consider, Windows and OSX are known because of their commercials, the Apple stores, the "I'm a PC" ads, the other products they carry such as the iPod, iTouch, Zune, Xbox, etc. They have a foundation, a name, an establishment. They have a damn corporate business.

Look at Linux. What do they have? They have open source software development, support, and do a damn good job at it for laptops, netbooks, desktops, and servers, whether it be a high end server or a little home file server. They do a damn good job at what their target is. How does Linux get spread again? Word of mouth.

No ads.
No business.
No other products.
Linux doesn't have any commercials.
No portable mp3 players.
No game consoles.
Just... Linux.

It's all by word of mouth.

When you consider that, in comparison to how OSX and Windows have the market share they do, suddenly what little ground Linux holds in the world is actually VERY, VERY commendable considering the path they took to get there.

### Post by Ivo Moelans on 2009-12-11
> **mivo said:**
> If I were in the business of selling pre-built computers, I would include Windows, too. Why? Because it's easier to maintain and keep updated , there is plenty of support (and trained support personnel) available, it's more desktop-ready than the alternatives (except OSX), and, above all, it is what people seem to want.


If I were in that business I would probably too. Only because people are brainwashed into thinking computer=Windows. And I would charge heavily for deborking their PC every six months or so. If I wanted to make money with computers, I would be pushing Windows like hell.

The rest of your reasons? You've got to be kidding. Easier to maintain and keep updated? That's a joke and not a vey good one at that. Ubuntu is updated on a daily basis. More frequently if necessary. Windows... once a month. If a bug, a leak or something else is discovered right after Update Tuesday you can wait for a whole month. Updating Windows requires rebooting. Updating Ubuntu not (except when the kernel or x.org is updated). I just keep working while updating.

This goes for the OS and all programs installed via repositories. In Windows some software have their own, unpredictable, update routine. Sometimes you have to pay for that-- again. Others... you have to look after it yourself. The only thing that updates daily in Windows is the virus scanner. What is a virus? I forgot.

Ubuntu is the easiest system to update, bar none.

Yes for Windows there is more support and personnel. It **needs** it. And it costs money... again.

Ubuntu is more than desktop ready. Granted, some refinements could be made on the UI. Coming from XP with it's antiquated single workspace, bloated Explorer etc., Ubuntu feels as a major upgrade.

But you are right. What MS has done right is the marketing. They seem to have convinced a majority that theirs is the only good OS. Of course, that won't last. E.g. Europe is going more and more the way of FOS. A lot of people, including me, wouldn't want to go back to the nightmare, the driver-hell, the unhelpful wizards, the rigid way of organizing and doing things that is Windows. The proof of the pudding is in the eating. I wouldn't go back if *they* paid *me*.

Granted, the brainwashing runs so deep that it will take a few decades. But think back a few decades when fat, shiny American cars where what a car was meant to be. Not any longer.

### Post by mivo on 2009-12-11
> **Ylon said:**
> Windows Seven (unable to make Vista, XP, 98 things work)

I skipped most of your post, but I'll respond to this claim, because it's symptomatic of most of your statements: It is either unintentionally uninformed and well-meaning FUD, or it is deliberate FUD. Either way, you make so many claims that, in my opinion, either make no sense or can't be backed up. Share some links and hard facts with us. What (relevant) Vista and XP programs don't work in Windows 7? Which manufacturer went out of their way to please Microsoft? What's the percentage of  returned defective Windows netbooks vs. the percentage of returned Linux netbooks?

The cited bit is one of those statements. I have Windows 7, and I have not found one Windows application or game to not work in it. This includes XP and even pre-XP software. (Note here that I only have the professional version and no CPU that supports virtualization, so the XP "emulation" does not play a role.) To me, it's fairly obvious that you are not running Windows 7 (hey, you miss out on a pretty good experience - as someone who didn't care much for Vista, I am genuinely impressed with how well it performs, even after several months now).

There is nothing wrong with Linux, and it works fine on the two computers I use it on. But that doesn't mean it's somehow the holy grail of software engineering. At its core, it was meant for servers, and desktop functionality was an afterthought. Much work has been done, and much work is being done, but it isn't anywhere near Windows 7 or OSX when it comes to seamless integration and user-friendliness. It does require more competence and more learning, but it also does allow for more choice (and more opportunity to mess things up, seeing how everyone here uses sudo like it's the remote control of their satellite TV receiver).

I dearly love both my Ubuntu box and, especially, my Arch laptop. But because I have years of experience with running Linux, I also know that while I can give anyone a copy of Windows and they'll be more or elss fine, I know I can't do the same with Linux. Not right now. (I had less trouble with a FreeBSD desktop four years ago than I had with 9.10 last month.) Again, I'm not saying a Linux desktop can't be a fine desktop. It can be that. I just feel it's suited for fewer people than Windows is. Currently.

See, I have given dozens of Live CDs to people, when I felt someone might be interested. The resonance has never been overwhelming. I stopped using Ubuntu's Live CD for then when I realised the brown theme is a turn-off and people always seem to go straight to Youtube or try to play back an MP3 file or a DVD (and none of that worked). But even with distros that are a little less US law supporting, people still see nothing that motivates them to switch. There's no iTunes (like it or not, it is popular) and a ton of other software people are used to. Gnome looks dated, and KDE 4, after two years of an apparently public alpha test, is only now becoming attractive and stable.

Then there's the repositories. Good idea, if you don't want the latest versions (Arch and other rolling distros are an exception, but they don't make the best first Linux choices). There are many benefits to them. But there are downsides, too. So, three days ago Thunderbird 3 came out. As a Windows user, you go to mozilla.org, download the exe, install it, and you are done. If you are a Linux user, you can also go to mozilla.org. And download a zipped tar ball. **Oops!** Not only is it a zipped tar ball, but it's actually the source code. Now, some Linux users can compile it. However, I can tell you that my elderly mother couldn't. But she can download the Windows exe and install it. And you will find numerous other examples like this.

Linux will eventually grow out of this phase, though, and I think that six-months releases will eventually become obsolete (if they don't, or the process isn't really improved so that you don't usually have to do a fresh install, market share will never increase to a significant level). In the meantime, the best you can do is to help support Linux. Not by bashing Windows or OSX, or weaving baseless, unfounded conspiracy theories that you fail to back up with any actual, verifiable data, but by helping people. You write long, political manifests, all about Microsoft, Windows, Apple, but looking at your beans (post count outside of this chat area), you have not really spent much time in the support areas of the forum.

That is one place to start to make Linux/Ubuntu an even better alternative to Windows or OSX. The advocacy stuff is entertaining for the Café crew, but it is never really productive and it's only something to do while you wait for the coffee to get ready or for a program to finish compiling.

---

### Post by Roasted on 2009-12-11
> **mivo said:**
> 
I also know that while I can give anyone a copy of Windows and they'll be more or elss fine, I know I can't do the same with Linux. Not right now. 


This comment stuck out to me...

You need to remember, Windows is familiar to the eye of everybody. People know that start button, the windows logo, etc. Even people who aren't interested in technology... They know it because it's everywhere. Does that mean Windows is easier to use than Linux, like you suggested above? Absolutely, positively, not.

ZDNet put this to the test with KDE 4, telling everybody it was Windows 7. Everybody was fooled, and thought it was extremely easy to use. Oh wait, KDE runs on Linux. ;) There's a video of this on YouTube, check it out.

It's not that Linux is hard to use. It's just very different. What if we could just clear everybody's memory cache in the back of their brain so they have no idea what an operating system even is. THEN dump Windows in front of them. Suddenly it seems much harder to use, doesn't it? 

Linux is definitely ready for the desktops. I believe it has been for a while now. However, Linux is not familiar to people, and most people don't know about it and what it can do for them. 

But there's two powerful things the *buntu linux community has that Windows does not:

1 - A LiveCD, to allow people to try out the operating system before installing it. Can you do this with Windows? Pfft, no. In fact, Windows will format your hard drive, copy the files, THEN ask for the registration key. Last month I installed 7 on my Aunt's laptop, but the one digit I couldn't figure out since her lovely chihuahua chewed on the case. Case and point - Vista laptop down, Windows 7 unable to install. User friendly, really. Ultimately we figured out the missing digit, but not without a lengthy headache of trail-and-error troubleshooting. Would have been nice to have the key pop up first so if I couldn't figure it out, I could at least reboot back to Vista, have it running, and call Microsoft in the morning.

2 - A forum user-base with a vast amount of knowledgable users, who post here frequently and always offer constructive help, ideas, etc. Ever post on the Windows forums? I have. I'm lucky if I get 10 views on my newly posted thread in 5 days time, whereas here I'll post a thread and have 10 replies in an hour.

### Post by aysiu on 2009-12-11
I guess it depends on what you meanby *more or less fine*.

Even though I am not in any kind of official helpdesk position, I have been *de facto* tech support for Windows-using family, friends, and even co-workers.

The vast majority of Windows users need help and very often. If they didn't have Windows power users in their social circles, they would pay the Geek Squad to rip them off. Most Windows users are not fine with Windows on their own. They keep using Windows because they need Windows-only programs, are they're afraid of change, or it just came with their computer.

### Post by Zoot7 on 2009-12-11
> **Roasted said:**
> I think competing with OSX and Windows can be taken two different ways.

In my opinion, and I cannot tell you how firmly I believe this, Linux absolutely positively can compete against OSX and Windows. But I speak this from a functionality standpoint, user friendliness standpoint, etc. The nature of open source software ensures that Linux has limitless capabilities, and in my opinion, already far exceeded the standards that OSX and Windows can only ever dream of achieving.
I agree about user friendliness, provided you don't care about the latest version of every piece of software. 
I am of the opinion that either gnome or kde (never really used the other desktops so I can't comment) are far far easier to find your way around at both an advanced and basic level than either the XP, Vista, or Windows 7 desktop. (I can't comment on OSX).
Just to take an example, last year I put my parents on Ubuntu. The logic was that they're the types that just use their machines for internet browsing, skype, music/video, they're also the typical types who want their machines to *just work* when they turn them on, and don't want to be bothering with Anti-Virus apps etc. All both of them really needed were a few icons on the desktop to switch over to it completely.
Both of them are now are very capable of finding their way competently around the gnome desktop much much easier than XP.
That's a testament to the user friendliness of Ubuntu (don't know how they'd get on with another distro, but if it was gnome based, probably just as good.)

> **Roasted said:**
> You have to consider, Windows and OSX are known because of their commercials, the Apple stores, the "I'm a PC" ads, the other products they carry such as the iPod, iTouch, Zune, Xbox, etc. They have a foundation, a name, an establishment. They have a damn corporate business.

Look at Linux. What do they have? They have open source software development, support, and do a damn good job at it for laptops, netbooks, desktops, and servers, whether it be a high end server or a little home file server. They do a damn good job at what their target is. How does Linux get spread again? Word of mouth.

No ads.
No business.
No other products.
Linux doesn't have any commercials.
No portable mp3 players.
No game consoles.
Just... Linux.

It's all by word of mouth.

When you consider that, in comparison to how OSX and Windows have the market share they do, suddenly what little ground Linux holds in the world is actually VERY, VERY commendable considering the path they took to get there.
That is true, but from a standpoint of attracting Hardware and Software vendors Linux ain't going anywhere really.
It's simply too fragmented, packaging is one example (among many others).
For instance take this from an end users point of view when they want to install software.
For Windows you download your executable and double click, done.
The only thing that approaches this in Linux is if you download a package for your distro, and that'll work fine if you're on a fairly well supported up to date distro with a widely used package manager. But if you're on an older version you could even run into dependencies when installing it etc.
There are other conundrums Linux can also introduce such as kernel patches breaking a driver (this happens to me on a periodic basis with Ubuntu these days).

Anyway here's how I think Linux in general could compete with Windows or OSX.
Firstly standardize it to a degree where it's less messy for Software vendors to port their software to it, or for Hardware Vendors to develop drivers for it.
Stick with solid, tried and tested bases (much like Red Hat does).
And also if dependencies weren't the issue they can be for people on older distributions (Hardy now for example).
...There's other issues aswell, but they're the first that come to mind at the moment.

That's my take on it. Ubuntu has come in leaps and bounds as of late but it's not there as a comercial alternative to Windows for everyone yet.
JMO though...

### Post by Roasted on 2009-12-11
> **Zoot7 said:**
> I agree about user friendliness, provided you don't care about the latest version of every piece of software. 
I am of the opinion that either gnome or kde (never really used the other desktops so I can't comment) are far far easier to find your way around at both an advanced and basic level than either the XP, Vista, or Windows 7 desktop. (I can't comment on OSX).
Just to take an example, last year I put my parents on Ubuntu. The logic was that they're the types that just use their machines for internet browsing, skype, music/video, they're also the typical types who want their machines to *just work* when they turn them on, and don't want to be bothering with Anti-Virus apps etc. All both of them really needed were a few icons on the desktop to switch over to it completely.
Both of them are now are very capable of finding their way competently around the gnome desktop much much easier than XP.
That's a testament to the user friendliness of Ubuntu (don't know how they'd get on with another distro, but if it was gnome based, probably just as good.)


That is true, but from a standpoint of attracting Hardware and Software vendors Linux ain't going anywhere really.
It's simply too fragmented, packaging is one example (among many others).
For instance take this from an end users point of view when they want to install software.
For Windows you download your executable and double click, done.
The only thing that approaches this in Linux is if you download a package for your distro, and that'll work fine if you're on a fairly well supported up to date distro with a widely used package manager. But if you're on an older version you could even run into dependencies when installing it etc.
There are other conundrums Linux can also introduce such as kernel patches breaking a driver (this happens to me on a periodic basis with Ubuntu these days).

Anyway here's how I think Linux in general could compete with Windows or OSX.
Firstly standardize it to a degree where it's less messy for Software vendors to port their software to it, or for Hardware Vendors to develop drivers for it.
Stick with solid, tried and tested bases (much like Red Hat does).
And also if dependencies weren't the issue they can be for people on older distributions (Hardy now for example).
...There's other issues aswell, but they're the first that come to mind at the moment.

That's my take on it. Ubuntu has come in leaps and bounds as of late but it's not there as a comercial alternative to Windows for everyone yet.
JMO though...

You bring up some very valid points, and while I agree with you, perhaps our personal opinions are just somewhat conflicting to minor degrees. Mostly because I believe *buntu is very capable of being a desktop OS. It's one thing if somebody needs photoshop and all kinds of other Windows-specific programs, but if you take the average joe who just needs internet, a place to store music, pictures, etc, it's really a very capable operating system.

And granted, you do bring up a point. Linux needs to standardize. That's a given. However, fortunately for us *buntu users, I feel as though (not to sound self centered and full of myself) that *buntu would more or less be higher on the list to port software/drivers to because of *buntu being the most popular desktop distro out there. Granted, you really have two forks - Debian and RPM - and as long as Debian ports work then we're in the clear, along with a lot of other distros.

I don't really agree with what you said in regard to Linux is going no where in terms of software vendor support, mostly because I came from the days of Linux where there wasn't any flash support. To use YouTube, you had to bounce back over to Windows. Look at it now. We have flash support. We have nvidia drivers. We have ATI drivers. Linux tends to have better hardware detection than Windows. If that's the case, how can it be said that Linux is going no where in terms of software support? I believe the exact opposite, I feel as though Linux is moving at a very rapid pace.

But hey, at the end of the day, you have to use what works no matter what operating system it is. It just so happens Linux does a better job in every category for me, so that's a win there. :)

> You bring up some very valid points, and while I agree with you, perhaps our personal opinions are just somewhat conflicting to minor degrees. Mostly because I believe *buntu is very capable of being a desktop OS. **It's one thing if somebody needs photoshop and all kinds of other Windows-specific programs, but if you take the average joe who just needs internet, a place to store music, pictures, etc, it's really a very capable operating system**.
It is indeed, for the vast majority of people yes, provided their hardware is linux friendly and they, as you say just want the basic day to day apps. That's generally what I base my introduction of Ubuntu to others on. 

> **Roasted said:**
> I don't really agree with what you said in regard to Linux is going no where in terms of software vendor support, mostly because I came from the days of Linux where there wasn't any flash support. To use YouTube, you had to bounce back over to Windows. Look at it now. We have flash support. We have nvidia drivers. We have ATI drivers. Linux tends to have better hardware detection than Windows. If that's the case, how can it be said that Linux is going no where in terms of software support? I believe the exact opposite, I feel as though Linux is moving at a very rapid pace.
True it has come in leaps and bounds as of late. It's getting there, but it's not quite there yet.
My point was it's just not a viable platform for many Hardware Vendors or Software Vendors to officially support the way they do Windows. For instance it'd be a lot of work for say.. Adobe to port Photoshop to Linux and it doesn't justify the effort and expenses required. 
For instance when you can support ~90% of the market in one sweep with support for a single platform - Windows - why put the same effort into the other 10%, especially when the other 10% is a lot more work to do so. The ends don't justify the means, they just don't see money in it, and it is after all a Capitalism fueled system for Commercial Hardware and Software vendors.
I'm just being practical, I would love for this not to be the case, but sadly it is, and it's not going to change for the imminent future. 

> **Roasted said:**
> But hey, at the end of the day, you have to use what works no matter what operating system it is. It just so happens Linux does a better job in every category for me, so that's a win there. :)
Ditto! For me Linux trounces Windows. I'd dump the latter totally if I could run my games and my specialized Windows only apps under it. :)
Mind you, I did buy Windows 7 under the student offer and I'm glad I did (it's good, but as far as I'm concerned it's no Linux ;) ).

### Post by mivo on 2009-12-11
> **Ylon said:**
> Again, if your ATI work well on your Windows with Windows driver.. is not a merit of the Microsoft work.. but the ATI good job. My point is this: get the damn ATI plan good driver for Linux (nvidia was able toa and didn't go bankrupt).

Except one, all my computers have an Nvidia video card/chipset. Oh, and my netbook has an Intel chipset. In the past few years I deliberately bought Nvidia cards because they work well in both Windows and Linux.

ATI's Linux support has always been poor. The open source drivers shape up nicely, but the closed drivers have always been the pits. One of my older computers (now retired) had an ATI X700pro. It was loud already in Windows, but in Linux the fan always blew at full speed (no fan control in ATI's drivers) so that it sounded like a turbine. I don't mind noise all that much (grew up in the center of a large city), but that was too much for me. I put XP back on it and got a new computer with an Nvidia card for Linux (it had a problematic Abit board, and had all sorts of issues with Ubuntu 7.04, but that's another topic).

But see, Microsoft doesn't make ATI suck at making Linux drivers. They just didn't want to, or saw the need for it, but instead, eventually, released the driver specs. Nvidia does make good Linux drivers, but doesn't open the specs. Both could do better.

### Post by Frak on 2009-12-11
> **mivo said:**
> But see, Microsoft doesn't make ATI suck at making Linux drivers. They just didn't want to, or saw the need for it, but instead, eventually, released the driver specs. Nvidia does make good Linux drivers, but doesn't open the specs. Both could do better.

Well, take Nvidia for a second. Much of the driver codebase is the same between Linux and Windows, seriously. It works natively in Windows, and is wrapped on Linux. But here's the thing, I can't release a proprietary driver for Linux, I can't. I have to make it run in userspace, which is limiting in a large amount of ways. Though, even if I could release proprietary drivers for Linux (like I can for BSD), Linux doesn't have a stable ABI to work with. As an example, Windows and OS X have a stable kernel ABI that rarely changes. Every release it undergoes a slight change, but nothing difficult for the IHV to fix. Linux, on the other hand, doesn't have a stable ABI. As an aside, the Linux foundation scorns Windows ABI because it does slightly change over time, so it is not always a stable implementation for older drivers (though there are new interfaces to replace them). Linux, on the other hand, they tout as being great because the ABI is unstable. They claim that your driver will automatically update itself because the community is there to update it, which is logical fallacy at best (Apple and Microsoft can say the IHV will update the driver, which is right. If they want the driver to exist, they'll update it. Linux, on the other hand, assumes that people will come out of the woods to fix the driver.)

Linux needs a stable, standard ABI for IHVs to develop against. This whole "just open the code, and we'll do all the work!" crap isn't going to fly with companies. There are secrets hidden within the code that a lot of IHVs don't want to lose.
