### Post by rsvr85 on 2009-12-12
I have been using Ubuntu on and off for the last few months and consider myself a complete and utter noob.
I'm not here to just slag off Ubuntu or start a flaming match with anyone, i'm merely stating my opinions.
For the most part, what lets Ubuntu down (at least for me) is it's user un-friendliness.  When i first installed Jaunty, i wanted a dock for my desktop.  OK, Firefox open, Google "dock for jaunty".....what the hell are PPA's, GPU Keys, why are there several ways of installing software, why do i have to type my password in every 10 seconds, why do i have to open the terminal and gpedit this and that to update software sources (what ever the hell that is)??  These are things that put people off straight away.
I hear people screaming from the roof tops that Ubuntu has the best support out there, the community is what keeps it going, it's amazing.  I'm sorry but i agree with [Reston Marcus]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=965610") (AKA ???????), i have sought help here a couple of times, and have either been greeted with rash responses, none at all or half hearted support.  How are people meant to get started or get themselves out of trouble?  It's almost like you guys persecute newbies.

I want to love Ubuntu, i really do, i like the concept & ethics but i find it hard.  The OS out the box is ugly, un-polished, buggy and too damn hard for an average user to use.  Luckily, i'm an experienced Windows user so that's helped somewhat, but not much.

Ubuntu will never come close Windows or OS X, Ubuntu should have tried to capitalize when MS looked like they were digging themselves into a grave with Vista, they didn't and will probably live to regret it.  Windows 7 is a [b]massive[b] improvement over Vista.  It's fast, efficient, stable, looks amazing and most important of all, user friendly.  I doubt they'll release another bad OS for sometime.  Of couse, Windows has it's flaws but they seem like nothing when you spend 3hrs trying to get your wireless driver to work or reading up on sudo for hours on end becasue the apt-get just will not work.

Ubuntu users seem to rely heavily on Compiz to goad new users in, it won't be forever until someone ports a similar app to Windows, then what!? :p  

At the end of the day, each to their own.  You get what you pay for.  Windows 7 cost me £50 ($110 approx) and i think it's worth it, it simply works out of the box and after the initial updates i've had nothing but smooth computing from minute one.  Ubuntu cost me nothing (i even took advantage of the free CD's) and has taken hours of research and reading in these very forums to get a *very* basic understanding of how things work.  The terminal for a novice user is a pretty intimidating prospect too, especially when some nob eds in forums (not this one) tell you that typing;
rm -rf /
rm -rf .
rm -rf *
will solve all of your driver troubles.  Or doing a sudo????.....i've now read about sudo and understand how it works but average joe won't know this of have the inclination to learn either.  
IMO, the most important thing for Ubuntu, is it needs to get more user friendly before it expects any sort of market share.

Thanks for reading :)

P.S - [Ylon]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=131669"); Free technical support is included in the price of retail Windows 7 ;)

### Post by Zoot7 on 2009-12-12
> **mivo said:**
> But see, Microsoft doesn't make ATI suck at making Linux drivers. They just didn't want to, or saw the need for it, but instead, eventually, released the driver specs. Nvidia does make good Linux drivers, but doesn't open the specs. Both could do better.
For the record, ATi (in comparison to Nvidia) have had their fair share of problems with Windows drivers aswell.

> **Frak said:**
> Linux needs a stable, standard ABI for IHVs to develop against. This whole "just open the code, and we'll do all the work!" crap isn't going to fly with companies. There are secrets hidden within the code that a lot of IHVs don't want to lose.
Which is why you've several IHV's that are particularly hostile towards open source, case in point - Broadcom. It's a grim reality for Linux and it's not going to change anytime soon. Solution for the Linux user - avoid their products, which isn't really viable for an OS that is touted to take on Windows or OSX IMO.

> **rsvr85 said:**
> I want to love Ubuntu, i really do, i like the concept & ethics but i find it hard.  The OS out the box is ugly, un-polished, buggy and too damn hard for an average user to use.  Luckily, i'm an experienced Windows user so that's helped somewhat, but not much.
Agreed on the ugliness out of the box. But whether it's user friendly or not is subjective. Mostly it's not actually hard to use, just different. for instance I'd wager if you put a reasonably competent Windows user in front of a KDE desktop then they'd find their way around easily after a while.
The software installation path is different, I agree. But is it really prohibitively user unfriendly? I don't think so, once you get used to it anyway. It's different and in some cases is more involved but apps like Synaptic and the Software Centre do make it easier.
But to customize to your hearts content, you're going to have to get down and dirty with it which is a lot more hands on than Windows any day.
That said there are a lot of short comings that Windows just doesn't have, dependency issues as I mentioned before are one such pitfall.
Anyway, if somebody is coming from Windows and Windows is all they know then by default if something is not Windows it isn't going to user friendly to them.
However put someone in front of Ubuntu (or a similar Linux distro) from day 1 and they'll probably find Windows very hard to use as a result. Every OS has a learning curve, some more steep than others.

As for buggyness, the releases to stick with for Stability are normally the LTSs, for instance if you use Hardy now, as long as your hardware is linux friendly you should have pretty much zero problems with it from a stability standpoint. Karmic IMO is a testing release for Lucid, there were a lot of features not ready for their primetime. I do like Ubuntu a lot but I wouldn't pretend for a second that someone should install a new release until it's out for at least a month, nor would I recommend people to use every release either, maybe skip every second one and wait for a month after release, and even then do a clean install. Which is hardly a path for a Mainstream OS geared at the masses IMO.

So I do agree, just not 100%. :)

> **rsvr85 said:**
> Ubuntu will never come close Windows or OS X, Ubuntu should have tried to capitalize when MS looked like they were digging themselves into a grave with Vista, they didn't and will probably live to regret it.  Windows 7 is a [b]massive[b] improvement over Vista.  It's fast, efficient, stable, looks amazing and most important of all, user friendly.  I doubt they'll release another bad OS for sometime.  Of couse, Windows has it's flaws but they seem like nothing when you spend 3hrs trying to get your wireless driver to work or reading up on sudo for hours on end becasue the apt-get just will not work.
Agreed on Windows 7, but I wouldn't say it's a *massive* improvement really. It is a good OS and I do like it a lot, but IMO it's not too dissimilar to Vista after 2 service packs now. Mind you, I'd still choose it over Vista though.
As for wireless card problem, it depends really. If you do your research and look up some Linux friendly hardware, you can have a "Mac Like" experience with Ubuntu, ie. where things *just work*.
That said though, even then there are pitfalls that aren't present with Windows.

> **rsvr85 said:**
> Ubuntu users seem to rely heavily on Compiz to goad new users in, it won't be forever until someone ports a similar app to Windows, then what!? :p
There's one already on Windows that does the Desktop cube among other effects is there not?

This led companies like Google and Opera to host their own repositories (.deb party, apt-get political structure).
You give *apt-get upgrade* on your terminal.. and you get Opera9, Opera 10, Opera 11 and so go on.


Microsoft way is... China way.
A _unique_ perfect structure that wouldn't be modified until the "ruler part" (Microsoft) didn't choice which is the best way to make your computer work.

### Post by airtonix on 2009-12-12
> **powerpleb said:**
> I whole heartedly agree with that. Also, it is frequently those sort of users who just have to have the latest and greatest shiny hardware too, which usually take some time to be fully supported by Linux and this is where the majority of complaints about Linux stem from. By the time the super-duper fancy USB soundcard has become supported in Linux, the Windows uber-power user has already got bored with it and bought a newer and even more expensive one.

These users aren't really 'power users' in the sense that they envisage themselves.

They are just victims of 1) microsofts hold on the hardware driver development ideals and, 2) that something free or something bought has more or less value than either.

> 
Some site are try to copy this (old) feature from much time on Linux. Still, they (the Windows service) are unable to propose software updates as Linux do.


Microsoft can't & won't do this because of the philosophy of profit and the restrictive nature of the user license behind their operating system.

---

### Post by Frak on 2009-12-12
> **airtonix said:**
> These users aren't really 'power users' in the sense that they envisage themselves.

They are just victims of 1) microsofts hold on the hardware driver development ideals and, 2) that something free or something bought has more or less value than either.

Trust me, Microsoft doesn't have and doesn't need a hold on driver development. As I said earlier, Linux itself is stopping driver developers from contributing. With Microsoft, you can release your source code or not release your source code, and have a driver that will remain relatively stable for generations to come. The implementation only slightly changes between release. With Linux, you are **forced** to release your source code, and the driver will need to be updated every generation. It is the same with userspace drivers as well on the deprecation front. Linux and its userspace change too much, too often, and becomes a heavy burden on the IHV.

### Post by RickSGM on 2009-12-12
I for one love Ubuntu.  I have it on my desktop, plus a dual boot with Vista on my laptop, and have just installed it on my wife's desktop as well.  

There was a problem with Wubi on the laptop, but it's fixed now.  We don't have any high end machines.  We only could afford the ones that were priced low and on sale.  Ubuntu works great with those.  

I tend to push a computer hard, as does the granddaughter when she's here.  When my wife had vista, she always came to use my desktop because it would do all she wanted to do, where vista wouldn't.  My wife now has Ubuntu on her desktop, and dearly loves it.  It's not lagging or giving her heck on the games she goes to.

I can't speak for the high end games, for we mainly do pogo, facebook apps, and games like that.  We occasionally go to youtube, use office software (OpenOffice is good, btw).
The only thing bad is Wine doesn't do the mirc script I use very good.  That's the only reason we have an installation of windows in the house.  As for me, I give a thumbs up to ubuntu.  Just a few things in the command line to get flash and java right, and it's been great.  

I'm not a geek or superuser, nor am I totally ignorant on computers either....maybe know just enough to be dangerous.  lol

BTW:  the help on the forums here has been good.  Only had one problem that I couldn't get solved, but it was no biggie.

---

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2009-12-12
With Linux's enigmatic public image, it's easy to assume just about anything of what people think of it and why it "hasn't caught on yet."

If you live where I do, people have a very different opinion of Windows and OS X. It's more between Linux and OS X here than Windows vs anything else. Linux has a lot of support in my community, and plenty of people use it primarily.

I think it might be because of all the Linux netbooks, to be honest. XD

### Post by Chame_Wizard on 2009-12-12
> **rsvr85 said:**
> I have been using Ubuntu on and off for the last few months and consider myself a complete and utter noob.
I'm not here to just slag off Ubuntu or start a flaming match with anyone, i'm merely stating my opinions.
For the most part, what lets Ubuntu down (at least for me) is it's user un-friendliness.  When i first installed Jaunty, i wanted a dock for my desktop.  OK, Firefox open, Google "dock for jaunty".....what the hell are PPA's, GPU Keys, why are there several ways of installing software, why do i have to type my password in every 10 seconds, why do i have to open the terminal and gpedit this and that to update software sources (what ever the hell that is)??  These are things that put people off straight away.
I hear people screaming from the roof tops that Ubuntu has the best support out there, the community is what keeps it going, it's amazing.  I'm sorry but i agree with [Reston Marcus]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=965610") (AKA ???????), i have sought help here a couple of times, and have either been greeted with rash responses, none at all or half hearted support.  How are people meant to get started or get themselves out of trouble?  It's almost like you guys persecute newbies.

I want to love Ubuntu, i really do, i like the concept & ethics but i find it hard.  The OS out the box is ugly, un-polished, buggy and too damn hard for an average user to use.  Luckily, i'm an experienced Windows user so that's helped somewhat, but not much.

Ubuntu will never come close Windows or OS X, Ubuntu should have tried to capitalize when MS looked like they were digging themselves into a grave with Vista, they didn't and will probably live to regret it.  Windows 7 is a [b]massive[b] improvement over Vista.  It's fast, efficient, stable, looks amazing and most important of all, user friendly.  I doubt they'll release another bad OS for sometime.  Of couse, Windows has it's flaws but they seem like nothing when you spend 3hrs trying to get your wireless driver to work or reading up on sudo for hours on end becasue the apt-get just will not work.

Ubuntu users seem to rely heavily on Compiz to goad new users in, it won't be forever until someone ports a similar app to Windows, then what!? :p  

At the end of the day, each to their own.  You get what you pay for.  Windows 7 cost me £50 ($110 approx) and i think it's worth it, it simply works out of the box and after the initial updates i've had nothing but smooth computing from minute one.  Ubuntu cost me nothing (i even took advantage of the free CD's) and has taken hours of research and reading in these very forums to get a *very* basic understanding of how things work.  The terminal for a novice user is a pretty intimidating prospect too, especially when some nob eds in forums (not this one) tell you that typing;
rm -rf /
rm -rf .
rm -rf *
will solve all of your driver troubles.  Or doing a sudo????.....i've now read about sudo and understand how it works but average joe won't know this of have the inclination to learn either.  
IMO, the most important thing for Ubuntu, is it needs to get more user friendly before it expects any sort of market share.

Thanks for reading :)

P.S - [Ylon]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=131669"); Free technical support is included in the price of retail Windows 7 ;)
What the heck are you talking about?google on "kubuntu guide","PPA" or "ubuntu guide" is enough.):P

> I for one love Ubuntu.  I have it on my desktop, plus a dual boot with Vista on my laptop, and have just installed it on my wife's desktop as well.  

There was a problem with Wubi on the laptop, but it's fixed now.  We don't have any high end machines.  We only could afford the ones that were priced low and on sale.  Ubuntu works great with those.  

I tend to push a computer hard, as does the granddaughter when she's here.  When my wife had vista, she always came to use my desktop because it would do all she wanted to do, where vista wouldn't.  My wife now has Ubuntu on her desktop, and dearly loves it.  It's not lagging or giving her heck on the games she goes to.

I can't speak for the high end games, for we mainly do pogo, facebook apps, and games like that.  We occasionally go to youtube, use office software (OpenOffice is good, btw).
The only thing bad is Wine doesn't do the mirc script I use very good.  That's the only reason we have an installation of windows in the house.  As for me, I give a thumbs up to ubuntu.  Just a few things in the command line to get flash and java right, and it's been great.  

I'm not a geek or superuser, nor am I totally ignorant on computers either....maybe know just enough to be dangerous.  lol

BTW:  the help on the forums here has been good.  Only had one problem that I couldn't get solved, but it was no biggie.

I definitely agree about the older hardware, i recently bought my my girlfriend an old, cheapy Toshiba Satellite lappy as she uses it for similar purposes to yourselves, i got it with XP SP2 on it.  I of course, clean reinstalled as soon as i got it home and it ran like molasses, it was terrible.  I straight away clean installed Jaunty on it and it's been good as gold.  We've had a few mishaps with it but nothing too serious.  Ubuntu is definitely softer on hardware requirements and resources than Windows ever will be.  I have 3GB RAM in my PC and there's no need for 2/3 of it, even with compiz fully enabled and running beautifully!  However, this is surely a trade of for some of the finer parts of the Windows GUI (Aero).  

Gaming is also big draw back for Ubuntu users.  Before i even looked at an Ubuntu desktop before i was under the impression that you couldn't really play games on a Linux/Ubuntu machine, of course now i know this isn't true, but i wasn't the only person under this illusion, which isn't a good thing.

### Post by Uadebisi on 2009-12-12
I have been on the Ubuntu path for a couple of weeks as I have grown tired of AV, AS & AM scans, FP's & possible infections with Windows. So, I was thinking of a Mac until my website tech support guy suggested Ubuntu. Well, I played with the Live CD for a week & liked Ubuntu very much so, I saved it to my HD & ran a dual boot with XP. The only compatibility issue I had was with my HP 1020 printer which only required me to download a plugin from HP, and that was quite easy. I liked Ubuntu so much that after only two days, I wiped out Windows!

Keep in mind that I am an average computer user. I would say that I know more about computers than most people I know but, I am not a tech expert by any means. When I speak with those that are, I realize how little I actually know. I learn what I need to learn when I need to learn it. On the first couple of pages in my first thread here, I sound as if I have no computer experience at all, as I was in uncharted territories. But I learn quickly ;)

So, back to my Windows removal....ever since that day, about 6 days ago, I have had nothing but problems with Ubuntu. It has been one issue after another. I spent virtually every waking moment for a few days in this forum, trying to rectify my problems. After a couple of days of getting support, I have been unable to get any further help, & believe me I have tried. So, not only is Ubuntu glitchy, but the support has been lacking for me as well. I realize that everyone here volunteers their time & for that I am grateful but when I cannot get the help I need, where does that leave me? My main computer is still not fully functional & I still have questions as to why some of the initial issues are seemingly now resolved. This whole Ubuntu experience has been extremely time consuming & a huge inconvenience on many levels, as we used this computer for most all of our needs. 

Before I experienced any of the problems, I ordered a new machine from Sytem76, who have since convinced me that I should not experience the same sort of issues that I have had installing Ubuntu on my Windows machine. BTW, the folks at System76 have been absolutely incredible to work with thus far. They have already gone above & beyond the call of duty.

I have begun to lose hope that I will ever receive the help I need in this forum. Although, the more I play around with & customize my Ubuntu install, the more I like it, I may still need to go back to Windows, as presently, whenever I restart, my screen resolution changes changes requiring me to manually reset my icons on the desktop & tables. It's just a pain in the neck & I still wonder if my printer will continue to function or if I will again experience start-up issues, as I am not entirely sure why such problems have been remedied. I also found a strange file in my trash that I did not put there. I need to know what the story is with that as well.

So, in summary, up until this point, I would have to agree with the original post in this forum, which I find very disappointing. It has been said that the definition of Ubuntu is "an ethic or humanist philosophy focusing on people's allegiances and relations with each other." In this spirit, next to the "Download Ubuntu" link on Ubuntu's homepage, there should be a warning for all newbies as to what may be in store for them. Perhaps a link to this thread. 

If after reading this, anyone feels like spreading a little Ubuntu, please have a look at my threads, at least the first one...

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1350959](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1350959)

Here are the rest:

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=8475875#post8475875](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=8475875#post8475875)

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=8471513#post8471513](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=8471513#post8471513)

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1349165&page=10](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1349165&page=10)

**Happy Holidays to all & may you all be at peace!**

**Happy Holidays to all & may you all be at peace!**

I do feel sorry that you had issues with Ubuntu, but after reading through your threads, I'm not surprised.

You need to understand, Ubuntu is Linux. It is not Windows. Period. When you get the CD from the HP manufacturer, chances are it won't have Linux drivers. But 9/10 times, that's okay, because drivers are built into the Linux kernel that runs beneath Ubuntu, aka the core of the brain.

I think you need to use Ubuntu with an open mind. Just relax, do some reading about it, and tinker around. Your issues you had with Ubuntu indicate to me you were trying the Windows way of installing things on Ubuntu, which is not possible.

Believe me, Ubuntu is not that hard to use. But you can't use Linux and have Windows-like ideas on how to do things, and in the exact same regard you can't use Mac OSX with Windows-like notions either.

They're very different platforms and have different ways of doing things.

---

### Post by RiceMonster on 2009-12-12
> **Frak said:**
> With Linux, you are **forced** to release your source code, **and the driver will need to be updated every generation.**

that wouldn't even be a concern if you submit your driver to be included in the mainline kernel, which would be silly not to do if you've opened your code. Of course if you don't want to open the code, you're going to facing said problem, and a lot of companies have good reasons for not opening their code.

Also, there will never be a stable API for the Linux kernel. It's a conscious decision not to have one, which does have obvious downsides, but big upsides if everyone opens there code and gets there driver included in the kernel, which obviously will never happen. See Documentation/stable_api_nonsense.txt in the kernel source for more information.

---

### Post by Frak on 2009-12-12
> **RiceMonster said:**
> that wouldn't even be a concern if you submit your driver to be included in the mainline kernel, which would be silly not to do if you've opened your code. Of course if you don't want to open the code, you're going to facing said problem, and a lot of companies have good reasons for not opening their code.

Also, there will never be a stable API for the Linux kernel. It's a conscious decision not to have one, which does have obvious downsides, but big upsides if everyone opens there code and gets there driver included in the kernel, which obviously will never happen. See Documentation/stable_api_nonsense.txt in the kernel source for more information.
I'm just talking about some drivers that have been included into the mainline kernel. They're updated, but slowly, and many times they lose some featureset in order to stay compatible. Say a featureset relied on a large amount of handles that were deprecated, that feature may be placed on hold for a new release in order to get the base system out the door.

I would much rather the company make the decision about how the driver works. More often than not, a company would rather make the decision of working on it before time to include as many featuresets as possible, which would work because of a stable ABI, but instead, it is handled by the community which would much rather have a bare-bones driver out the door.

If anything, this is all theory. Theoretically, it would work. In reality, that's a loooooot of code to sift through. Code between them isn't necessarily "consistent" which can lead to issues in the future. There's a lot of cooks working on a bunch of pies and each one isn't necessarily theirs. It's a theoretical matter that shouldn't be taken with more than a grain of salt to its actual outcome.

---

### Post by Uadebisi on 2009-12-12
> I do feel sorry that you had issues with Ubuntu, but after reading through your threads, I'm not surprised.

You need to understand, Ubuntu is Linux. It is not Windows. Period. When you get the CD from the HP manufacturer, chances are it won't have Linux drivers. But 9/10 times, that's okay, because drivers are built into the Linux kernel that runs beneath Ubuntu, aka the core of the brain.

I think you need to use Ubuntu with an open mind. Just relax, do some reading about it, and tinker around. Your issues you had with Ubuntu indicate to me you were trying the Windows way of installing things on Ubuntu, which is not possible.

Believe me, Ubuntu is not that hard to use. But you can't use Linux and have Windows-like ideas on how to do things, and in the exact same regard you can't use Mac OSX with Windows-like notions either.

They're very different platforms and have different ways of doing things.Roasted,

I appreciate your reply & you having a look at my threads. And, of course, since all I've ever used was Windows, I am sure I am programmed to think Windows. However, I cannot see how most,  if not all of my problems, have anything to do with whether or not I've ever used Windows, except that I am used to having an OS that works w/o all the sort of issues that I've experienced with Ubuntu.

I probably have about 50 hours of actual time invested just trying to get this thing to work without issue, so I think my mind is fairly open. Yet I am still having unacceptable problems so unless I can get some more help, it's back to Windows for me:( And I actually love working on Ubuntu, I just need it to function w/o so many issues.

### Post by yester64 on 2009-12-12
> **PariahVayne said:**
> There is some truth to that but they all have one thing in common;

_**WINDOWS**_ 7 Home Basic

_**WINDOWS**_ 7 Home Premium

_**WINDOWS**_ 7 Ultimate

Windows is far more unified than Linux is almost every aspect. It's strange how many Linux users slate Windows but refuse to acknowledge any of the lingering problems with their chosen O/S.

Ubuntu is great in a lot ways, but it has some very painful flaws. Xserver/PulseAudio/upgrading for example, and it's not quite the 'minority' that have these issues as some people like to pretend. Personally I think ubuntu missed its chance of not capitalizing more when Mircosoft was wounded with Vista. Now it looks like ChromeOS will push it even further back into the shadows.

Have to disagree with the statement for the following reasons.
1) Unix/Linux is around for 30+ years. In this time it did not disappear and evolved over time, seen improvements and gained shares.
2) other OS who were propetary lost shares and went down the tubes. Remember Os/2 or GEM. Almost no one uses them today and there is little to no development going on.
3) Windows is indeed leading the pack. Mostly because the business world uses Outlook and Office. And then of course the games. All games are released to 100% on the windows platform (not counting consoles). This is the reason why Windows is leading and not because its well made.

Linux will be always a system for the person who has either no money to spend, or is a believer of the copyleft or GNU movement. Or in other terms, who believes in community driven efforts.

The question is, must Ubuntu (or Linux) compete with Windows? We are seeing right now changes in the way we use an operating system and the integration of the internet in our lifes. I would not be surprised to see proprietary software to change. Linux offers a more open and free approach and also a more powersaving option.

Also, i remember that there were times where Apple was on the brink of oblivion.
The OS was outdated and prone to freeze (bomb). Only with the imac and (most importantly) ipod Apple is on top.
What i am trying to say is, if a company makes bad decision you be out of luck since no one can change the system other than the company who makes it.

Yes, Linux is imperfect but no other OS is either. What Linux lags is a good business model like Steam or iTunes. If software could be channeled it could be much better. We will see improvements there too.

### Post by ecw on 2009-12-12
It seems to me that the vast majority of problems with Ubuntu (and other distros, but I don't have personal experience of them) is actually that the PCs they run on are made of of an almost infinite combination of different elements and the user must therefore tweak the distro to fit their machine.  PC manufacturers are legally forced to ship their PCs with Windows, and therefore invest considerable time and effort in making sure it works on their particular combination of e.g. graphics and sound cards to preserve their reputations.  The fine detail of the 'Windows' on my laptop is quite different from the 'Windows' on yours.  There is no works-out-of-the-box version of Windows you could install on a home built PC and expect to work first time.  You'd have all the same problems of getting the right drivers, etc.  Ever listened to a gamer gripe about how their new high-powered graphics card has caused problems?  That's you and I, with Linux, on our store-bought laptops.  If you have a store-bought PC with Windows, someone has done all that work for you.  That doesn't make Windows a better operating system, just better sold - and who wants to put money into the pockets of salesmen?

### Post by mivo on 2009-12-13
> **Ylon said:**
> Windows hadn't fame. Did not need that. Their OS is, plain, **forced** in every PC. 

No, it is not. It is a deliberate **choice** that manufacturers and vendors make (and benefit from). The alternatives are seen as inferior or undesirable (incompatibilities, support nightmare, market acceptance). It seems to be you who is in favour of forcing vendors or manufacturers to do something they obviously have no interest in. Welcome to the free market.

Plus, Frak wasn't talking about "now", but about the time when Windows was new. It was not "standard" back then and it did not usually come with new computers. Most programs at the time were for DOS. It was not a great choice for gamers either (see some similarities to the Linux situation here?), because gaming in the DOS era frequently required tweaking (ah, the fun I had with QEMM!) and squeezing every bit of memory and performance out of the machine.

So, Windows faced a market that wasn't all that open to innovation or change. There weren't "Live CDs", USB sticks, Youtube videos or otherwise free samples. You had to go to stores and look at their demo computers. Internet didn't exist in today's from, and the few people that used online forums were on AOL, CompuServe ($16,80 per hour for a 9600bps connection at the time) and local BBS networks like FidoNet. Word of mouth propagated much more slowly.

In short: In many ways, Linux/Ubuntu has it **much easier** to get attention and spread quickly than Windows did when it was a new system. I believe this is what Frak meant.

You also continue to blame Windows for the problems that Linux has. Do you really, seriously think that if Linux were the better (for "most people") choice, offered the same hardware support and was as seamless as Windows, it would not very quickly overthrow Windows and OSX? In truth, even when Windows sucked (the three years of the Vista era), Linux did not manage to improve the desktop market share to a relevant degree (and arguably increased not at all). This alone should really drive home the message that the **success or failure** of Linux on the desktop **is not tied to Windows**. 

Way too much energy is spent on "blaming Windows" or "bashing Microsoft" or "hating on Apple". Use it for something productive and contribute code, learn about driver development, write How-To documents, file bug reports at Launchpad, etc.

### Post by yester64 on 2009-12-13
I have the impression that we are talking out of context if we are talking about the DOS days.
Remember, the time (at least for me) began with DOS3.3 and it was painful.
Most people started off with a C64 (which was dominant over DOS) and all the games and program got written for the C64.
DOS took over with Visicalc, thats when you see the masses move to the PC and thats when MS gained the marketshares.
I remember the time as i squeezed every bit of memory (sorry you need at least 256mb more to run the game). I switched over to DR-DOS which was way better then MSdos but it MS made it incompatible and so you were with some games out of luck.

To some extent i don't get the discussion. Are we talking about how much better Windows is or how worst Linux is?
If you think Windows is superior you must run Windows. Linux has a totally different approach to the business and if i look back (before 2000) we had only X Windows. There was no Gnome or KDE.
So things actually improved a lot.
Linux is so easy to use as Windows. People complaining sometimes like that Windows is so easy to use and never has any problems.
The time i switched to Vista my printer drivers did not give me anymore manual duplex printing (Brother). How nice that you get rewarded if you made the switch and then your hardware does not work as same as it did before.
Remember that Windows has its Registry? This registry leads to some slowdown over time. You install and uninstall programs and it gets cluttered. Plus it can make your system quite unstable.
I loved Windows for the games, but that was about it.
Mac is nice too, but to be honest a Mac is even more close and restrictive then a 0815 PC. Upgrade a Mac with a new gfx card or try to upgrade the cpu.
But i do agree that MacOSX is easier to use if you are old and have a hard time with computers.

The funny thing is, that it is kinda the same discussion we had in the Amiga days. Everyone was saying how much better MS is, but the Amiga OS was way better then Windows at that time and easier to use.
To bad that Commodore was badly managed. The managment believed too that PC was so much better.

Besides, one thing that gets forgotten here is, how MS came to place their OS in the first place.
MS cut a deal with IBM to license their OS to every system sold. Thats how DOS and Windows became the standart. Because everyone else after had to include their system too.
Now in contrast look at Linux. Their is no BillGates how cut deals to license the OS to every PC that gets sold.
So you can not really compare these 2 systems as they are different in how they get into the market place.

Feel free to criticize me, but this is how i see it.

---

### Post by mivo on 2009-12-13
> **yester64 said:**
> Most people started off with a C64 (which was dominant over DOS) and all the games and program got written for the C64.

Just games, at least for the most part. The C64 wasn't dominant in the office or business space. But there were plenty of systems at the time and numerous OSes. I had a VIC-20 at some point, but skipped the C64 for an Amstrad CPC 6128 (with CP/M as its OS). That one had so few games that I was "forced" to learn how to program ;), and I managed to get a copy of dBase which for some reason fascinated me, so I got fairly competent at it without having actual use for it. Next followed a TOS-based Atari ST, which was then replaced by my first "PC". To date I regret that I never had a Spectrum.

Never had a Spectrum so i can not relate to it. :(
My first PC was a Amstrad 1640 (with 640MB) but without a harddrive. Monochrome display was in at the time. :)
And i remember i bought the wrong writer software at the time which could only be installed on a harddrive and i had none. So i exchange it.
I tossed the computer and replaced it with an Amiga which had color.

### Post by myolbug on 2009-12-15
I know that this is a bit of an eternal thread, but I just want to add my two cents.

I am an Ubuntu Newbie.  Somewhat.  There is a boatload I don't know.  I got my feet wet with an old version of Linux, way back in 2000.  I don't remember which one.  I didn't get it and I wanted to...

However, I went on and used Windows for a while later until I was told about Ubuntu 6.XX.  I tried it and aside from having trouble installing something outside of Synaptic, I loved it.  I have been all Ubuntu now for about two years, using my Win XP only for downloading music via Rhapsody.

I have been in California for the past month and I have been using Windows again, and in my opinion, I really do prefer Ubuntu.  I can do so much more and the layout of Ubuntu is very logical and HELLA faster.  I can search for files etc faster and more intuitively.  The applications are SO much easier to find, as they are logically sorted by the category, such as games, office, internet, etc and not just by name wherein you have to search in some bloated popup window thing.

I haven't used Win7 but honestly, I have no need for it.  Ubuntu gives me everything I want and more.  Even KMyMoney is more logical and Intuitive than Quickbooks.  But, as I have ordered a notebook for my son, I will get a chance to try it out.
Now, combine all of this with the fact that it is FREE and SO many programs are free, I just don't understand someone's dependence on Windows.

All of my Windows friends that have seen my Ubuntu 9.10 with a bunch of goodies in Compiz turned on are in love with all of the little tricks it can do.

I have used a Macbook pro while down here and I like it, but to me it just feels like an altered Linux, but with a HUGE price tag.  SO, again, why should I use it? True, I may not be able to use Garage Band or Final Cut Pro. I would like to use Garage Band, but not enough to overpay for a MAC.

### Post by powerpleb on 2009-12-16
> **Zoot7 said:**
> Openoffice is a good replacement for MS Office and it's a fairly competent Word Processor *provided* you can stay away from the .doc form. But it's still not as easy get things done as it is in Office 2003 (Can't speak for 2007, can't use that interface), nor is it as powerful.

See, I think these things are largely subjective. I use Office 2003 at work and OO 3.1 at home, and have become relatively proficient at both of them. 
Both are excellent pieces of software, and while I accept that OO can occasionally be somewhat more confusing than MS Office (especially when the spell checker decides not to work), 99% of the time the fact that I am using one or the other is much of a muchness really. Beyond that, OO is a tad quicker than MS Office which IMHO balances out some of its short comings and lack of polish.

As for Gimp and Adobe products, I accept that Gimp isn't much competition for the more serious user. That isn't to say that it isn't good at what it does, and for tweaking images at home Gimp is fine and much better value. I am sure there are serious designers out there who swear by GIMP. However, for a lot of professionals Windows or a Mac becomes a necessity. But all that could change if Adobe ever decided to release software for Linux.

### Post by needhelppeeps on 2009-12-17
Ubuntu vs Windows 7: My take

i have an older laptop running ubuntu and a brand new one running windows 7, both were purchased as lower end models (around 500$). Both have 15" monitors, obviously the new one has more memory (2x) and horsepower.

Ubuntu: Most programs I needed were in the distrubution channels but I had to go outside of that to get a few things which may have lowered the security of my machine. The system works great for the most part and runs a heck of a lot better on this old machine than Vista ever did. What I would change: The sound was funky and hard to get it to stop coming though the speakers when headphones were in. Having to install video codecs from outside repos. Gyachi is ok but it would nice if yahoo published a linux messenger. In other words, my issues with ubuntu outside of the sound are probably beyond ubuntu's control.

Windows 7: Doesn't include hardly anything in a known safe repo so I have to trust a ton of third party software. I like that it prompts for passwords before allowing system changes like vista. The user interface is similar to ubuntu in terms of functionality. Sometime the UI is slightly cleaner in windows applications.. for example I get annoyed with having to enter twice when posting a facebook im. It's nothing to stay up late fretting over, though. Licensing is a pain. For example, with vista I have my code but the disk stopped working so now I cant reinstall it. My laptop didnt ship with a windows 7 disk so I'' prolly acronis the thing and make like 16 copies because windows is always such a pain about having software plus keys and all.

Overall, I prefer ubuntu. I dont have to pay to get newer versions of the system, I get repos where I know most of the software I use is safe, the UI is fairly similar to windows in terms of functionality and ease of use, and it runs better on older hardware than windows 7 does on newer hardware! Windows has the advantage of having more programs tailor to it, but with those programs may come spyware and other fun stuff. The headaches of having to worry about having lost a disk or product code or having to pay to get newer versions of the OS are also annoying. I can do almost every in ubuntu that I want to do (still hate the IM yahoo clients and have trouble with some protected movies) and often do it far better than in windows and do so using software I trust. For me, I will keep a windows machine around for the one or two things that dont work in ubuntu, but ubuntu is what I use 95% of the time. IMO if you're a total computing novice, use windows. If you can't stand to ever research a problem to find a solution, use windows. If you're reasonably computer literate, and don't need a ton of protected porn or windows games, use ubuntu.

---

### Post by rsvr85 on 2009-12-20
> **~sHyLoCk~ said:**
> Simple: Don't use Linux. 
Use Linux only if you are willing to learn, manually if needed editing configs or using the CLI.
Don't compare your windows experience with Linux. if windows work for you, stick with it. Ubuntu,suse, have been making efforts all these years to make Linux experience for novice users easier all these years, but still you guys complain. You don't even want to open a terminal. That's not the way Linux works. 

Regards

What a shame responses like this are made.  Unfortunately almost all new Ubuntu users will be comparing their experiences with Windows, as this is the platform they're (possibly/hopefully) migrating from.  Linux shouldn't only be used if the user is willing to learn, what a ridiculous thing to say.
I'm no novice to Windows which has helped a little in my conquest to nail Ubuntu, even i find it very un-friendly.
When a complete newbie is asking questions here on the forums like "how do i install/uninstall xxxxx program?" and the responses are something like;
"**_do_** a sudo apt-get...... sudo aptitude safe-upgrade....sudo apt-get remove" etc. etc.  All terminal commands, it's hardly a surprise people are hosing their systems &/or running for the hills confused.  In Windows, you rarely have to use the command prompt, there's a GUI for almost everything.  This seems to becoming true of Ubuntu too but when everyone gives terminal commands to perform simple GUI tasks for responses here, it's off putting.

I think it's important to remember, Linux/Ubuntu will not just be used by people who are willing to learn/understand the system and it's naive to think/expect this.  It's also important to remember where you experienced guys came from (remember your first experience?) and that a large percentage of new users will be coming from Windows no matter how much you grumble about it.

> **yester64 said:**
> Mm... to sad, that we can not have them natively. Very sad.
But i think there might be an app for that.
Seriously, i haven't tried any of them on linux but i can imagine that you can get them to run with either wine or crossover.

I'm not even going to attempt Photoshop CS4 Extended in Wine, i may as well save the hassle and boot into my Windows partition when i use it, the same goes for Office, in Windows i'm using the very nice Office 2010, again, i'm confident Wine will not do it justice (although i shall try!).

~RSVR85

### Post by ~sHyLoCk~ on 2009-12-20
> **rsvr85 said:**
> What a shame responses like this are made.  Unfortunately almost all new Ubuntu users will be comparing their experiences with Windows, as this is the platform they're (possibly/hopefully) migrating from.  Linux shouldn't only be used if the user is willing to learn, what a ridiculous thing to say.
I'm no novice to Windows which has helped a little in my conquest to nail Ubuntu, even i find it very un-friendly.

Linux should only be used if the user is willing to learn.
There fixed for ya.

> **rsvr85 said:**
> 
When a complete newbie is asking questions here on the forums like "how do i install/uninstall xxxxx program?" and the responses are something like;
"**_do_** a sudo apt-get...... sudo aptitude safe-upgrade....sudo apt-get remove" etc. etc.  All terminal commands, it's hardly a surprise people are hosing their systems &/or running for the hills confused.  In Windows, you rarely have to use the command prompt, there's a GUI for almost everything.  This seems to becoming true of Ubuntu too but when everyone gives terminal commands to perform simple GUI tasks for responses here, it's off putting.


Well that's a part of Linux. Ubuntu has tried to hold your hands all these years, suse and mandriva, every such newbie-friendly distros have been working hard to make "new user" experience better for you. However, the truth is things will break one day. Then what? There is no alternative to CLI and commands. There is a huge difference between using linux and  using windows. The best remedy of windows that most tech Support guys ask you to do is "Have you tried turning it off and on again? Try re-installing your system."..etc. We don't do that. When you use Linux you are expected to make efforts to understand and learn about your system. You can't take things for granted. You must know what breaks your system and how you can fix it. You must look under the hood and try to find out how things actually work. It's like driving a car and not knowing anything about how it functions. What will you do in case of an emergency? Just wait for a lift in the middle of the street when you can just easily open the hood and fix it yourself? Linux is meant to be used, learnt, modified and distributed.

> **rsvr85 said:**
>  It's also important to remember where you experienced guys came from (remember your first experience?) and that a large percentage of new users will be coming from Windows no matter how much you grumble about it.


I do remember. It is for those people who rtfm'd me, it is for those people who asked me to "google it" , I know what I know today. I know about linux today by spending time going through the man pages and using the internet. I didn't get a special training or degree from any special university. Most people learn Linux this way. If people keep on spoon feeding you , you will never get the urge to do it yourself, to learn something you must understand and do the necessary hard work. I can only show you the path, you will have to walk it. ;)

Regards

### Post by rsvr85 on 2009-12-20
> **~sHyLoCk~ said:**

There fixed for ya.
Rubbish, what about people who have no choice but to go for a free OS?  Some people don't have the time/inclination to learn a new OS and battle through the attitude of the Ubuntu community.

> The best remedy of windows that most tech Support guys ask you to do is "Have you tried turning it off and on again? Try re-installing your system."
I assume you haven't sought much support for Windows then?  Take a look [here](http://www.sevenforums.com), you should be pleasantly surprised to find Windows users actually making intelligent suggestions and yielding good results. 

> When you use Linux you are expected to make efforts to understand and learn about your system.
Who makes these expectations?  It's silly to say you're expected to make efforts to learn your system before using it.  Especially when Ubuntu is touted as the easiest to use system "*in the world!*"/"it just works"

> You can't take things for granted. You must know what breaks your system and how you can fix it. You must look under the hood and try to find out how things actually work.

See, i went the other way. I deleted my windows for good and to be honest i don't look back.
Sure i might have stones in my way since not every windows software will work, but i don't have to.
If you need to run certain apps from windows, you might keep windows just in case.
But for the most part, i can run windows apps just fine. I settled for crossover which makes it easier for me to run things.
The one thing that has to be considered is, that linux works differently from windows in many ways.
The terminal is in no way the same as the command prompt in windows. So even you or any other new user are scared or wonder why you have to deal with the terminal, it opens you a much broader access to the operating system.
You can indeed run the system with just the command prompt, which you can not in windows.
My very first use of linux was confusing as well as to see the command prompt and than not knowing what to do. It can be very overwhelming to see it and not know the commands. But once i learned the basics, i don't want to miss it anymore.
But again, i might be more of a nerd in that respect.
I am sure linux will be more easier with every release and perhaps does not require in the future to open the terminal anymore.
I never owned photoshop so i can not related to that problem. But i would write certainly a letter to adobe and complain that i want to run it under linux. If they consider you as a valued customer, they should give you help to run it under linux.
The one thing i recommend anyone who is new to linux is, to perhaps get some books about linux. Thats what i did. I did the same with Windows because even Windows is not that easy if you never used it. The assumtion that Windows is very easy, does not account to the parents that never used a computer and don't know about all the features Windows has or how to solve a problem once one arises.
The bottom line is, i assume, that you have to get involved with any system you install.
btw. type in the command the following 'apt-get moo'. :)

### Post by endeavormac on 2009-12-20
> **arrow.69 said:**
>  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

This is how I used to be, now I am the complete opposite, and it stems from me doing different things with computers.

I used to use Windows predominantly, and would switch over to a linux distro, only to move back to Windows about a month later. This would happen maybe twice a year.

Now I do this. I run linux, some new game comes out, I jump over to Windows, play the game, realize Windows sucks as a quick development environment for C, go back to linux, don't look back.

It really depends on how you use your computer. For most people, linux is definitely not the correct answer. However, some of us really enjoy apt-get installing libraries and then using pkg-config to link them when building. You can get everything to work in Windows, but it's a pain. 

Long story short, Ubuntu gives me the freedom to develop quickly, hassle-free, the way I want, using lots of libraries. When it comes to coding in C and python, the two languages I spend most of my time in, *Windows cannot compete*. I really wish it could, it would make my life so much easier.

### Post by Manyette on 2009-12-22
Having used WinDoze since the beginning, I think I'm pretty familiar with it.  I have also used (and programmed for) OS/2, and all the earlier command line systems.  I tried Ubuntu back at version 8.04, and since I was on a laptop, decided that it "just wasn't there" as yet.  I've tried all the versions since that time a couple of years ago.  When 9.04 arrived, I told myself that "they have finally gotten there"!  And I have had no reason to change my mind.  A couple of points others haven't made:

It's free!

I no longer have to update my antivirus software as part of my startup routine.

The updates come automatically, daily, and not once a month unless the are "critical".

After 10 or so years of XP, they are still filling "security" holes.  Sad commend on security IMHO.

My new laptop came with Win 7, and needless to say, it lasted just long enough to try it for a day, and then install 9.10. Anyway, just one mans opinion, who is very happy, and waiting for the 10.04 LTS anxsiously!

### Post by iponeverything on 2009-12-22

What your missing is that it is not a competition. 

"Recurring Discussions" is great place for this. I have been hearing this same stuff since 1994. Every new windows release brings a new crop of "linux is doomed" posts. Windows 7 has the same privacy, spyware and virus problems as its predecessors --  "huge leap" yea.. a huge leap in profits for scamers and anti-virus companies. Coincidence? I don't think so. As long as Linux remains useful, it will remain reverent. As long as corporations treat their customers like milking cows, there will always be Free software.

### Post by emeraldgirl08 on 2009-12-22
I'm one of those people that had absolutely zero experience with Linux. 

Didn't even know what it was and only was concerned about how we had XP Pro and we couldn't get any aero on it. Well...not that concerned :P

Anyway. Everyone has their own reasons for using an OS. Some have to use Windows b/c of work or school. I migrated over to Linux b/c I wanted something new and was a just a smidgen daunted by the terminal interface. I think the use of Linux also has a lot to do with how much patience someone has and the willingness to learn. Some people get turned off by the very first thing that goes wrong and start bad-mouthing Linux as if it's supposed to work OOB on the very first install and do their dishes! 

And act like they had to pay for the OS!

I love challenges and for a NOOB to learn linux just goes to show it's not that hard. I'm hardly an expert now but have become pretty comfortable and use Linux 95% of the time. These days I use Windoze to update my anti-virus and play one game. I don't hate or say anything bad about Windoze but have done one too many reinstalls on relatives computers b/c of malware. That's one aspect of the computing world I don't miss here!

I just don't get the fanboy/girl flaming sessions :confused: Why would you want to complain about either OS if you have a scenario like mine. They both serve their purpose and when people see you dual-booting they know you are definitely not a NOOB. That makes me feel SO smart lol. 

Synopsis. Use OS' to your sitch. Have a good day :)

On average, BSD users seem a lot less prone to zealotry and seem, also on average, more mature.

### Post by DarkLilith on 2009-12-22
I honestly think Ubuntu has a very good chance of competing with OS X and Windows. For one thing, it's free, which gives it a lot of bonuses, because I don't know about everyone else, but I don't mind having to put in a little extra work and some research to get certain programs/apps to work on Ubuntu if I don't have to pay a ridiculous amount for it. :D  Another thing is that even though I know I will have to eventually figure out more about the command prompt, I would rather do that than struggle for hours trying to get my Vista to run simple programs (I had a lot of issues with my Vista, but honestly had no issues with XP).

I just like the way Ubuntu is laid out. It's nice looking, it runs exceedingly smoothly, even though I still have a little less than half my HD still devoted to Vista (in case of compatibility issues, a little nervous to jump completely into Ubuntu until I know a bit more!). I also like the idea that I can get into the guts of my system without having to argue with my operating system. 

As it stands right now, I partitioned my HD to make Ubuntu my main OS about a month ago and I have only switched over to Windows once. I plan on eventually fully switching to Ubuntu once I'm more comfortable with the command prompt. So for me, there's no competing. :)

---

### Post by madnessjack on 2009-12-22
Mac works because they use their own hardware
Windows works because Microsoft works with hardware manufacturers
Linux works because people works their balls of getting it to work with Windows hardware

If Ubuntu wants to go mainstream, they need backing of hardware manufacturers, even if they go down the Mac route and sell "Ubuntu machines".

Windows is great, Mac is sexy and Ubuntu is cool because it goes against the grain (eg monopoly, business etc - it's like an OS for the people by the peopel)

### Post by mivo on 2009-12-22
> **Ylon said:**
> Performs? Performs what? Windows XP(SP3) is more fast and stable than Windows 7.. and Ubuntu is more fast and stable than Windows XP(SP3). Or you was talking about.... Vista?

I skipped most of your post, because we covered this repeatedly already, so I'll just answer the question:

Windows 7 performs better on my main desktop than Ubuntu 9.10 does. Faster, smoother, problem-free. I did not use XP on this computer. XP does perform better on my netbook than any Linux, since the latter does not support hibernate/suspend and uses up the battery faster. On my older laptop, Ubuntu 8.04 and 9.04 perform better than XP. No difference between XP and Ubuntu on my secondary desktop (both were *not* easy to install on it, but it did run FreeBSD out of the box).

### Post by JimInLakeland on 2009-12-22
> **Aflack said:**
> What holds linux back for me is the extreme feeling of unpolished-ness... if that's a word.

And the little bugs that are different on every single installation. Ill install on my comp and have some weird bugs.
Install on a friends and have some random weird different bugs.
And same goes forever...

You just described Windows installations as well. 

As far as the polish, I believe Ubuntu 9.10 with the extremely smooth fonts, clean icons and 3D desktop environments easily beat Windows and is a close second to MacOS X environments.

I find the stability to be superior to both Windows and MacOS X (although I have far more experience with Windows). Just like Windows, if you have the most optimized hardware for Ubuntu, the installation will go smoothly (i.e. nVidia GPUs instead of ATI).

Can it compete, though? My question is this: Does it have to compete? Like Steve Jobs said about Mac: for Apple to win, Microsoft does not have to lose. For Ubuntu to win, Apple and Windows do not have to lose.

Ubuntu reminds of why I became interested in computer 30 years ago. And the Ubuntu community is second to none.

### Post by MuppetandChums on 2009-12-23
> **arrow.69 said:**
> I'm gonna get incredibly bashed for posting this, but here it goes.

I've been running various Linux distributions on a bunch of computers since I was 13.  Windows would piss me off, Vista got released, or sometimes I just felt like playing around.  I've been through MEPIS, OpenSuse, Kubuntu, Ubuntu,amongst others.  It eventually became clear to me that Ubuntu was the overall best desktop distribution out there, if only for the tremendous amount of support there is out there for it.

I love Ubuntu.  It's a very capable OS and I love the idea of running a completely free and open operating system.  I've probably run some variation of Ubuntu on and off for the past four years.  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

However, I have to admit that Ubuntu simply can't compete anymore.  Windows 7 is a huge leap for Windows, and OS X has always been user-friendly and functional.  I know that under the hood, Linux is more stable, quicker, more secure than Windows.  But the truth is, to the end-user on the desktop front, the experience you get running Ubuntu doesn't measure up.  The problem isn't a lack of applications of features.  Ubuntu just lacks polish and integration.  Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users.  I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability.  Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer.  They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system.  They want it to just work for them.  Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now.

What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

By and large I agree.

But you are comparing software clearly targetted at different communities. I would not have the vaguest idea how to, for example, to rip a DVD and re-encode as a good quality avi/mkv in Ubuntu but I have mastered the ' skills ' in Windows.

Paretos Law applies 90%+ of users demands/wishes will be met by a vanilla flavoured OS with a small % of current apps. Ubuntu will not encroach commercially on Windows territory unless

1. MS release another Vista AND user cannot afford MAC OSX or
2. Unbuntu hides all the linux switches/bells/whistles (that make it attractive to its current user base)  and makes it IDIOT proof for the woman/man in the street...vanilla flavoured shell for the Average PC user.

Vista forced me onto MAC OSX because it was so unstable (awful). I do not want to be held hostage again by the arrogance & indifference of MS, hence my experimentation with Ubuntu. I am IT literate....but I still cannot get Skype to work ( or better understand why it doesnt ) nor can I really understand the difference between boot managers & boot loaders & their interaction with GRUB2....nor can I understand wihy 9.1 will NOT instal on my old T42 Thinkpad. These sort of issues will marginalise Ubuntu & users and keep it from becoming mildly mainstream.

Muppet & Chums

### Post by graphius on 2009-12-23

no, I disagree, if you buy a computer where the factory has preinstalled Windows and dealt with all the driver issues you will have no problems.

Windows would not boot from the (legitimate) cd. Just for kicks I tried running Ubuntu from the live cd. No problem. After talking to the customers to find out their needs we installed Ubuntu. So moral of the story, I am not sure if windows can compete with linux (especially in the long term)

### Post by mivo on 2009-12-23
> **graphius said:**
> I recently had to fix a laptop where the dvd drive did not work. There were other problems as well so a full reinstall of Vista seemed like the best idea. Windows would not boot from the (legitimate) cd. Just for kicks I tried running Ubuntu from the live cd. No problem.

You are comparing a 3+ years old original installation CD of Vista to a two months old installation CD of Ubuntu. For a proper comparison, either use a 6.x version of Ubuntu or Windows 7.

I agree, though, that installing an older version of Windows isn't always free of trouble. Installing XP from my 2001 retail DVD on my secondary desktop left me without a working ethernet card (the model had not existed in 2001). The fix was easy, though: downloaded the driver on another computer from the manufacturer's web site, put it on an USB stick and installed it. Took five minutes. Downloaded SP3 next and had a fully updated XP version. Try using the upgrade feature to update even a 24-months old version of Ubuntu, or getting a non-working network card to work in the same way I got the ethernet card to run. Or for even more fun, try it with a Linux distro released in 2001. :)

### Post by erixoltan on 2009-12-23
If you have a problem with your Ubuntu system, there is a great community to help you and the solution is usually free.  But the average computer user isn't up to the challenge since their brain may lock up if they have to type anything starting with "sudo".

I am administering numerous Ubuntu systems for friends and family who have older hardware and had to reformat their computers after virus problems, popups etc.  They are pretty happy with it, but then again they can just bring it to me if there is any sort of problem. I think the point is to coexist more than to compete.

### Post by Danimoth on 2009-12-23
Strongly disagree with OP. I think Mac cannot compete with win7 or ubuntu(or other flavor). Given the current situation of OS, the critical thing is compatibility. "All" programs are created for windows and it gives them a great advantage. Therefore:


Windows: You want compatibility for a professional program you use and only runs in windows? You use windows, end of story. Yes you could use wine, but lets not start on that, because when it comes to work, you want something 100% functional. So, you pay some money for compatibility.


Linux: Free and does 95% of things you need to do. The last 5% is for those professional programs already mentioned. Compatibility problems may apply for you if you are in that category. Off the top of my head, autocad & photoshop will require you to use windows. And please don't tell me you are a photoshop user just because you crop images and fix red-eyes...


Mac: You pay(a lot actually) for this and also get compatibility problems. Also, no advantage whatsoever in comparison windows or linux. Oh, and if microsoft is accused of vendor lock-in, then apple is 1000 times worse. 



Alright, with mac out of the way, we can talk seriously. I think ubuntu is able to compete with windows very well, and i believe, in most things, it actually beats it. The only problem right now is(surprise surprise) the programs that are windows-only. If you are stuck with windows due to these, then you have to cope with it. 

The progress of wine plus various commercial software companies already noticing what linux is able to do, already offer Unix versions of their software. This helps more people adapt, and more people means more companies will be interested to also create Unix versions. I think we are currently at a turning point regarding operating systems and a expect the battle between windows and linux to be very very interesting...


edit: Oh sorry, i also forgot games. Yeah, if you want games the safe bet it windows.

Yet on the flip side, I can fire up Kubuntu. Things just make sense. It's solid. Reliable. Stable. Easy to use. And yet, wait... wtf? It's free? 

For the record, I got my Mac for free. Otherwise, I wouldn't own a Mac. :lolflag:

### Post by TWO on 2009-12-24
<rant>

You cannot claim to be an easy to use operating system if you cannot even get Input Method Editors right. As someone who needs to type in different scripts and is currently unable to run Windows/OS X due to faulty computer/money constraints, having to battle with installing SKIM/iBus/UIM alone and then the problems that each has bought me just isn't worth it. 

I appreciate the efforts of countless volunteers around the world, but if the likes of Canonical are pumping money into this OS, then at least get the basics right.

I just don't understand. The wealth of programs available through the repositories is amazing, but when you have some which haven't been maintained for years, some which are half-baked and the rest which are just plain buggy, then you do begin to wonder.

Sticking with installing things, how about making .deb the standard way to install programs along with the repositories. Why would we/should we have to adopt the compile a binary method? It may only be me, but I have never come across a more cumbersome installation method, and don't even get me started on using ./configure...

That and I can't even play music CDs at the moment.

Honestly, the pretty window switching effects on the latest Kubuntu count for nothing right now. Glad to see some improvements, but I grow tired of IME failing with every upgrade...or (K)Ubuntu opting for less featured programs as the new standard e.g (K)Packagekit/iBus...

</rant>

Gosh, i read this kind of message a lot.
You need to understand some things first.
Linux distributes programs in different ways. For one you can install the most popular programs over the software center. So no compiling there, unless you select the tab for seeing whats going on under the hood.
Many other programs come in just source form and therefore require you to compile them yourself.
Linux does not have executables like Windows does. So you will never see an .exe file which you can click on.
Windows and Linux (or Unix for that matter) work very differently.
You can always check the sourcecode if you wish to know whats in the program and you may even alter the code.

The other thing is that in the linux world, there are many many projects going on. Most of them are privately done, meaning there is no company working on the program. So there are people like you and me working on a program in their sparetime.
Some projects just die, for several reasons like no time or any other reason.
However you can takeover the code and continue to code the program further.
And yes, there might be some programs there are buggy. Altough i rarely had a buggy program and most of them work fine.

Besides, Windows is a permanent workplace too. Windows itself is getting fixes on a daily base and some 3.party programs get no fix at all and you might paid for that.

And whats the problem with your Input Method? I don't get that. It is something i never used i have to guess.

Anyway, since its a holiday, i wish you and everyone here a nice holiday. Cheer up :)

### Post by amandaG on 2009-12-25
I think it's great that you've been trying Linux and still try Windows. There's nothing wrong with that and I sure hope you won't be "bashed" for expressing your opinion, even on an Ubuntu forum. I appreciate your opinion and your efforts.

To answer your question as to why someone would prefer Ubuntu, or maybe you would like to know why a Windows user would switch and then prefer any Linux distro, I would like to leave my reply.

I personally don't advocate anything, regardless of my opinion, because it's just that, my opinion. Everyone is entitled to one. :)

I like Linux, and I do like using Ubuntu most of the time, because it teaches me. I don't mind the tweaking or searching for ways to get certain hardware to work, because I learn about computers in the process. When I used Windows, this was not possible for me unless I paid for subscriptions or support. 

The freely available documentation for all of the different versions of Windows sent me into major confusion. I didn't like that I "had" to run a program(s) to prevent attack on my operating system. I also didn't like that I had to worry about defragmentation of my hard drive.

I wanted an operating system that I could use, (not that I needed one that didn't cost money), without the worry over security or having to pay additional costs for support from a large company that I wasn't all too sure that I trusted with my personal information.

Finding Linux was like finding a large expanse of land that no one owned and that I was allowed to camp on, enjoy, and leave things like wood already chopped for the next camper. May sound funny, but kinda fills me up with that same feeling... even though it's a computer.

Anyway, I do agree that each person would do well to check out the available options, whether Mac, Windows, Linux'es, or UNIX'es. At least you aren't ignoring the alternatives to one OS or the other. At least you have the logic and intellect to try other possibilities and then choose what's right for you. I can't and wouldn't blame someone for using something that one understands better or that saves one time. People have different priorities and who am I to judge that. I wouldn't. I'm just gonna say that for me I do prefer Linux and some Unix'es to Windows because I get to learn a lot more about what goes on in the operating system and I have more control. If on the other hand I weren't interested in computers, I would definitely pick the one that gave me the most functionality without requiring in-depth knowledge or extra time in aquiring computer knowledge.

You can't really compare people's interests or aptitudes. Each person has his/her strength and/or interest. That's how we can all work together to provide something that someone else can't or isn't compelled to accomplish. I definitely appreciate scientists who study and work on projects that make everyone's lives better barring the need for everyone to develop his/her own approach. It's in this way we contribute to each other's well being and convenience. I sure as heck wouldn't condemn a farmer who grows the food I eat just because I'm not interested in growing my own food, etc... So why would anyone condemn someone for choosing what works best for them, as long as it doesn't hurt anyone else? I dunno. So rock on with Windows, Mac, or whatever you like best! And thanks for the thought provoking post!

Happy Holidays!
Amanda

### Post by margazhang on 2009-12-27
Wow, this thread is too long to go through all the postings! I have to say something that is critical - forgive me if someone has already said it above.
 
The one and only thing that makes me switch to Ubuntu is this...

Windows gets slower and slower when you use it. The more you use it, the slower it gets! To get Windows to run faster, you have to do the DEFRAGMENTATION process once every month :-(

I find Ubuntu (and many other distros of linux/unix) solves that problem once for all.

So there is a FUNDAMENTAL difference between Ubuntu and Windows. Read the word FUNDAMENTAL. The difference has its root in the file structure - correct me if I am wrong.

### Post by new2ubuntu on 2009-12-27
I like linux... a lot. But anyone who says Windows 7 is not the best offering Microsoft has ever had is huffing glue. It is faster, more stable, and people like the eye candy. It still has viruses, fragmentation, and security problems for joe user.

Linux is secure and rock solid, however, the programs for it often are not. There are a few things so awkward and/or unreliable to do in Linux that I reboot into windows for these tasks.

In the end, it depends on what you use your computer for. I have setup systems for people who only use their computer to surf the web. For those people, I install and configure linux for them once and never hear from them again.

If you are running a server... there is nothing close. NOTHING.

But many things are frustratingly difficult to do in Linux so if one of those things is something you do regularly... you will probably head back to windows.

### Post by MisfitI38 on 2009-12-28
Windows XP SP3 is a great OS. 
Windows 7 is starting out very strong in a lot of areas. Even though I use GNU/Linux as my main OS, I must acknowledge 7's speed (same performance as XP on dated hardware), convenience of installation (a few clicks), nice looks, ease of personalization, et cetera. 
Choosing an OS usually means embracing its overall methodology and sometimes, philosophy. Those that choose GNU/Linux and waste energy on hating Microsoft and Windows, are also the ones that will be bitten by version release regression and other breakages. Likewise, those that use or choose to use Windows will be susceptible to its ubiquitous malware and 'blackbox' nature.
There are some real strengths to both platforms, and choosing one or both is fine as long as the applications needed are available.
Since 2004, when I started dabbling with GNU/Linux, I have maintained the view that MS Windows will continue to dominate the desktop market. Nothing that has transpired since then has changed my opinion of this.
Much of the generation of Americans growing up now will never know what life was like before cell phones and iPods, but very, very few of these young people want (or let alone need) to use GNU/Linux. 
Very few people really want to choose or actually *use* an operating system. They want to use applications and software, and for the foreseeable future, Windows covers this simple need well enough. 
The biggest obstacle to new innovation is an existing product that is just good enough. Windows (and Mac OSX for that matter) are more than good enough for the overwhelming majority.
