</rant>

I *partially *retract my earlier rant.

I recommend **Linux Mint **to everyone who isn't too concerned with the whole "free" software mentality. Whilst it is essentially Ubuntu, it is such a breath of fresh air not to have to spend hours enabling mp3 playback and the like. 

And some of the Mint tools including the Mint version of the GNOME menu are ingenious! It really has rekindled my love of Linux OSes!

My earlier input method editor worry seems to have been eradicated with Mint and admittedly, **iBus** IME did work under **[COLOR=Orange]Ubuntu[/COLOR]** for me as opposed to [COLOR=Cyan]**Kubuntu**[COLOR=Black], which is a shame, because I have been a huge fan of Ubuntu + KDE. [/COLOR][/COLOR]

> **yester64 said:**
> Gosh, i read this kind of message a lot.
You need to understand some things first.
Linux distributes programs in different ways. For one you can install the most popular programs over the software center. So no compiling there, unless you select the tab for seeing whats going on under the hood.
Many other programs come in just source form and therefore require you to compile them yourself...


But I must make it clear to an earlier replier to my last post: I understand that Linux is not Windows or OS X. It is an optional alternative for desktop computing, not a commercial competitor to the big two. We Linux users are in the most part not your average end-user; we scrutinize the OS mercilessly and end up starting threads like this one. But unlike the average user, we have the capacity to modify our systems to our own tastes...it's just that sometimes, as is in our human nature, we'd like for certain functions to be able to be executed as effortlessly as possible. Hence, my never-ending gripe against tarballs!! Yes, OP, there are not .exe files on Linux- I'm not a Linux novice, I understand the ins and outs- but there are indeed **.deb **files, and they're the next best thing to .exe...

### Post by darkeye123 on 2009-12-28
As for myself, I have chosen Linux as my permanent OS.
Of course, this is just a personal opinion.

The main reasons I chose it is because it is so easy to integrate with my current hardware, and because of the depth of customization. I know you can customize a lot of features in Windows, but its not really the same as adjusting every little setting in your system.

In reply to the thread itself, I understand your concerns, but I think that Linux is competing quite nicely. Most supercomputers run Linux ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Supercomputer](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Supercomputer)), and it is beginning to appeal to quite a large audience. Its security and stability has definitely helped me decide on what OS to run on my servers, and its ease of use, even from a windows point of view, was unbelievable. 

I think that the reason Linux is so great is not because of the market share it has, but for the support and options it has for its users. If I want a distro that I can heavily customize and get involved in command line, there's ones for that. If I want a distro that is easy to use, there's a distro for that. If I want a distro that looks great but still runs on older hardware, there's one for that. If I want a distro that runs great as a server, there's a distro for that. 

In fact, one of my friends came over and when he saw ubuntu, he wanted a copy of the disc. I don't like linux because its ultra-popular. I like linux because it is community driven.

### Post by sram.shadow on 2009-12-29
These are all very good points. 

Perhaps the biggest advantage Windows has over Linux is the immense amounts of commercial and hardware support. Dell makes great computers but they always seem to have a nice little sticker on them that says "Designed for Windows"...same goes for HP.. A lot of commercial software companies like Adobe publish specially for Windows. If Linux had the same amount of support/partnerships with hardware manufacturers and software developers it would probably be on the same level as Windows. 

But then you have to keep in mind, one of the biggest advantages that Linux OS' have over Windows is security. Theoretically if Linux were to receive the same amount of commercial software support as Windows does, it would make it less secure and easier for hackers and trojans to target. (The only reason i switched over from XP to Ubuntu was an unescapable blue screen)

Also, if you really think Ubuntu can't even COMPETE, I think there are many things we should evaluate in that debate, and I think a structured debate would be a good idea.

For me it seems if people think about it and take some time to learn the alternative then they will be better off - we would all be better off.  But to compete in the marketplace is not possible at the moment.  Most people are reactionary in nature and in the financially motivated commercial world we live in ubuntu cannot compete until someone (I know dell sort of tried) gets KDE onto new laptops in the computer shops.

---

### Post by entangled on 2009-12-29
I don´t think you can say that people don´t care about the OS and then imply they will upgrade to Windows 7 - and also pay for it?
 
I think many (not all) people are motivated to buy when they see advertising. That´s why commercial companies spend a lot of money on advertising.

Ubuntu would get a lot more customers if it was advertised on TV. But TV adverts cost money and Ubuntu is not for sale. It is not ´competing´ in the same sense as Windows with OSX and even there the competition is not directly comparable because Apple own the platform for OSX.

So things are just as you would expect. Sales figures don´t mean that Ubuntu is inferior. Sales are not, by themselves, a measure of quality.

### Post by AgentZ86 on 2009-12-31
Well, this is simply untrue

Linux does and is already competing it's growing not shrinking.

Simple fact by example:
Does Windows burn DVD's ?
NO
Not without DVD software, which is not something you have to hunt for on the net for some free version which usually has spyware,viruses and malware.

Does Linux burn DVD's 
Yes
Most Distros have multitudes of free options for that and typically included in the distro by default without any worries of spyware,viruses and malware.

The question is can Windows ever compete with the security of Mac or Linux ?
NO

Does Windows do things Linux or Mac can't ?
Yes
But depending on what you personally need to do, you may not even care about those things that Windows can do with all the other strings attached that Windows requires with Virus software and more.
You can't even open a word document or read/write to it without some viewer or MSOffice which is mostly the typical doc.
Linux has this ability by default as well.

Does Linux do things Windows can't and never will ?
Yes
This is very true, and yet it's also true the some of the things that Linux can do you may also not care about, but it depends on the user and what you want to do with your computer as well.

Anyhow, I think this concludes that Linux has a large following and is growing
More and more hardware companies are providing linux support and even laptops,desktops,netbooks etc. are offering discounted pricing for these pre-loaded with linux because of the OS savings.

I think that the question has already been answered by the huge following at distrowatch.com no question it's already competing and doing well in it's continued growth.

Thats all I know

### Post by Blue_Wolf on 2010-01-06
Well market shares say so but it's just nice to have something like this that's free, fast, reliable to a point. I'm having a hard time trying to figure it out so I'd rather just go back to 9.04 to avoid the issue for the time being. I love OS X I'd prefer a Mac over a PC any day. You don't need to be bashed for an opinion, to me it's more so people showing respect for your opinion and that's what to me is a respectable quality in a person. I agree with what you said Windows has a lot of features that make it appeal to people more, So does OS X though and so does Linux. ### Post by brion@cbkidder.com on 2010-01-06
Ubuntu works well enough for me now that I have been able to walk away from XP. Open Office replaces MS Office, Gimp and Inkscape replace Photoshop & Illustrator, and I'm thrilled every time my machine boots up in under a minute compared to ages for Windows. I have maybe a tenth of the crashes as I had before, and the only thing that makes me use Windows any more is websites that refuse to work within Linux or on Firefox...

One thing I don't like is having to go into Terminal for mundane activities, that bugs the crap out of me. But I notice I haven't had to do it much since upgrading recently.

The bottom line for me is -- my Ubuntu machine can run Windows better than Windows can run Windows. If I ever absolutely need XP, I can run it in my VirtualBox virtual Windows machine.

### Post by graphius on 2010-01-06

I am sorry, I really don't get why everyone is raving over Windows 7. Yes it is better than Vista.... um yeah....

### Post by brion@cbkidder.com on 2010-01-07
> **danielrmt said:**
> What "mundane activities" require you to use the terminal?

Sorry, I should have been clearer. Here's an example of when I have to use terminal for what should be a simple action. Often, for whatever various reasons, there are files or folders that are created by a program, but are created as locked or owned by root. The only way I can relocate or delete those files or folders is to go into terminal and use sudo or change the ownership, which is time-consuming and frustrating. I would much rather Ubuntu merely asked me for permission to move or delete via a dialog box within the gui instead of forcing me to open terminal and use sudo.

For example, a system backup program automatically creates a folder for the current backup within my home directory. After the backup I like to burn the image to DVD and delete the folder off my machine. Since it's locked, the only way to delete it is via terminal.

Many thanks to aysiu--now that I have a launcher for gksudo nautilus in my main menu I can do it all within the gui. Thanks aysiu!

---

### Post by brion@cbkidder.com on 2010-01-07
> **guvnr said:**
> .. that's weird .. what sites?  (shorely not?!)

There are a handful of sites that refuse to work under Firefox or Linux. One you may have heard of is GoToMeeting. In Linux you can be a participant in one of their meetings, but you cannot host. Another is a company that handles time cards and other sundry HR stuff for companies; their [website]("https://www.intersourcing.com/BrowserNSupported.htm") is strictly IE only. I have run into others, not often but just enough to be a PITA.

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2010-01-07
I don't want to sound elitist, but rather pacifist. In my experience, comments like, "Ubuntu cannot compete with OS X or Windows 7" fall on deaf ears when it comes to me, my family, and friends who actually use Ubuntu on a daily basis.

We're all happy with it. We get our work done, and do it fast. We don't have computer headaches. We just use our computers efficiently, get the programs we want for free, and get to make our computer exactly how we want it, to each their own personal taste.

We also enjoy software freedom (not as in price) on the side, but these days people aren't compelled so much by the ethics as they are with the ease of use and practicality of Linux.

I don't think Linux is competing with anyone. It's just being itself, and most people who find it are happy with it.

### Post by lemmyc on 2010-01-08
I haven't read all through this thread, but I'll chip in my thoughts.

Linux and Ubuntu are for me a kind of miracle the way they have come together out of so many disparate parts. I think this is a great achievement and give huge credit to the open source software community for it. 
Luckily the success of Linux for servers has served as a strong foundation on which to build desktop-oriented operating systems.

At the same time I think the effort of developing Ubuntu is really wasteful in its use of energy and time. The difficulties in making different packages work together seem huge. Also sometimes in using it - I guess everyone here has spent hours at one time or another trawling through help forums and endless web sites to find a way to get some hardware working on their system without breaking something else in their setup.

The www and the size of the user community just about makes it possible to keep on top of this underlying problem, but to me it feels like constantly rowing against the current.

I like the approach of an OS like Haiku, where it is still open, but everything is tailored to one distribution and written from the ground up. That seems so much more efficient and less wasteful of people's energies. If the dev community there was half as big as the Ubuntu community it would be mind-blowing.

Having said all that, Ubuntu 9.10 is for me now 'easy' enough and covers all the main bases (browsing, mail, office apps, wifi, printing, gui interface for all main tasks) for a non-tech user that I have installed it for my wife on her laptop instead of XP - and she is delighted with it.

I agree, Linux/Ubuntu is what it is - take it or leave it. That doesn't stop me from dreaming about what a really great open source OS could be like though ...  :)

### Post by gshockxc on 2010-01-18
Putting my two cents in with everyone else.  I switched from Windows to Linux for one reason: Control.  I got really tired of feeling like everything I did on MY machine had to fit within the confines of what Microsoft deemed as appropriate.  

I'm not a gamer (though that could be fun).  I got used to  Windows, and it was readily available.  I got pretty good at doing a lot of things.  Savvy, but not an expert.  After a while, there were more things about Windows that didn't work, and I still didn't have control to fix the issues.  So I decided to switch to Linux.  I still use Windows XP at work.  But it's a company machine, so I can't customize it to work best for me, and if something breaks, I'm not allowed to fix it, even if I know how.  

I like having control over what I do.  I moved to Ubuntu for that reason specifically, BUT, there are several huge latent benefits.  
[LIST=1]
[*]There are as many unique installations as there are users.
[*]You have the opportunity to learn, and thus control what limitations your system has or doesn't have.
[*]You control the safeguards, what, if, and how many
[/LIST]

Sure, I've been annoyed at things I didn't understand, frustrated by terminal commands I couldn't begin to remember without writing them down, and befuddled by the endless amount of information that's available.  Several people have said the same thing, "It's all about what 'YOU' want your O/S to be."  For me, Ubuntu (and Linux) works because it's free, there's a community of support, and I control what, when, where, why, and how for MY O/S.

That's why I'm a Ubuntu user (Karmic)

### Post by Malakai on 2010-01-19
I can say one thing for sure, on the netbook front Ubuntu 9.10 runs smoother, looks better, and has virtually no drawbacks vs windows. The main thing that has kept me off linux for the last 10 years as a full time OS is gaming, and I do not use my Netbook for games, it can't run anything even relatively new or with a 3d engine.

So for me, without lack of gaming ability as an issue, Ubuntu 9.10 is absolutely a viable and even optimal choice of OS. It looks gorgeous with compiz and gnome, font rendering is simply breathtaking (I imported a bunch from my win7 install, but its the rendering that counts), Im still in awe at how much better fonts look in ubuntu than win7.

I had issues when I first installed with the wlan and bluetooth drivers with my Asus 1000he, both had poor connectivity and signal quality, lots of drops both of my bluetooth mouse and the network connection.

But after searching these forums I replaced the default drivers with the compat-wireless ones and all is well, working perfectly for over a day now. Lucky too that it fixed both bluetooth and wlan hehe. The install scripts for the package were also very nice & easy to use =)

### Post by Ralph Hinkley on 2010-01-19
Ok. I know I'm the newest of the "noobs", but I had to chime in on this. I have only been using Ubuntu for a few months right now, but already I have encountered the occasional bug and have had to learn how to fix it - STEP 1.) LOOK HERE. (Thank you much for all the support.)

But as for the question can it compete? I have to say en emphatic "YES". Microsoft already dominates the market with windows, yet they continue to push farther and farther to limit what you can do with YOUR computer. Every Apple product couples beautiful design and innovation with the most restrictive software in the industry. So with these 2 giants in positions to move the software industry in whatever way they choose - why are any of us here?

That's my point. I'm sick of buying a piece of technology that has fantastic capabilities but is limited by proprietary software. I'm tired of watching my hard drive spin incessantly because my OS is bogged down with garbage that I never wanted, and can't remove. Obviously I'm not alone. The iphone is arguably the most popular phone on the market right now, yet countless people feel the need to "jailbreak" their phones so that they can do what they want with them. Every person on this forum has their own reasons why all the "polish" of other OS's isn't enough for them.

So is Ubuntu as widespread as Windows and OS X? - no. But can it compete - I'd say it already is.

### Post by BT1 on 2010-01-20
I am a fifteen year Windows user, 10 of those years having intermediate skills in deploying and working on Windows systems. I remember Windows 95, 98, staunchly opposing XP because it needed 128 megs of ram minimum while windows 98 needed so much less (lol!). I was abhorred with Vista's "Aero" thing and still to this day while using any Windows system convert the theme to "Windows Classic". Windows 7 thus far does not impress me because I don't like the new layout. That being said, I initially did not like each step forward when it comes to Windows updating their OS. With Ubuntu, each 6 months is like Christmas to me and I spend the whole month before release chewing at the bit going to sleep excited and waking up to check the countdown. No other OS has done that for me. Even when Windows 7 came out my expression was more like "Eh, gotta spend another $150+ on the newest OS...blaaah." rather than "AHH, EXCITEMENT OVERLOAD!" Keep in mind I still use both Ubuntu and Windows on a daily basis for work and play.

When someone says "it's not polished enough" in reference to Ubuntu Linux being complete and bugless, I have to agree, it's not. I've installed various .ISO mounters only to get a message that my ISO isn't a certain type, and it won't let me mount it. I have to switch over to my Windows partition to use Daemon Lite Tools which mounts it with ease. Things like that happen when I use both systems. Where one won't do something, the other has a simple solution and it does go back and forth really.

Are the desktops the final word? No. To be honest, Win7 and Ubuntu have ugly desktops to me. I don't like all the space age blue crap in windows, and I really don't feel like booting up into Ubuntu around my friends and having the default theme look like a turd was smeared onto my background. I understand the philosophy behind Ubuntu's default, and I understand Windows as well. I change both desktops as that is usually immediately on my list of "to-dos" when installing both systems.

At school I am surrounded by XP on everything from Library computers to work computers, and they all run okay. The school ones are optimized for net browsing and MS Office, so they run super fast at what they do. That being said, I've run Xubuntu on a School computer unoptimized, and it ran faster. This was largely due in part to the fact that there was a super power anti-virus installed that scanned every click of the mouse it seemed like. That's something in my eyes when an unoptimized fresh install of Xubuntu runs faster than a tweaked version of XP, even with the AV excluded.

Both XP and Ubuntu are easy for me to use, but you have to think about the fact that I've used Windows for 10 years on an intermediate level. I've only been serious about Linux for a few months and thus far I am able to do 90% of what I am able to do with ease on Windows, on Ubuntu with a short learning period.

Possibilities. One of the things that got me into Linux was the fact that I could have a full Linux installation on a thumb drive and walk around with my operating system in my pocket. I'm not just talking about a LiveCD, I'm talking about having my /boot /home /var, etc all installed on a thumb drive like people normally have those installed on a hard drive. That's a possibility that never existed for me in Windows. Another possibility that's opened up for me due to free Linux distros is setting up my own servers. I could never afford to buy operating systems that ran like servers, or even afford to buy or configure my windows systems to run a native server. With Linux I can do this in a powerful, and free way. It has me drooling at the prospect of doing something I couldn't believe I could do before. I'm not studying guides on how to do it, for free, online. Linux opens up possibilities.

Viruses? I never really got viruses on Windows unless I was stupid and illegally downloaded music or something, so 98% of my windows experience has been virus free. Ubuntu doesn't have viruses? Well that's great but I've learned from Windows so I wouldn't be looking for viruses with Ubuntu anyways.

There's so much more to compare and contrast, and it usually goes both ways on certain things. But the main reason why I still install Ubuntu Linux onto my most cherished electronic devices is not because I think it is a good "Windows Alternative", but because _it_ _is_ _a_ _great_ _operating_ _system_ _period_.

Have fun with the discussion, it's been a long read lol.

---

Some good points, although I do have to disagree with some of them. It's undeniable that Ubuntu 9.10 does have a number of bugs which are slowly being sorted but I don't see where your user-unfriendliness is coming from. Ubuntu is the first Linux which "just works" and doesn't need the rest of the day to set up (unlike Windows). Also, Canonical have found ways of getting around the command line and the cryptic graphical utilities.

I think that OS arguments are a matter of opinion, for example, my friend is a huge Mac OS fan and I can see why, another of my friends is a Windows fan. It's just a matter of swings and roundabouts!

---

### Post by BT1 on 2010-01-21
> **xpressense said:**
> I have had Macs for years and years, never used Windows, hust Mac and Linux, one difference thats sticks out between Mac & Linux communities is that Linux forums and in particular this one has endless threads about Windows, :D go on a Mac forum and it's a breath of fresh air as you may have a small section out of sight at the bottom for Windows discussion.

;)I contribute this to Mac users being content and same goes for Windows users on their forums, this place would improve ten fold if "Other OS talk" be brought back to keep the endless cycle of Windows threads off the main forums. sometimes reading through the Cafe is like pulling teeth.[-X

Another viewpoint, Mac is flooded with pop-culture addicts and that automatically lowers the intelligence of the group as a whole. Lower intelligence = less questions, less good debates, and less utilization of information regarding differences and approaches. I work in a book store and I can swear by my big toe that 7 out of 10 of the people who come in looking for Mac software usually give me a blank expression or one of genuine "I'm thinking hard" looks when I ask say, "Okay, just so that I give you the correct version of this software, do you have a 32 or 64 bit Windows installation, or do you have a Mac?" I never fail to get the response "Uh, it's an Apple?". Yes, they say it like its a question *_***I***_* need to answer for them. More than a few of the college students who walk in with an iPhone stand there looking for their "PSC-POLITICAL SCIENCE" books under the big "PSY-PSYCHOLOGY" sign, all while checking their e-mail on their iPhone trying to figure out why their book isn't on the shelf. I could honestly say the same for more than a few Windows users, but it looks worse for the Apple community to me since the market share is so much smaller for Apple. This means Microsoft can afford a large amount of morons because there's still at least 5% of their +90%market share that's more technologically intelligent than the whole 5%(ish) of Apple's Market share that's a combined mixture of both smart and dumb users. 

Now with all that said, my goal was to point out that I don't expect a lot of people on Mac forums to be intelligently discussing the differences, advantages, and development issues between Windows, Mac OS, and Linux.

Here on these forums it is understandable to have a great deal of people talking about Windows and Apple because this is an open source forum full of people who don't want to pay $150+ for the newest Windows OS, or pay $2000+ for mediocre hardware on a Mac. Many people here hate both companies because of their business ethics and business plans. Is it so hard to believe they would be a frequent topic of discussion on an Open Source Forum?

I'm not saying all Apple users are stupid, just that because of Apple's dependence on pop culture for converts (iLife!!!! Blah!), they're bringing down the average IQ level. In this forum you see a lot of people who got smart about MS and Apple and went to look for an operating system that *most people don't even know exists yet*. Not only have they gotten rid of their old OSes, they've more than likely done their own base Linux install (rather than have the OS already pre-installed onto their hardware out of the box), did their own system updates, and downloaded their own video card drivers. Most of the Windows/Mac users I know wouldn't know how to get to step 1 (my managers have me doing most of the trouble shooting on our XP PCs at work because all they know how to do is use Office/Excel type stuff).

In summary, _it's a different type of forum here_. I consider most of the average computer using people on these forums to automatically be a step ahead of their average computer using neighbours simply by installing their own Linux Distro themselves. May not sound like much to the computer wizards out there, but for the average computer user, that's huge! (How easily we forget eh? ;-)) Don't expect this forum to be like the other two.

### Post by Daremo_06 on 2010-01-25
I wasnt planning on reading this thread as I was originally searching for posts about Grisbi, but after reading multiple pages I wanted to chime in.

I have been running exclusively Ubuntu since last march or so at home.  I also have a work laptop running XP so I have a windows machine when I need it.

I have to agree with the original poster's points about Ubuntu.  Too often to change something means having to research it, post here, wait for a response, then do some stuff.  Its highly frustrating at times.  I am an extremely technical person, but sometimes I don't feel learning how to edit x.org just so I can get 2-3 monitors to work.  I would much rather just connect them, click on a auto-config of some sort and let it go.

That is the point he was trying to make, that there are too many things that are *NOT* intuitive to the avg user.  

Here is a great example.  On the NVidia X Server package when you make changes to dual display, such as changing positions of screens, you might want to save those settings to X.org.  Well in 9.04, you had to run it via GKSU before you could save it because x.org required root access.  Someone else caught on to this and reported it as bug.  

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/hundredpapercuts/+bug/200868]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/hundredpapercuts/+bug/200868")

Well it took almost 2 years to change it to allow you to save the file.  If you read through the thread you can understand what I am talking about when I say things are not intuitive.

I think its a great OS, but it definitely needs to be "defragmented" before it truly competes with MS to the avg user.

---

as for me, Mac OS X is the same as ubuntu, but u have to pay for it.

> **Daremo_06 said:**
> I wasnt planning on reading this thread as I was originally searching for posts about Grisbi, but after reading multiple pages I wanted to chime in.

I have been running exclusively Ubuntu since last march or so at home.  I also have a work laptop running XP so I have a windows machine when I need it.

I have to agree with the original poster's points about Ubuntu.  Too often to change something means having to research it, post here, wait for a response, then do some stuff.  Its highly frustrating at times.  I am an extremely technical person, but sometimes I don't feel learning how to edit x.org just so I can get 2-3 monitors to work.  I would much rather just connect them, click on a auto-config of some sort and let it go.

That is the point he was trying to make, that there are too many things that are *NOT* intuitive to the avg user.  

Here is a great example.  On the NVidia X Server package when you make changes to dual display, such as changing positions of screens, you might want to save those settings to X.org.  Well in 9.04, you had to run it via GKSU before you could save it because x.org required root access.  Someone else caught on to this and reported it as bug.  

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/hundredpapercuts/+bug/200868]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/hundredpapercuts/+bug/200868")

Well it took almost 2 years to change it to allow you to save the file.  If you read through the thread you can understand what I am talking about when I say things are not intuitive.

I think its a great OS, but it definitely needs to be "defragmented" before it truly competes with MS to the avg user.

I have to agree with a lot of what you said.  But even though it's not intuitive to the user, or requires us to do more work, I would rather have that option than the one where Windows does whatever it wants without my explicit direction.

I'm running Karmic, and the Plasma desktop will crash from time to time, and a few second later it will start back up.  If that was a Windows machine, the GUI being tied to the OS would be crashing my system repeatedly.  

Give me control, I'll deal with the frustration.  If you want Linux, but don't want to have to deal with it, then just get a Mac.  I'm on my girlfriend's MacBook Pro.  I've got to say, if I hadn't already gotten into Linux and built my systems, I would definitely go this way.  But that's the $/time tradeoff we all have to make.  How much time do you put into learning, before you decide that it's cheaper if you just pay for the OS that works out of the box?

We all have our choices to make.

### Post by biomedtech on 2010-02-07
It's been said many times, but bears repeating.  

What do you want for nothing? 

How nincompoops rant and rave over the differences between **Free** and billion-dollar operating systems is stupid. **Free** does 100% of what I need at home, on a personal basis:

Talks to my wireless N internet
Provides compats with MS Office
Provides compats with e-mail
Provides compats with web browsers (*remember when netscape used to cost money*?)
Compats with BT
Plays streaming video. When I see a pirated movie in Usenet, I just click on it, and it plays.  
Compats with most music files
Compats with my PDA
Compats with regular printers that I can buy anywhere. 

And a whole bunch more that I probably don't understand to appreciate. 



That being writ, there are some things that Ubuntu (**Free**) doesn't do well, but that is where I change to a not-free system to accomplish the task.  

I don't try to bash **Free** for not wiping my *** the way I like.  

I appreciate things that **Free** does for me, such as not having to spend money on anti-virus apps, adware apps, and not needing to run redundant firewalls. I love **Free** for saving money on utilities. **Free** gives me all these things on a portable computer, instead of a 400 Watt desktop machine. 

I love **Free**. Especially since I was once an Amiga owner, where everything was even more expensive than a Mac. 

I also own a Mac, but only use it for the 1 or 2 times that I need to edit a high-definition video, or need just a little more cpu power than my Eee PC 901 can provide(but that's not **Free**'s fault)

Stop being stupid.

### Post by lautarop on 2010-02-08
(not going to read up all the discussion...probably just the same things you can find on the web, but I am going to post my humble and brief opinion on this)

1) Ubuntu is not "hard". It may be different, but, just because now everybody is used to how windows handles the she-it, it doesn't mean that linux is harder. There is no absolute "intuition parameters". They are relative; so, given the win background most people have, linux may not be that intuitive. But give your grandma a mouse, they wont have a clue to what they have to do. I've had to deal with lots --god, lots-- of old folks that just have no idea on how use computers, and windows didn't make it any easier...you just had to train them to do stuff as if they were animals; after good training, they gain the "intuitive windows feeling"...but it is just that, training. I don't see why THAT has to be the training people should get


2) Suppose linux is harder and windows is easier for some people; ok. Now, why is it **that** unreasonable that, maybe, you just* need* to learn new things to use a computer? You already accepted to learn that "grabbing and dropping icons" (do you know how hard it is for some folks to get that concept, when they have no idea of what you are talking about) is the most efficient way to deal with information, why don't you try to learn commands to do that?


- - -

Well I think that got messed up, the point (I think) I wanted to make was:

The "windows is easier than linux" argument has to flaws:

1) It is not clear what "easier" means. If you are just talking of people used to windows, then it is silly to say that windows will be easier to them.

2) Even if you can prove that "the windows ways" are related to some easier-to-learn patterns, that doesn't mean that there is nothing you need to learn to know how to use windows.



blah, nevermind

### Post by juan legarda on 2010-02-08
I was so excited to receive my first Linux, finally something "more reliable" than Windows and Apple.

Well, for the first two hours it was just a pleasure. Now, two weeks have passed after just trying to get it running, it has gotten so many bugs: the full system has crashed after "update installation," the applications did not work after re-booting it, wireless is still missing, the documents went missing out there...

Sad. I would recommend Linux to a kid learning programming, I would not to a professional working on serious stuff, nor to a university/college student who would be at risk of losing everything at the most unexpected moment.

Sad. Reality sometimes is so disappointing...

### Post by nair on 2010-02-10
My opinion is that operating systems across the board have really blossomed in the last 6 months. I've been a computer nerd for at least 5 years now, and I don't remember such drastically postive, monumental OS developments like the ones we've seen, and I certainly don't remember them all coinciding within such a short timeframe, (although, to be honest my knowledge anywhere outside of Windows XP was extremely limited, so I can't judge the non-Microsoft competitors).  I've personally spent a lot of time with Windows 7 for work since a couple weeks before official release.  A couple weeks before that I bought my first mac, a mac mini, JUST so I could toy around with OS X 10.6, and I knew mac was compatible with some of my favorite applications.  And a few weeks before that I messed with Ubuntu 9.04, and it was the first Ubuntu distro I had personally messed with that was seemingly 'automatic' and 'plug-n-play' enough for some Windows tool like me.

A year prior, around September '08, I messed with my first Ubuntu distro, 8.04, which I distinctly remember being a royal pain to get functioning (1) with graphics drivers for a high-end graphics card, and (2) a wireless NIC.  It still caught my eye though, as I eventually came back for more jostling/learning.

I started using Ubuntu 9.10 a little over a week ago, and it really DOES seem polished to me.  9.04 seemed almost as spiffy, and for me, a complete linux nab, it was the first distro I would consider very usable.  I skipped 8.10 however, so I have no opinion there.

Ultimately, Ubuntu/Linux only runs about 20% of the applications natively that matter to me, and native is all that matters to me also.  Wine does not interest me in a practical sense, (although I suppose it does in a technical sense).  These applications range from computer games to online poker clients like Full Tilt and Pokerstars to business applications, including some of which are proprietary to Microsoft like CRM 4.0, which integrates with Outlook and I.E., which integrates with Windows, etc.  So, with a few exceptions, Ubuntu for me is still only a tool for tinkering and not something I would use for entertainment, work, or increasing my productivity.  That said, I think Ubuntu still really rocks and is absolutely great for what it is.  Recent distros really have kicked it up a notch, possibly due to more widespread acceptance, most notably from Dell.  It just so happens, however, that recently Apple and Mr. Softy have stepped it up as well.

### Post by mickie.kext on 2010-02-10
> **murderslastcrow said:**
> 
The whole point is that you're not supposed to be satisfied with Linux

The point is that people should not bitch about Linux just because they do not know how to use it. They got if for free (of charge), with freedom included (something you can't get with Microsoft or Apple no matter how much you pay) and least they can do is learn to use it. They probably were not so happy with Windows either when they first bought computer (or when they first time got attacked by nasty viruses), but they did not have know any better so they bowed their heads and kept using Windows. 

If you not have time to learn linux and ask your questions on forums, Canonical (or Red Hat, or Novell) is there and you can buy a support contract from them. It is cheaper than Windows licence, and with Windows you do not get any support. Just right to use software, without source code or any warranty. You get right to use Linux when you are born, no licence fee required, but do not expect warranty for something you got for free (of charge). If you still do not get satisfied with Ubuntu after buying support or you are not satisfied with kind of support you got for your money, you get a right to bitch about it for being to not accessible enough to ordinary user. But do not  bitch about something about you don't know.

Like [RMS said]("http://www.youtube.com/watch#v=aU960y2PXis&feature=related"), people had used to fight and die for freedom, and we are just required to learn to use (or pay someone to teach us to use) free and open source software to have freedom in world of computers. It is really easy choice.

> i was using ubuntu 9.04 for over 6 months and it didn't crash ! fathers XP and brothers 7 cash once a week

i had more crashes in my first month with ubuntu than i did in the previous 4 years of xp. crashes aren't as random as people would think, most of the time there's a reason for it. if you know how to use windows xp, it's not much more likely to give you problems than linux. like i said, vista was gash, took 5 minutes sometimes to boot up and log in on my laptop that loads ubuntu in under a minute, but by all accounts windows 7 is better.
anyway, i'm rambling, what i mean is that you can't compare different people's experiences. my dad always has trouble with his pc because my nieces are forever downloading rubbish to install. my xp install on my desktop has been reinstalled once (due to a major hardware upgrade) in 5 years, and still works as well now as it did when i first installed it and NEVER crashes. 
 i suppose technically in linux there's less that you can mess up when you install something, and if you're installing something that's likely to break your system then, generally speaking, it's something that needs to be installed from source which in itself means you have to be pretty handy with linux to install it in the first place, and should be able to fix any problems that are caused. with windows it's easy to install something that can break things, but so long as you're careful that isn't necessarily a problem.
people say you need to be careful with linux because one wrong command in a terminal can wreck your system, so both os's have their drawbacks.

### Post by Giant Speck on 2010-02-12
> **madnessjack said:**
> I had a BSOD the other day, and I was quite surprised! This machine had been running without restarting for about 5 months and XP crashed citing nVidia drivers.

Windows doesn't actually crash that much. Guess it's just FUD :P

The only time I had a BSOD with Vista was when I tried to see if Windows Media Center would detect my USB TV tuner OOTB.  It did, but it didn't like it.

The only time I had a BSOD with 7 was when I tried to install the software for my heart rate monitor.  I plugged in the IR receiver and for the most part it worked.  However, after leaving it plugged in for a couple hours, I got a BSOD.  It turns out that Polar doesn't make a driver for 7 for this specific receiver.  It was actually a miracle that it worked that long before crashing the computer.

### Post by needhelppeeps on 2010-02-13
Although I've had windows installations crash, I agree there is often an obvious reason for it. Bad sectors on the hard disk are one such cause that will cause random freezups, blue screens, IE problems, and all sorts of random things. My Vista installation did randomly crash with no hardware issues, but still it worked for almost 2 years prior to crapping out. Backups people :) All important data should be backed up to at least 2 different media, anyway.

I love Linux because if my OS needs reinstalled, I never have to worry about if I lost the install disk or license key. I never have to worry about activation issues. Applications to do 99% of what I want to do are freely available and I dont have to trust random websites and closed source development to get most of them. I have windows 7 and still choose to fire up ubuntu instead of it. Ubuntu runs as fast or better(even though its on older hardware). In my opinion, linux based systems are the way forward. There was a time when I doubted it could ever be a serious desktop distribution would come out of linux, but that time has passed. It's really progressed to the point where I feel comfortable installing it or other peoples' systems and recommending they use it for general purposes. I'd like to start buying systems with no OS loaded. I don't feel like M$ needs to get a kickback just so I can dump it to install ubuntu.

---

Another pissing contest. Seems like there's a lot of these threads these days. Dead boring......

### Post by misguided monkey on 2010-02-13
Crashes have not been an issue for me since win98, but I can report this: I recently performed clean installs of both win7 and Ubuntu 9.10 on two different machines (both dual boot.) In both cases Ubuntu worked better out of the box while with Windows 7 I needed to search for and install additional drivers to get everything working. 

This is a huge improvement over the first time I tried Slackware 15 years ago. It is true that some tweaking might be required but, unless you buy a computer with Windows 7 installed at the factory, Windows requires tweaking too. And when you go looking for help online, it's easier to find high quality help for Ubuntu. In fact, you can find EVERYTHING you need on just a few web sites (this being one of them.) I dare you to navigate to something useful on microsoft.com without shouting obsenities.

### Post by Kenny_Strawn on 2010-02-20
iDon't let ordinary users modify my source code.

iDon't have a full repository of software and drivers for the purpose of easing their installation.

iDon't allow users to copy my disks without my consent.

iDon't allow users to install me on more than one brand of computer (without being "activated", or locked).

iDon't have a productivity suite prepackaged.

iDon't boot to a full desktop environment from my install CD.

iDon't have a desktop cube....

LinuxDoes.

### Post by Dumae on 2010-02-21
Yes , I agree with you, Win 7 as evolved, refined made for dummies ( to a extent). It is very good , user friendly, I like it.
Ima new to Ubuntu , and this can be tricky, needs patience and ( frustating, if you are new) stimulating ..QUOTE=arrow.69;8410427]I'm gonna get incredibly bashed for posting this, but here it goes.

I've been running various Linux distributions on a bunch of computers since I was 13.  Windows would piss me off, Vista got released, or sometimes I just felt like playing around.  I've been through MEPIS, OpenSuse, Kubuntu, Ubuntu,amongst others.  It eventually became clear to me that Ubuntu was the overall best desktop distribution out there, if only for the tremendous amount of support there is out there for it.

I love Ubuntu.  It's a very capable OS and I love the idea of running a completely free and open operating system.  I've probably run some variation of Ubuntu on and off for the past four years.  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

However, I have to admit that Ubuntu simply can't compete anymore.  Windows 7 is a huge leap for Windows, and OS X has always been user-friendly and functional.  I know that under the hood, Linux is more stable, quicker, more secure than Windows.  But the truth is, to the end-user on the desktop front, the experience you get running Ubuntu doesn't measure up.  The problem isn't a lack of applications of features.  Ubuntu just lacks polish and integration.  Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users.  I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability.  Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer.  They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system.  They want it to just work for them.  Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now.

What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?[/QUOTE]

### Post by yester64 on 2010-02-21
its funny that this thread is still alive.

I am not sure why people argue about that anyway.
On Linux you can not demand things to happen. They evolve over time or maybe never. Its an open community.
I haven't found a software replacment for newsbin on linux to this day. I don't blame linux for it. There was perhaps never a demand or perhaps no one want it to make it in the first place.
Even if people say, why why, i still do love mac's for the really nice hardware and software. And if i had the money right now, i would buy one.

Every system has the apps you like or want and you may not find them on linux. What linux has is the community driven development and a very open OS which you can costumize so much that it maybe doesn't look like the original anymore.

Never cared about the cube or any 3D eyecandies. Most of the stuff i want, works with linux and i am very grateful for that.
And there are a lot of possiblilties, but don't expect everything to work like it did with Windows. It is a different world and as such works different.
I doubt linux will replace windows in the future. It is not easy enough to be operated by someone who has no knowledge in computers.
Not sure if that has to be a goal, but well....
From that point of view i would say, linux does not need to compete since you have two totally different ideas. This isn't business oriented.

### Post by aviedw on 2010-02-21
Maybe Linux simply isn't for everyone. Being like Windows or mac Os X might certainly take Linux away from its original purpose, Being Open. OS X is Unix wish is similar to Linux but its rapped so tight that most users would be hard pressed to find the command line or even know what it is for. 

Linux is different, If this piece of hardware doesn't work or if that graphics card wont allow dual or triple monitor support or if that web cam wont work or that cell phone wont connect who should we blame? Ubuntu or the hardware manufactures who wont make their drivers open. 

It takes a while for things to be ported over because developers have to either create something from starch or develop some type of work around thats sometimes buggy at best. 

My blackberry wont connect to my computer for updates through the internet. Should i blame Ubuntu for should i blame RIM for not creating desktop software for linux. 

And remember there is no profit motive. 90% of the world uses windows, Ati creates a new graphics card and wants at the very least 90% of the world to use it, what should it do? Of course create a driver that is support on that platform. Its all about profits and reaching the most end users. It has nothing to do with the quality of Ubuntu.

So to be fair when i first started with Linux i was frustrated much of the time. If it wasnt my sound it was my graphics card and if it wasn't that it was some other component. But support has improved over the years. I remember when if you had an Ati graphics card people would just tell you good luck.

### Post by mickie.kext on 2010-02-21

As for Ubuntu competing with OS X and Windows 7, no. It can't compete. But, I also don't think it should. On the server, it should be able to compete but, on the desktop, I don't think Ubuntu, or Linux period, should try. It wasn't originally designed to compete with the big names on the desktop. Doesn't mean it's bad, just means that it was designed with something else in mind.

### Post by aysiu on 2010-02-22
> **ktat said:**
> I'd agree that windows and mac give the average user - one who just want a "computer that works" - a better computing experience.   I don't. I know too many Windows (and, yes) Mac users who *constantly* have computer problems and ask **me** to help them fix those problems. The computer does not just "work."

What I will say is that Windows and Mac have better support ecosystems in two regards: [list=1][*]If you use Windows or Mac, when you have a problem, you are far more likely (than if you were to use Linux) to know someone who can fix your problem, whether that is a friend, family member, workplace or school helpdesk, or hired tech support. If you use Linux, you are more likely to be stuck using Google searches, forums, or IRC to help you solve your problems.[*]Even though there are a lot of peripheral devices (webcams, portable media players, printers) that work with Linux, very few of these devices actually advertise themselves as working with Linux. Whereas most devices will explicitly say on the box whether Mac and/or Windows is supported. Same with commercial software.[/list] So, a better computer experience? Yes, definitely, because there are structures in place to support Windows and Mac **when** (not if) things go wrong. A computer that works? Any school or business without a tech support department is foolish. Things always go wrong (and that's true of Linux, too).

Computers are human-made machines and are thus flawed.

### Post by mk4 on 2010-02-22
One of the reasons I switched from XP to Ubuntu is that I don't want to pay and use hardware resources for Anti-Virus and Firewall.

I think it's obvious that there is a correlation between market share and number of viruses available.

If Linux were to become more user friendly and take say 50% of the market, there would be a lot more Linux users who would open malicious email attachments, click on phishing links etc. And the platform would become more attractive for virus writers.

So, I hope that Linux never becomes mainstream OS and stays safe haven, even if I have to pay the price of occasional troubleshooting, fiddling etc. Anyway, I feel that's less hassle than having to defragment, reinstall and/or buy newer hardware every 2 years.

### Post by James Keating on 2010-02-22
I have just installed Windows 7 on two machines that mainly run Ubuntu, just in case Windows becomes necessary, like when I switched to an optical network connection and setting up the router required programs that only run on Windows or Macs. I must say I was pleasantly surprised. It looked better than XP and was easier to use. 

However -- though one installation was flawless, the other was not. On a common Acer Aspire laptop, Windows got the screen resolution wrong. That was easy to fix once I realized why everything looked awful. The fonts were terrible, too. One the first machine, tweaking the fonts was easy. On the other, I tweak away and the fonts still are awful. More important, Windows is unable to put this machine to sleep. Ubuntu had no such problem. I have had sleep problems on my own laptop, and though it took a lot of searching and testing to get it to work, I did get it working. On Linux, all problems have solutions. On Windows, you have to live with them.

As for the UI, Windows has nicer icons, but Ubuntu is easier to use. (And I suspect that sleek icons is a big part of why Windows gives so many people an impression of professional polish. I don't care for icons and put none on my desktop, but Windows 7 did look good, even after turning off all effects and going to the simplest theme.) Pretty much everything I could want is installed by default, and the rest is a snap to get. Windows doesn't come with anything superior except the video editor. Finding out what it does come with isn't easy, because the program menus are buried so far down -- the program menu itself is all but hidden. My other Windows UI bitches are too numerous to list. Windows' strongest point is having keyboard shortcuts for nearly everything, though few people know them. Linux's strong points begin with a sensible home directory and multiple workspaces and keep on going. 

As for resources, the second machine only has about 640 Mb of RAM. With all the gewgaws turned off, Windows 7 still needs nearly 400 just to run, leaving not much for any programs. That's an improvement on previous versions, as far as I can tell, and much better than I had feared, but is more than twice as much as a standard Gnome setup with Ubuntu. 

As for security, well. When I was looking for an Alexander Haig "I'm in control here" video clip on Google last night, I clicked on a search result that took me to a page that immediately ran a popup of a fake virus test in a false XP window, said my machine was infested and tried to download malware to my machine and run it. Since I was using Ubuntu, my machine remains clean. If I had been on Windows, it would have been infested in the first half an hour with no stupid behavior on my part at all.

### Post by mickie.kext on 2010-02-22
> **danielrmt said:**
> I think you mispelled "want", so I ask you: why would they want their hardware to work on Linux?

Because every hardware vendors main concern should be to make their hardware usable on as much platforms is possible. Linux is second most popular OS(OS X does not count because Apple pick which hardware works and which do not so  is not so much of a concern for IHVs) and they need to support at least 3-4 OSes if are really serious vendor. 

Every old school hardware vendor (IBM, Sun, Digital, Intel, SGI, HP... even pre-Jobs Apple) always had documentation about using hardware which include sufficient information how hardware works on low level, so savvy users can make driver if they really wanted to and if their OS is not supported. Those companies was first to contribute to Linux kernel. Now days, retarded companies like NVIDIA hide every bit of information for no reason.

There is not sane reason why they would not want to have Linux supported, especially  because  they can have that with little or no resources spent, since they can only let documentation free like AMD did, if they are rally cheap and uninterested for Linux. If they have genuine interest, then they can hire at least couple of kernel hackers and have top class Linux drivers. But there is no excuse for not even providing technical documentation.

### Post by Zoot7 on 2010-02-22
> **mickie.kext said:**
> Who else should be blamed? It is their job to open source drivers and contribute to kernel if they wont their hardware to work in Linux.
To get drivers to play nicely with Linux you invariably have to open source them, and a hardware vendor isn't going to want to do that whatever way you dress up the benefits of supporting Linux. There very well could be company secrets contained within that they don't want the world to know.

> **iRock said:**
> Claiming 1% is BS. There is no real way of knowing how many people are running Linux when we don't have to register each install. Why is it that almost everyone I know, either uses some form of Linux or have other friends that do? I have given out a few copies of Puppy for people to use because they prefer not to leave cookies on other people's systems. How do you get an accurate count of those people?
Yes the 1% is most likely inaccurate to a certain extent, but is it *really* that inaccurate?
How many businesses do you see running Linux? 
In my own line of work the preferred platform for Design Engineers is Linux (invariably Red Hat), but that's the only example I myself can think of. Also if I cite my own circle of friends, maybe 20% run some flavour of Linux but they're all either Engineers, Physicists or involved with IT in some form or shape. Hardly the norm by any account.

It's hard to get adequate numbers because of it being free, and you could argue that OEMs like Dell wouldn't see money in selling Linux pre-installed systems if it only had a 1% market share, but again that can't be backed up.

IMO the 1% is probably a tad inaccurate, but I couldn't see it being very inaccurate.

> **NoaHall said:**
> You really have no idea how money works, do you?

Developing drivers for that 1% - COMPLETE WASTE OF MONEY. Sure, do it if you INTEND for your hardware to be used with Linux, but not if you're just trying to make a "one fits all" rule. Because 99% is pretty damn near all.
Open documentation is only fine for the parts they can OPENLY DOCUMENT. They can't document things which they aren't allowed to, and even the stuff they can openly document is sometimes a bad idea.
Exactly. Why the heck bother with the 1% that is Linux when you can support ~90% of the market in one fell swoop with a far easier platform to develop for?

### Post by ViperChief on 2010-02-22
> **James Keating said:**
> If I had been on Windows, it would have been infested in the first half an hour with no stupid behavior on my part at all.


To imply that you would've been infected because of a pop-up is either FUD or ignorance (note that I said ignorance, not stupidity....there's a difference). I've come across those pop-ups numerous times and I have never had an infection due to one. Actually, I have never had an infected machine (be it Windows or Linux).

I still maintain the idea that it won't be long before people with malicious intent start writing a bunch of malware for Linux. We already have the #1 thing that's needed to get malicious code run and that's people who will trust whatever they're given and run it. We are starting to see that now (look how many "Just download this and run it" posts we see here). People here may have good intentions, but what happens when the trusting user googles something and just trusts people who aren't so good-natured?

I think using the Windows/malware argument is past its prime. Linux now has the same type of people Windows has in terms of userbase (i.e. trusting, sometimes just ignorant of the dangers, types). If you're going to argue in favor of Linux, use real advantages that Linux has (for instance, not having to reboot after updating a help file...really, Microsoft?).

### Post by ViperChief on 2010-02-22

How many people really do backups? I have a feeling that someone that falls to an attack like that doesn't really think about backing up too much.

### Post by Tristam Green on 2010-02-22
> **Post Monkeh said:**
> but the point is it is the person who lost the files who is suffering. if the whole system needs reinstalled then it's everyone who uses the computer who suffers. limitation of damage

<CFO> "Hey Post Monkeh, I seem to have lost all my files including our Quarterly Earnings Statements for Q309 that I've been working on all week, can you restore them?"
<PM> "I'm sorry Mr. CFO, I can restore it from last night's backup tape but any changes made today will not take effect at all."
<CFO> "WELL THAT DOESN'T HELP ME, OUR BOARD MEETING IS TOMORROW HOW DID THIS HAPPEN?"
<PM> "It appears you got a virus that wrought havoc on your Home directory, sir...but the good news is that the system is rock-solid and wasn't affected!! You know what they say, the good of the many outweigh the needs of the few."
*Two days later, PM's looking for a new job.

- versus -

<CFO> "Post Monkeh, I can't seem to access my home directory, what gives?"
<PM> "Don't worry sir, we seem to have been compromised slightly.  It only affected system portions of the server, but all the sensitive corporate data is safe in the respective user's home directories.  We should have the main system up and running within a few days, but in the meantime we can piggyback the files onto another server via file sharing."
<CFO> "Good thinking, Post Monkeh."
* Two days later PM gets a pat on the back at Associate Recognition Day and a $20 gift certificate to Applebee's Bar and Grill.



Which is the lesser of two evils here?

---

### Post by HarshReality on 2010-02-22
I'll agree with the free assumption and ability to reinstall. Took me @ 6 years to convince my wife to goto XP and even now she crashes it so often doing follish things (or not). Linux.. /home is a seperate partition ALWAYS so you just reinstall and away you go. This isnt to say you couldnt put Users on a seperate partition in Vista XP or Win 7 its actually easy to do but after so many times if your not backing up the activation (which the typical user doesnt) you end up having to call MS to activate your OS.. Personally I dont like having to involve outside sources into just getting my machine to START.

On a side note.. both my kids (ages 10 and 12) have Dell minis and windows was the stock OS. After 3 days I quote "daddy.. would you take this crap off its too slow" and low and behold they both got netbook remix. One is even running the alpha.

But politics will always be the same.. you can argue both sides of a fence and get nowhere. In the end game.. if you like it then use it. Its all about choice ;)

### Post by akand074 on 2010-02-22
I found this thread very amusing when I read the first post because my cousin and I have been talking earlier about how Windows 7 is great (compared to previous generations) but how both Windows and OSX could never match up to Linux. The thing about linux is that the possibilities are endless and you can completely flip your whole OS around and change it to however suits you best, allowing you to configure it to run its best for what you want to do with it. You also lose the constant reboots and instability of windows (all those damn freezes). OSX is based of unix just like linux, its really just a very modified version of the linux kernel but they made it more user friendly and feature-full like Windows which you would think would make it the best OS because it takes the mentality of linux but the functionality of windows. Surprisingly its not, I don't know how the managed to mess it up, instead of taking the best of both worlds they almost took the worst of both worlds. Its got the better resource efficiency like linux, but I mean your very limited, I mean you have more hardware and software support in linux than you do in OSX and your not able to configure it much like you can in linux. And also you get more instability and "weird errors for no reason" like in Windows. I mean if your just doing one thing with the OS like Video editing or something than it might be the best OS to use for what you need it for, but overall I would honestly say OS from best to worst in my personal opinion would be Linux, Windows, and then OSX (trying not to get other OS involved like Solaris, etc.) 

I'm sure everyone has different opinions and may disagree with some things I had said but overall you can't really say which is "best" although I just did for my opinion, but generally its what is best for what you need to do. But for general usage and for those who aren't completely computer illiterate and refuse to learn, linux is great because it gives you the opportunity to do what you want and gives you no limits. Especially when you take full advantage of the command line, I'm only a novice with it but experts can do things 10x faster for some stuff. 

In conclusion, I completely disagree with the fact that Ubuntu does not match up to OSX and Windows 7 but quite the opposite.

---

### Post by Frak on 2010-02-22
> **lancest said:**

Intel doesn't fully develop Linux drivers. They released the source code, so a lot of the work comes from contributors (albeit the Intel drivers suck terribly). ### Post by jackdale on 2010-02-23
I have 3 computers at home:
10yr-Old x86 Ubuntu 10.04 (32bit)
MacBook Pro: Mac OS X 10.6; runs VMWare Fusion2 with Ubuntu, windows XP, trixbox, OpenSolaris and (would you believe it) FreeDOS with GEM.
New x86-64: Ubuntu 10.04 (64bit) dual boot with Windows7.

I'm just a simple end-user.  I'm not a die-hard Linux fan (ie I would not use it if it wasn't worth while).

My current use of the different systems are as follows: Ubuntu 80%; MacOSX 15%; Windows7 3%; Others 2%.

Ubuntu offers me the flexibility, reliability and efficiency that I need (and all the eye-candy that I could possibly want).

Recently my wife needed to use an old (1996) windows 95 program for work.  There is no current version.  So, I booted up WIndows 7 to use its compatibility "trouble-shooter" (aka trouble-maker).  It did not work (I tried all the different possible settings).  So, next I tried WIndows XP (On the Mac using VMWare).  Again, no luck.  So, I tried it on the Ubuntu-box with WINE compatibility layer.  Guess what? It worked without having to do anything special.

I've heard ppl say Windows7 is very reliable and doesn't crash, etc, etc, etc.  OK, so it doesn't go into the blue-screen-of-death very often, but I have had too many problems with programs stalling and performing poorly (despite being designed for Windows 7).  Windows 7 recognises my wireless card, but its ("windows only") drivers don't handle it very well (frequent drops).  Ubuntu?  Natively detected the wireless card (although it is not officially supported) and works without any problems.

The new Windows 7 start-menu and navigation are both a mess and difficult to change.

Don't even get me started on all the rubbish with needing to have anti-virus, etc.  (Oh, and having to download the most basic of Desktop environment programs on a slow broadband connection is not cool).

Ubuntu can't compete with Windows 7? Get real.

OSX is a different matter.  I love Mac OS X.  It is a solid OS.  Mac OS X share many good things in common.  THere are some things that are done better on the Mac and some better on Ubuntu.  But, despite my die-hard love of Mac OS X (ie I would still use it if it was rubbish), where are all the programs?  It is so hard to find useful things for it.  MacPorts is a good idea, but still in its infancy compared to OpenBSD/FreeBSD and Ubuntu/Debian (and its not even officially supported).  Ubuntu? sudo apt-get install [almost anything I want/need]  (or if I want to browse, I can use the Ubuntu Software Centre).  I do like the Mac dock.  But cairo-dock is almost as mature and has some additional functions that make Mac's dock look infantile.  I also like the native compositing in OS X.  But Compiz leaves it for dead and is actually more efficient/functional (at least the way I have it set up).

Ubuntu can't compete with Mac OS X?  Yeah right.


As for the other distros that I have used (OpenSolaris, OpenSUSE, Mint, VectorLinux, OpenGEU, OpenBSD, FreeDOS+GEM, Windows XP (and older), Mac OS 9 (and older), ReactOS, etc), well lets just say that there is a reason why I'm sticking with Ubuntu.  I'm not a die-hard linux fan.  I just find that Ubuntu just works and is as flexible as I want/need it to be.

---

### Post by magic-neophyte on 2010-02-28
The bugs flatten out several months to half a year AFTER every release. Or they are often quite easy to fix. (In my experience WAY easier than reinstalling buggy Windows drivers!) 

I am practically bugless on several systems, including my macs. I feel OSX cramps me into using one glossy interface, with broken support for only a small portion of the open source software.

Linux is the platform of choice for me.

The Ubuntu default interface is very cool indeed, if tweaked with a little.

But hey, Enlightenment is just around the corner. If you want to feed the pockets of Steve Jobs and his stockholders, be my guest.

### Post by Solethara on 2010-02-28
The problem is, most people is afraid of things like terminal or making your own choices.

What people want in their computer is:

1. A player that can play anything 
2. Office program that can type.
3. Problem free wireless and internet connection.
4. Managing their phones mp3 players etc... with their computer
5. Browsing net.

These are what a normal everyday computer user wants. Now lets see where ubuntu fails:

1. Both ubuntu and windows fails this. Windows needs CCCP and a decent player while Ubuntu needs a decent player and codecs (So, the same thing.). Notice  faults are same. But the problem is when in Windows you do 2 googles, download 2 exes double click, next, next... and you are ready to go. On ubuntu you have to type in commands and guess what? people are scared of typing commands, even though the process is nearly same.
2. Ubuntu covered this fairly good but Microsoft Office is just too popular.
3. Direct connection (using a cable) got no problems, but wireless is the real downer when it comes to competing with Windows. Its just too problematic and too complicated for an end user. I use ubuntu on my desktop but if I'm ever to buy a laptop, I don't think I will use Ubuntu on that because of wireless problems.
4. Since Ubuntu isn't mainstream companies could care less, and this aspect is also problematic. Is there anything Ubuntu can do to fix, well we are trying. And this is an important issue with your everyday non-technical user.
5. If you can establish internet connection this is not a problem. But I heard some people had problems with flash.

So If you want Ubuntu to become mainstream you have to this:

1. Include a .txt to how to download codecs and include an up to date mplayer with ubuntu. Make it easier than it is on windows. Don't make people search anything because they fear searching.
2. Nothing can be done about that but since MS Office is too expensive (most people uses cracks but whatever) I think OpenOffice has a chance in this area.
3. Wireless has to be fixed somehow. This is a really important issue. Actually I tried ubuntu 2 years ago but I was using wireless back than and it was really problematic so just for that reason, I had to go back to Windows.
4. Actually I don't need anything about this so I haven't researched it, but if this can be somehow fixed one of the biggest obstacle would disappear.
5. Flash problems is an issue but not that important compared to the others.

Sorry for my bad English.

---

### Post by Toaster Box on 2010-02-28
> **arrow.69 said:**
> I'm gonna get incredibly bashed for posting this, but here it goes.

I've been running various Linux distributions on a bunch of computers since I was 13.  Windows would piss me off, Vista got released, or sometimes I just felt like playing around.  I've been through MEPIS, OpenSuse, Kubuntu, Ubuntu,amongst others.  It eventually became clear to me that Ubuntu was the overall best desktop distribution out there, if only for the tremendous amount of support there is out there for it.

I love Ubuntu.  It's a very capable OS and I love the idea of running a completely free and open operating system.  I've probably run some variation of Ubuntu on and off for the past four years.  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

However, I have to admit that Ubuntu simply can't compete anymore.  Windows 7 is a huge leap for Windows, and OS X has always been user-friendly and functional.  I know that under the hood, Linux is more stable, quicker, more secure than Windows.  But the truth is, to the end-user on the desktop front, the experience you get running Ubuntu doesn't measure up.  The problem isn't a lack of applications of features.  Ubuntu just lacks polish and integration.  Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users.  I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability.  Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer.  They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system.  They want it to just work for them.  Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now.

What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

For me Ubuntu is the best OS i have ever used the only problem is most companies do not make their software for ubuntu.
they prefer to make for the mac or windows.
that is why i make dual boot with my windows 7.
but I still use Linux more  because it's more safe surfing in the net with him. I use windows 7 more for play games .

### Post by MattBD on 2010-02-28
> **magic-neophyte said:**
> The bugs flatten out several months to half a year AFTER every release. Or they are often quite easy to fix. (In my experience WAY easier than reinstalling buggy Windows drivers!) 

I am practically bugless on several systems, including my macs. I feel OSX cramps me into using one glossy interface, with broken support for only a small portion of the open source software.

Linux is the platform of choice for me.

The Ubuntu default interface is very cool indeed, if tweaked with a little.

But hey, Enlightenment is just around the corner. If you want to feed the pockets of Steve Jobs and his stockholders, be my guest.

I agree, OS X's user interface is inflexible compared to something like KDE (although for me KDE4 isn't quite there yet), although I like the consistency of its key bindings. It's also seriously lacking in hardware support - my MacBook doesn't support my mobile broadband USB stick, even after switching to Snow Leopard, and Ubuntu has supported that fully since Intrepid (even before then it was detected, just NetWork Manager couldn't control it).

Also, I find the lack of package management on OS X a real pain. People normally associate compiling from source with Linux, but I've had to do so far more times on a Mac. And I've always hated having to open individual applications then have them flash up an "Upgrading!" warning, so to some extent using a Mac is almost as bad as using Windows.

### Post by noneofthem on 2010-03-01
Actually, I switched to Ubuntu almost 5 years ago, mainly because of Vista and because I wanted to learn something new. I never looked back. I had some issues with my wifi and my ati graphics card in the beginning but these problems have long vanished. I really have to think very hard to find something that doesn´t work for me on Ubuntu. On the other hand I get sick when I have to use Windows at work or on some friends computer to fix it again. I think Ubuntu is getting better with every version and I don´t see how Ubuntu cannot compete with the other two. The main difference is how you do the administration but most Windows users don´t do that on their systems themselves either. They always call a friend to fix it. I showed Ubuntu to some friends of mine who were complete beginners. Even on Windows all they can do is use the internet, write letters or listen to music. For some reason these users had no problem whatsoever to switch to Ubuntu and they love it...

### Post by KiwiNZ on 2010-03-02
I will relate a story from here in New Zealand . A company producing and selling wine was finding their product was not selling. It had won many prizes  for quality and the price was lower than their competitors. 

They took their product off the market, relabeled it, doubled the price and relaunched it. The sales went through the roof.This was for the same product no changes were made to it at all.

Being the cheapest or free does not always equate to market share. There is a perception that lower the price means lower the quality.

There will come a time when in order to gain market share Linux Distros will need to consider higher prices for their product but before then CONSIDERABLE work needs to be done around compatibility , quality , support and drivers.

---
