### Post by aysiu on 2010-03-02
> **KiwiNZ said:**
> 
Being the cheapest or free does not always equate to market share. There is a perception that lower the price means lower the quality. I think it really depends on what the product is. People tend to think more expensive wine is better wine. The same is not true for paperclips. If I sold paperclips for US$.99 for 500 I'm sure my sales would be better than someone selling similar-looking paperclips for US$10.00 for 500. Paperclips are understood to be cheap, flimsy disposable items, so cost is a bigger factor in purchasing choice than perceived quality.

Also, some things are just understood to be free, regardless of quality (many people use free email from GMail and Hotmail without any complaints about it being free and thus low quality; Opera tried to charge for its desktop browser, but had to give up and just make their browser free, because people understood browsers to be free).

It all has to do with expectations. To be honest, I would be skeptical if someone (not a friend or relative) offered me free wine. What's wrong with it? Why is it free? That's because culturally it's accepted that wine is expensive, especially if it's quality wine.

People don't even mind free software. In fact, a lot of people I know deliberately go out of their way to get free software even on Windows and Mac OS X.

Culturally, though, it's ingrained in most computer users' minds that operating systems (Windows and Mac) cost money, and if not then it must be a pirated operated system. And Microsoft Office is so popular that a lot of people can't imagine that OpenOffice could offer non-power-users all the functionality MS Office does but for free. iWork costing money reinforces this idea. Unless you're a Linux user or programmer (the two are not the same, by the way), you probably think of operating systems and office suites as having to cost money or being suspicious (illegal, dodgy quality).

### Post by Post Monkeh on 2010-03-02
> **aysiu said:**
> 
It all has to do with expectations. To be honest, I would be skeptical if someone (not a friend or relative) offered me free wine. What's wrong with it? Why is it free? That's because culturally it's accepted that wine is expensive, especially if it's quality wine.


don't believe the hype. it can be hit and miss but there's plenty of cheap wine that is really nice. dunno about wine from NZ, but australian wine is lovely.

anyway, back on topic, it's like you say, it really all depends on the product.
a paperclip is a paperclip. in cars, you can compare the car specs. sure there are some "special" things about different cars that just make driving them nice, but most aspects of a car can be quantified by figures. computer equipment often has specs that tell you how good it is (although graphics cards confuse me no end)

but things like wine(alcohol in general), food, holdiay destinations, there are no real "definable" differences. it's all down to personal preference. and a lot of the time, most people just think that if something is expensive then it must be good. people do it for tv's too.

i suppose software could fall into this category too, but it wasn't always like that. before msoffice, there were numerous different office suites, and people would research and buy the one that suited them. or if they didn't need real power functions, they would just buy the cheapest one. now people just think that msoffice is the only option. or the only "good" option anyway. and likewise, most people just equate windows as being the only good option available to them. macs are too expensive, and anything free just can't be the same quality, can it?

i think the biggest problem with charging for linux would be trying to convince people that they don't need all the windows programs that they'll no longer be able to use.

### Post by aysiu on 2010-03-02
> **Post Monkeh said:**
> don't believe the hype. it can be hit and miss but there's plenty of cheap wine that is really nice. dunno about wine from NZ, but australian wine is lovely. I am not very discerning when it comes to wines. Two-buck Chuck tastes the same to me as a $500 bottle. Free wine not from a friend or a communion service seems a bit suspicious to me, though.

> 
i suppose software could fall into this category too, but it wasn't always like that. before msoffice, there were numerous different office suites, and people would research and buy the one that suited them. or if they didn't need real power functions, they would just buy the cheapest one. now people just think that msoffice is the only option. or the only "good" option anyway. and likewise, most people just equate windows as being the only good option available to them. macs are too expensive, and anything free just can't be the same quality, can it? I have a friend whose XP installation recently got borked. She reinstalled XP but had lost her Office CD and was freaking out. I suggested OpenOffice (I'm pretty sure she's not a power user). She was very skeptical and asked "Does it have Word, Excel, and PowerPoint?" Well, of course it doesn't. Those are Microsoft Office programs. But I explained they can open and save .doc, .xls, or .ppt files. Eventually it became a moot point because she was able to track down her Office CD. Still, it's quite obvious to me she thought of anything not MS Office as being suspect.

> i think the biggest problem with charging for linux would be trying to convince people that they don't need all the windows programs that they'll no longer be able to use. That's why I'm very hesitant to move people over to Linux. For people who might show the slightest interest, I'd rather introduce them to open source Windows programs. If they can do open source on Windows, the transition to Linux will be a lot less painful.

### Post by t.rei on 2010-03-03
I have yet to encounter a system that is easier to install and setup for enduser usage than ubuntu.

Windows 7 lacks so much support and basically always requires you to get a 'delivered driver cd/dvd for your computer and windows7 (correct version)'. Otherwise many many features just wont work.

examples: Multimedia-Keys, Touchpad scrolling, various USB devices, various webcams, ...

After setting up the drivers the "big clicky action" starts. Install all the apps and utilities that are needed on a computer. It's timeconsuming and tedius. And if these installed programms are to be used by someone else but the administrator... boohoo. (try setting up openvpn on windows7)

It's easier to set up, and for the most parts way more intuitive than windows. At least thats what my grandma said... after starting with ubuntu (she installed it herself - I was ... surprised!) and later getting her hands on a windows (XP) computer and she just wanted to put some pictures on a dvd.

So far the free desktop environments are improving at an incredible rate. By now the only people that actually find some things 'less intuitive' in linux are ones that started with windows or mac. And honestly - it works both ways.

If they get lucid stable - really stable - and everything working (plymouth being one of the critical parts to make it polished) it's going to rock!

### Post by prodigy_ on 2010-03-13
I don't like M$ one bit, but I must admit that Win7 IS a breakthrough, because:

- it's very stable (alright, XP x64 was also stable but it lacked in many other areas);
- it supports most hardware out of the box - no more 800x600 ugliness on first boot and no more using nLite just to add RAID drivers;
- new Aero looks brilliant - beats OS X (of course, Apple fans will disagree);
- pinning apps to taskbar and start menu is really convenient.

---

Right now Linux suffers mostly from the lack of adequate video drivers. I can stand ugly Gnome UI. I love command line, use it freely and easily write my own scripts. But if I need to boot into Windows just to get rid of video tearing, then I can't say that Ubuntu is a perfect desktop distro. Sorry, but we're still not there yet.

### Post by Doctor Mike on 2010-03-14
I think it was a PR mistake to promote Ubuntu as an alternate to commercial software. Not because it isn't a good alternate (better in some ways), but because it gets forced into direct comparison. People have been throwing money at problems forever. Why do they throw money at problems? Results are quicker, even if not perfect.

What people seem to be missing here is that Ubuntu and its partner distros have the potential to be the only OS anyone would ever want. User driven, supported and improved. The approach taken with Ubuntu is a long term development and support which is the only way to produce the ultimate stable, reliable, cost effective OS.

The only thing that concerns me is the idea that Ubuntu needs to be compared directly with any commercial distro.

### Post by Frak on 2010-03-14
> **Doctor Mike said:**
> What people seem to be missing here is that Ubuntu and its partner distros have the potential to be the only OS anyone would ever want. User driven, supported and improved. The approach taken with Ubuntu is a long term development and support which is the only way to produce the ultimate stable, reliable, cost effective OS.

Let me fix this for you:

What people seem to be missing here is that Ubuntu and its partner distros have the potential to be the only OS anyone would ever want. **Developer** driven, supported and improved. **Ubuntu does not take** the approach to long term development and support, **but instead focuses on very hasty updates and improvements which cannot** produce the ultimate stable, reliable, cost effective OS. (Excluding LTS which is such a confusing matter. It's not meant to be stable, just bug free, secure and supported for a longer time. It's an odd statement at best.)

I'm speaking from experience and observation. We still have people who work on the Linux kernel who truly believe that all driver modules should be GPL and mainlined (rainbow land), and believe that a constantly changing API/ABI produces a better supported environment. They believe that doing the former gets rid of the problems of the latter, and obviously not taking industry best wishes into concern. These people don't just work on the Kernel, they're everywhere. It's embarrassing to think that these people really think that companies will drop everything just to meet their demands. "We have a tiny marketshare, and only a few niche users, but if you want to develop for us you need to just give us the source code and relicense it to the GPL."

My 2 cents.

---

### Post by Shining Arcanine on 2010-03-15
> **arrow.69 said:**
> I'm gonna get incredibly bashed for posting this, but here it goes.

I've been running various Linux distributions on a bunch of computers since I was 13.  Windows would piss me off, Vista got released, or sometimes I just felt like playing around.  I've been through MEPIS, OpenSuse, Kubuntu, Ubuntu,amongst others.  It eventually became clear to me that Ubuntu was the overall best desktop distribution out there, if only for the tremendous amount of support there is out there for it.

I love Ubuntu.  It's a very capable OS and I love the idea of running a completely free and open operating system.  I've probably run some variation of Ubuntu on and off for the past four years.  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

However, I have to admit that Ubuntu simply can't compete anymore.  Windows 7 is a huge leap for Windows, and OS X has always been user-friendly and functional.  I know that under the hood, Linux is more stable, quicker, more secure than Windows.  But the truth is, to the end-user on the desktop front, the experience you get running Ubuntu doesn't measure up.  The problem isn't a lack of applications of features.  Ubuntu just lacks polish and integration.  Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users.  I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability.  Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer.  They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system.  They want it to just work for them.  Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now.

What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

I actually prefer Gentoo Linux and switched from Windows 7 to Gentoo Linux on my laptop in January. I have not looked back and I am very happy with it. It took a while before things were configured the way I like them, but that happens whenever you reformat your hardware and install a new OS.

What other distributions did you try? Was Gentoo Linux one of them?

### Post by Roasted on 2010-03-16
My entire family uses Ubuntu and has also tinkered with other flavors of Linux.

I really think the argument of Linux being ONLY for tech savy users is beginning to diminish. Think about it, what does it take to install Windows versus Ubuntu? They're both on par. What does it take to install software on Windows as opposed to Ubuntu's software center? If anything, the software center Ubuntu has it easier. 

You can do basic things with Windows, or you can do advanced things with Windows. You can do basic things with Ubuntu, and advanced things with Ubuntu. Let's remember that the average user won't need to compile a new kernel - for the same reason that the average user probably wouldn't have to rebuild the registry from ground up in Windows.

I'm not saying Ubuntu is easier than Windows, but my argument is clear - the tech knowledge that used to be required to even install any variant of Linux has slowly diminished to a very small gap.

In short - The main distros out there running the show certainly can (and are) competing with OSX and Windows.

### Post by andrew5859 on 2010-03-17
Hello Everyone,

I've read all of your posts and agree and disagree with them.....while yes Windows has put out some good and really good OS's, to which I'm currently running Win-7 Business edition.  Yes Windows has a good OS, but they also impliment too much coded crap into their systems which create too many glitches, vulnerabilities and flaws....their OS's have had too many security flaws which allows hackers to easily get into and take control.  Who's had the most problems with security issues, who's had the most problem with hackers exploiting areas of a business and stealing sensitive information...Microsoft.  Do you ever hear of anyone saying that their Linux system was hacked into?....NO.  Linux has been competing with MS fo a long time, why do you think that so many people have switched over, it's because Linux is the only OS that is free and open source to everyone.  I, like many of you have tried and run many different distro's....Ubuntu, Fedora, OpenSuse, Mandriva....they're all great distro's and they all have their own bugs and glitches, but you work through them.  I think the only draw back to Linux is that there aren't a whole lot of really cool games to play or install on it like Windows.  I also think that if Linux would incorporate the same kind of transparency code into their distro's like Windows, they would steal a great many people away from Windows.

You all forget that Linux is one of the securest OS's out there, why do you think so many businesses out there use it....because it's secure and free from viruses and trojans.  Right now, I would like to suggest to Linux....that whether it be in Ubuntu, Mandriva or whoever.....get or develop some come in your distro's that would accomodate for the aero glass effect that windows has, and I'll guarranty you'll get more people to switch to Linux and you'll definitely close that gap of competition between you and Windows.  Besides, Windows can't compete with you on the grounds of Open Source and Free...they can't touch that, and that's their downfall.        ;)

### Post by Shining Arcanine on 2010-03-18
> **Frak said:**
> Mine can qualify as a slide show. Sonnet Encore/ST G4 1GHz (Aftermarket processor).

My laptop has some issues playing Youtube videos too. It has a GeForce 7900 GS, which has hardware acceleration for MPEG2 video, but Nvidia only released drivers that enable this acceleration for it on Windows. Newer GeForce graphics cards have drivers that enable hardware acceleration on both Windows and Linux. As a consequence, Youtube videos are played using the CPU, so anything higher than 480p is a slideshow on my laptop as well. It has a Core Duo T2400. :/

### Post by jrothwell97 on 2010-03-20

Let's see... Minesweeper, Su Doku, Mahjongg, Solitaire, Quadrapassel (apparently a Tetris clone) and gbrainy. This is not a good selection of bundled games.

As far as "other" games go, choice is similarly constrained: aside from a few, you're consigned to playing outdated games that have been open-sourced, running games at half-speed through WINE or making do with half-completed games that are either multiplayer-only or feel like a nasty technical demo. (It's either that, or *Yo Frankie!*)

> **Roasted said:**
> My Mac struggles to play YouTube videos (not even full screened) on 1.2ghz PowerPC G4 chip and 768mb RAM with OSX and Flash 10 installed.

I think it'd crap itself if I ever ran it as a server of some sort. :P

Your Mac could probably handle running as a server just fine. (I have a far less powerful one running Tiger simply to act as a giant power-sucking wireless bridge, simply because I've been unable to find a wireless card to install in this machine.)

The problem is merely Flash - it is *diabolical* on any OS other than Windows, and it's not particularly good there either. This is why the adoption of HTML5 and open alternatives such as Silverlight is of paramount importance.

### Post by Trilby on 2010-03-20
I've been using Ubuntu 9.10 64 Bit for about a month now on my laptop. I also installed Windows 7 Professional 64 Bit on it around the beginning of 2010 and am currently dual booting. I use Ubuntu for internet things and programing and 7 for gaming and MSOffice (which they ruined with the 07 version). A couple of days of ago, windows 7 suddenly die on me, refusing to boot, meaning I had use Ubuntu (and therefore OpenOffice which until now I've never really liked) to complete a major piece of coursework. I was pleasantly surprised to find how well was able to work on the task on Linux. I've now fixed windows 7 but given that MSOffice had come down a long way from what it was and OpenOffice has yet to peak, I'm split on which to use. I realise that I'm talking more about the programs on my OS's rather than the OS's themselves.

Regarding the later, I'll talk more about them. Ubuntu 9.10 has most of the features Microsoft brought in with vista and 7 but generally more refined. One thing that really makes Ubuntu for me is multiple workspaces (and since Macs do that too, expect to see that in next version of windows). I typically work with a 2x2 set up and find it very useful for keeping my apps and folders organised, whilst seamlessly swithcing between 2 or 3 things an once.

As far a stability is concerned, 7 and Ubuntu are both very good and both crash rarely if you treat them right. Windows get some stick it doesn't deserve regarding stability (Except ME, I had a windows ME once. I still have nightmares) but Ubuntu is still probably better.

I also love the things you can do with terminal. I will admit that it takes some learning but once one knows how to use it, it very efficient. Presumably, similar things can be done with MS' command prompt but I've never heard mention of it.

And you can't talk about a GNU OS without mentioning all the FREE software available for Ubuntu and Linux in generally. Installing things though Ubuntu Software Centre is so quick and easy.
I don't think there are any consistencies with the interface, I picked up very quickly.

There is of course the security of Ubuntu. Did you know Microsoft intentionally its system open to allow a market in anti-virus software etc? Well they do.

Ubuntu is much easier to install, than windows 7. Using Wubi, it took me about 10 to 15 minutes and not much longer when I installed it properly a week or two later (don't use Wubi, except to trial!!!). Windows 7, on the other hand was real pain in the rear. I actually bought (because unlike Ubuntu, it isn't free) it in mid-December but wasn't able to install it until well into January because stuff MS sent me didn't run at all. Their customer service was diabolical. I'd bought 7 directly from them online but their website wouldn't acknowledge that. Phoning them up wasn't much good either, the people at the other end barely spoke English and just couldn't understand what the problem was. I actually managed to get it to work only when their website pulled itself together and accepted I'd bought the thing in the first place, and event then I had to shove another £9 into it. I know why it didn't work now but the whole debacle could have been avoided if MS had in included just one more line of information when I bought it.* ](*,)

Software designed by a company to sell is there to make profit. GNU software written by individuals to share is there to be the best program that person or people can make it. This is what Linux and the rest of GNU will always have over MS Windows and Apple Mac.

In concussion, I would use Ubuntu all the time if I could and maybe I will some day. I going to keep to dual booting for now though; I like my games and I want to get my money's worth.

Oh yes, one last thing -- wobbly windows (small w); brilliant. :D



EDIT: Ps, since the BBC have redesigned iPlayer, it doesn't work when I'm using Linux :mad:
EDIT 2: Ubuntu is better for live disks and back ups
EDIT 3 : *Contrast with the Ubuntu comunity who basicly couldn't be keener to help fellow users.

---

### Post by Kenny_Strawn on 2010-03-20
> **arrow.69 said:**
> 
I've been running various Linux distributions on a bunch of computers since I was 13. 

That's funny, I started at 15, when Windows Vista was released. I upgraded, and I regretted it. So, I downloaded gOS first, and it did not support my Wireless G USB adapter (Linksys WUSB54GSC). Not only that, but it also would not auto-partition like Ubuntu does. So, I downloaded Ubuntu next (Hardy), and it too didn't recognize the adapter, but at least it automated the partitioning process.

I got a netbook (Acer Aspire One AOA110-1545) in December 2008 (Christmas, and I was 15 1/2), and I wanted it to come preinstalled with Linux so that Linux automatically knew how to handle its Wi-Fi adapter.

When I moved in March 2009, I tested the Windows 7 Beta. It was good to start, but it did not boot as fast as Linux, especially Ubuntu. The RC was worse, and also had compatibility issues with my TV tuner. In June that year, I also bought a new wireless network adapter (Linksys Dual-band Wireless N PCI [WMP600N]), and it worked with Linux Mint 7 "Gloria" and Ubuntu Jaunty out of the box. I then got Karmic, Lucid, and now, a dual-boot of Lucid and Mint 8 "Helena" (with Lucid being the OS that boots first in GRUB).

Back on topic, I know that Linux is not for everyone. I have had problems with it myself in the past as mentioned, and they were definitely showstoppers, and almost made me lose faith in Linux. It wasn't until I got the new Wi-Fi adapter (or when I got the netbook) that my feelings changed and I liked Linux again.

I've been running various Linux distributions on a bunch of computers since I was 13.  Windows would **** me off, Vista got released, or sometimes I just felt like playing around.  I've been through MEPIS, OpenSuse, Kubuntu, Ubuntu,amongst others.  It eventually became clear to me that Ubuntu was the overall best desktop distribution out there, if only for the tremendous amount of support there is out there for it.

I love Ubuntu.  It's a very capable OS and I love the idea of running a completely free and open operating system.  I've probably run some variation of Ubuntu on and off for the past four years.  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

However, I have to admit that Ubuntu simply can't compete anymore.  Windows 7 is a huge leap for Windows, and OS X has always been user-friendly and functional.  I know that under the hood, Linux is more stable, quicker, more secure than Windows.  But the truth is, to the end-user on the desktop front, the experience you get running Ubuntu doesn't measure up.  The problem isn't a lack of applications of features.  Ubuntu just lacks polish and integration.  Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users.  I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability.  Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer.  They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system.  They want it to just work for them.  Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now.

What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

I disagree with the bugs part, i have had tonnes more bugs with xp than heron, ibex, jackalope or koala (yes i know you were talking about 7) and i don't want to even talk about vista, i skipped that edition. Windows is just lucky they made windows 7

### Post by CrimsonBizarre on 2010-05-03
> **biomedtech said:**
> It's been said many times, but bears repeating.  

What do you want for nothing? 

How nincompoops rant and rave over the differences between **Free** and billion-dollar operating systems is stupid. **Free** does 100% of what I need at home, on a personal basis:

Talks to my wireless N internet
Provides compats with MS Office
Provides compats with e-mail
Provides compats with web browsers (*remember when netscape used to cost money*?)
Compats with BT
Plays streaming video. When I see a pirated movie in Usenet, I just click on it, and it plays.  
Compats with most music files
Compats with my PDA
Compats with regular printers that I can buy anywhere. 

And a whole bunch more that I probably don't understand to appreciate. 



That being writ, there are some things that Ubuntu (**Free**) doesn't do well, but that is where I change to a not-free system to accomplish the task.  

I don't try to bash **Free** for not wiping my *** the way I like.  

I appreciate things that **Free** does for me, such as not having to spend money on anti-virus apps, adware apps, and not needing to run redundant firewalls. I love **Free** for saving money on utilities. **Free** gives me all these things on a portable computer, instead of a 400 Watt desktop machine. 

I love **Free**. Especially since I was once an Amiga owner, where everything was even more expensive than a Mac. 

I also own a Mac, but only use it for the 1 or 2 times that I need to edit a high-definition video, or need just a little more cpu power than my Eee PC 901 can provide(but that's not **Free**'s fault)

Stop being stupid.

This.

The value-for-money of Open-source software is infinite.

And why just pick one when you can have both, or all three?

I have to say Windows 7 is pretty good, and I do keep it on my computer so I can run games and other little things, but I boot into Ubuntu most of the time because **it does everything I need it to do**.

Though, my school uses the new Apple iMacs and I can't say OS X has anything above Ubuntu. I actually think in some cases that OS X is quite far behind, and anything that they have that is better than Ubuntu is not justified by the massive amount you have to pay for it.

> **Solethara said:**
> The problem is, most people is afraid of things like terminal or making your own choices.

What people want in their computer is:

1. A player that can play anything 
2. Office program that can type.
3. Problem free wireless and internet connection.
4. Managing their phones mp3 players etc... with their computer
5. Browsing net.

These are what a normal everyday computer user wants. Now lets see where ubuntu fails:

1. Both ubuntu and windows fails this. Windows needs CCCP and a decent player while Ubuntu needs a decent player and codecs (So, the same thing.). Notice  faults are same. But the problem is when in Windows you do 2 googles, download 2 exes double click, next, next... and you are ready to go. On ubuntu you have to type in commands and guess what? people are scared of typing commands, even though the process is nearly same.
Actually no, I just doubled-click an MP3 file and it got the codecs for me in Windows and in Ubuntu. They also have decent players, Winamp for windows, and Exaile/Rhythmbox for Ubuntu.
> 2. Ubuntu covered this fairly good but Microsoft Office is just too popular.Yeah, but it is because of a few extra features MS Office provides.
> 3. Direct connection (using a cable) got no problems, but wireless is the real downer when it comes to competing with Windows. Its just too problematic and too complicated for an end user. I use ubuntu on my desktop but if I'm ever to buy a laptop, I don't think I will use Ubuntu on that because of wireless problems.
I don't use wireless; can't comment.
> 4. Since Ubuntu isn't mainstream companies could care less, and this aspect is also problematic. Is there anything Ubuntu can do to fix, well we are trying. And this is an important issue with your everyday non-technical user.This is quite a problem. I use a Samsung MP3 player, and it just doesn't recognise songs that have been put on via Ubuntu, maybe because there are no linux drivers?
> 5. If you can establish internet connection this is not a problem. But I heard some people had problems with flash.
Probably the browser, I use Opera (great browser, but is not best friends with flash) and have the same amount of problems (hardly any) in both OSs.

### Post by Chrysantine on 2010-05-03
> **CrimsonBizarre said:**

to become mainstream we need people to know that there are alternatives...

but it still (like all things software) needs work

perhaps there will be a "super" version of *nix that can work in harmony with Windows, but be itself protected from viruses and such

Windows 7 looks pretty, but IMO it's not all that much better than XP/VISTA

i know its WAY to idealistic, but hey, if we don't work towards perfection, then why even try???

I've been through MEPIS, OpenSuse, Kubuntu, Ubuntu,amongst others.  It eventually became clear to me that Ubuntu was the overall best desktop distribution out there, if only for the tremendous amount of support there is out there for it.

I love Ubuntu.  It's a very capable OS and I love the idea of running a completely free and open operating system.  I've probably run some variation of Ubuntu on and off for the past four years.  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

However, I have to admit that Ubuntu simply can't compete anymore.  Windows 7 is a huge leap for Windows, and OS X has always been user-friendly and functional.  I know that under the hood, Linux is more stable, quicker, more secure than Windows.  But the truth is, to the end-user on the desktop front, the experience you get running Ubuntu doesn't measure up.  The problem isn't a lack of applications of features.  Ubuntu just lacks polish and integration.  Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users.  I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability.  Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer.  They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system.  They want it to just work for them.  Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now.

What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

Just yesterday I had a run-in with Windows 7. I wanted some pictures from the mother-in-law and gave her a hard drive to put them on. I figured with all of the hype and such that she would be able to manage this task all on her own. She is a bit on the helpless side but she's not really stupid. It turns out that she could not figure the hard drive out. I was ready to blame her until I saw the menu that greeted me when I tried this myself.

The number of options presented to the user in Win7 in this situation is a bit overwhelming. I can tweak kernel modules and I found it somewhat overwhelming. I forgave the non-geek old woman for not being able to handle this "simple copy operation".

Something more along the lines of old school 68K MacOS is what she should have been presented with. 10 options, most of them with a lot of geekish text is not terribly suitable for the average consumer.

Something doesn't have to be castrated in order for there to be sane defaults. Sane defaults also should not look like the Rosetta Stone.

The bottom line is what option will scare the n00b consumer the least? What option will require the least amount of "geek support" and hand holding? What option will allow the typical user to get stuff done?

The Mac (68K or Intel) is probably a better starting place for inspiration.

Ubuntu probably needs some of the flim flam too just to avoid being labeled as too drab.

### Post by JEDIDIAH on 2010-05-10
> **Chrysantine said:**
> You spend considerably less time repairing, tuning and tweaking it than you do on your average Linux system. Whilst it may lack certain "hackish" things which you can do on your average Linux system and less support for various hardware, what it does support it does considerably better. 

Having a completely unified UI and applications is also a benefit, no matter how much you're waving around the flag of 'freedom'. 

OS X has its own good sides as does Linux. I chose OS X for my desktops, Linux for my servers - I'm happy with both and they work great together.

"freedom" means that I can consume whatever I like in whatever format I like. If I choose to use Apple technologies (not just MacOS), then I have to cater to it. That might even be an unecessarily expensive prospect. Chances are, the "polished" Mac interface will also do less.

In the end: unless you are willing to be led by the nose into the iTunes store, you will probably end up installing much of the same Free Software on your Mac that you would install in Linux.

A "unified look" isn't so great when apps break basic HID guidelines (iMovie) or refuse to work on common data types (iMovie) or don't present a terribly scalable way to organize your files (iPhoto) or aren't very sophisticated about how it manipulates your files (iPhoto).

A blow by blow "why Apple's UIs are better than GNOME" would be a handy and interesting thing. As an owner of multiple Macs (MythTV boxes), I am highly skeptical of the reputed UI superiority of MacOS.

### Post by Xianath on 2010-05-10
The main problem with OSS in general is the lack of vision, direction, and consensus. The Linux kernel was estimated at $1.3 billion of engineering effort, and a complete distro at about $11 billion. Why, then, does it not have a filesystem like ZFS, virtualization like WPAR, or centralized management like AD? Simple. Without proper product and project management, the talent and work of thousands of gifted people is spread thin. Thankfully, many large vendors have taken lead on some major projects and we're seeing the results already. In time, I hope, they will bring some order so that we, the end users, can finally see a OS that is ready to compete at equal terms in both the desktop and server enterprise markets.

---

Desktop/laptop/netbook has never gotten the kind of funding those other Linux areas have.

It has everything you said.

### Post by Xianath on 2010-05-11
aysiu: This is just the type of thinking that I had in mind. It is a misconception as old as OSS. Eric Raymond put it best in "The Cathedral and the Bazaar" saying something along the lines of, "every great software starts with a programmer's need." It's not important what IT needs, it's what WE (the users) need. Every other appliance in your home gives you what you need, why should the computer be any different? This type of mentality worked great for GNU because its users were all programmers or other types of highly technical computer users, but fails to deliver in the 21st century.

Linux on embedded platforms, notably ucLinux, is indeed a viable OS choice. The problem comes when trying to build a platform around it. This is when the GPL shows its teeth. I've seen my share of embedded products give up on Linux and go *BSD instead, if only for licensing reasons. Technically, a lot is left to be desired when compared to, say, QNX or even VxWorks, but it's getting there -- again, with a lot of help from large vendors.

mickie.kext: The solutions you point out are great examples of how Linux and OSS haven't gotten there yet, and why commercial backing helps tremendously to overcome this.

KVM (RedHat) is not nearly in the same league with zLPAR (WPAR) and Solaris zones in terms of stability, performance and management. In other words, it's not enterprise ready yet. There's a reason why large IT shops run either native virtualization in Solaris/AIX/Win2008, or VMware ESX.

389 Directory Server (RedHat) handles only one aspect of AD and that's single sign-on. This is the very reason it's called 389 as opposed to 3268 directory server. There is no mechanism for policy enforcement, which is where AD shines. Try managing a network of 500 enterprise desktops individually vs. centrally, then you'll see what I mean. Ubuntu have something in the works in the form of Landscape but it will be years before they get on par, if ever.

BTRFS is a project started by Oracle with the help of IBM, after ZFS was introduced by Sun. ZFS was available in 2005 in OpenSolaris, and was stable in 2006 for Solaris 10. In comparison, BTRFS didn't even start development until 2007 and it is still in the 0.xx pre-alpha stages, with its own team NOT recommending it for production use at this stage. Again, demonstrating my point.

Linux is great for situations where budget is either too tight (limited home use, i.e. no serious gaming) or too abundant (enterprises with the IT budget to support the significant management overhead).

---

EDIT:
From wikipedia:
The estimated cost of the Debian codebase is 819million USD. 13 billion USD is for ALL packages included in the repository.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Debian](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Debian)

### Post by Xianath on 2010-05-13
I was going off this Wikipedia article: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux_kernel#Estimated_cost_to_redevelop](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux_kernel#Estimated_cost_to_redevelop) which refers to Fedora 9. In comparison, Solaris 10 was developed over 4 years at a cost of about $500 million. Now, I'm a complete lamer when it comes to finance, but based on historical gold prices, that's probably $1.5 billion in today's money, *for the entire OS.*

The sheer number of packages in Ubuntu (almost 30000 not including multiverse) further emphasizes my point. There are at least half a dozen implementations of every single feature you can think of. Take FTP servers, for example. Is there ONE that supports FTP/SSL, KRB5/LDAP SSO, and scripting? If there is, why are there two dozen more still actively being worked on? If there isn't, why are there two dozen, each of which supports a subset of this functionality? Effort is being wasted because resources are being spread thin, and that's exactly what happens when you leave a lot of technically gifted people run rampart without common goals.

I am all for freedom of choice, and like most of us on these forums, I think variety is a good thing. My problem is that I keep finding myself having to switch software (of which there's a lot) because none of it does 100% of what I need. I'd rather have two that work than two dozen that almost do.

---

### Post by Xianath on 2010-05-13
> Ubuntu doesn't create a whole bunch of FTP server applications. It just takes what's out there and compiles Ubuntu packages to put in the repositories.

I know :) Ubuntu does have its contributions to the OSS world but most of all it's bleeding edge Debian with a twist added stability and commercial support. My argument is not against Ubuntu, it is supporting the OP in that Ubuntu, and OSS in general, can not compete with commercial software unless it has a strong commercial backing. Heck, if it hadn't been for Mark and his financial backing, we wouldn't have had Ubuntu at all! Everything that makes Ubuntu better than Debian, including these very forums, are thanks to his support.

> Do you really think Mark Shuttleworth pays someone to write yet another FTP server application? 

No, he paid a lot of people to make a human-friendly Linux distro. One we wouldn't have had if OSS had been left to its own devices. Many large vendors invest in *specific* implementations of a given functionality. Canonical backs up GNOME, RedHat came up with RHEC, Nokia bought Trolltech for Qt, Novell invented NetworkManager, Oracle owns InnoDB, IBM is a major contributor to Apache HTTPd etc. Without a coordinated effort sponsored and guided by a major player, chances are very slim that an OSS project would ever become mature enough to compete with commercial alternatives. Clearly there is a future where OSS would be viable outside its current niche, or companies wouldn't be investing millions in developing it. That future, however, is yet to come and that, pretty much, is my point.

---

### Post by Shaunie on 2010-05-14
I think the reason Linux is not as main stream as Windows and Mac is down to advertising.

These companies are worth billions and spend millions every year on advertising.

As Linux is free I dont think it has those kinds of resources to compete.

However, 

I have only just installed Ubuntu 10.04 on my Acer travelmate so am very new to Linux.
I have installed a netbook remix a while ago on my daughters eeepc as windows was just to slow.

Bear in mind I am an IT admin so have known about Linux for years just round to trying it out properly.

I have liked about my experience so far with Ubuntu, It just seems to work out of the box no need to mess about with drivers ect ect. I dont think I have ever installed a flavour of Windows without having to search for drivers and tweak stuff.

Yes I have made a few tweaks to my Ubuntu install IE for the Dock and the cube (which I am still struggling to get working but actively searching the www to help before I start asking for help)

Also I have just asked 10 people in my office if they know what Linux and not one of them could answer correctly which surprised me.

I will always have my install of windows but I am liking Ubuntu and starting to use it more for everyday stuff.

After I showed a few people the Ubuntu OS one person did say it looks like a kiddies OS which I suppose it does but for the price I paid for it I think its worth it :D

### Post by OooBuntuRox on 2010-05-19
> **arrow.69 said:**
> I'm gonna get incredibly bashed for posting this, but here it goes.

I've been running various Linux distributions on a bunch of computers since I was 13.  Windows would **** me off, Vista got released, or sometimes I just felt like playing around.  I've been through MEPIS, OpenSuse, Kubuntu, Ubuntu,amongst others.  It eventually became clear to me that Ubuntu was the overall best desktop distribution out there, if only for the tremendous amount of support there is out there for it.

I love Ubuntu.  It's a very capable OS and I love the idea of running a completely free and open operating system.  I've probably run some variation of Ubuntu on and off for the past four years.  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

However, I have to admit that Ubuntu simply can't compete anymore.  Windows 7 is a huge leap for Windows, and OS X has always been user-friendly and functional.  I know that under the hood, Linux is more stable, quicker, more secure than Windows.  But the truth is, to the end-user on the desktop front, the experience you get running Ubuntu doesn't measure up.  The problem isn't a lack of applications of features.  Ubuntu just lacks polish and integration.  Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users.  I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability.  Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer.  They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system.  They want it to just work for them.  Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now.

What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

Gee, for a while there I forgot you were talking about Ubuntu and it sounded just like you were talking about MS-Windows. After using ms for years, paying attention to how frequent the ms-updates occur, paying attention to how often there is a security risk bulliten, a virus warning, another security breach discovered warning, expensive facelift updates, etc, etc... I'll go with Ubuntu.

Of course, a dual boot system can address your problem very well. Ever notice how easily and friendly Ubuntu will resize your partitions making that a possibility? Then there are swappable HD trays. Lets not forget Ubuntu/ Linux live CD's. You don't have to own more than one computer to run more than one operating system these days.

Honestly, I think that one of the biggest attractions to those who use Ubuntu and stay with it is the fact that you can play with it and tweek it to your hearts content. And when you break it or get sick of the way it looks, or simply want a clean install... you DON'T have to spend an hour or more on the phone with tech support convincing them that you are not an evil bootlegging pirate. MS will probably be around for a long time. But I think that is due more to the fact that they (ms) are good marketers and have a lot of deals with computer companies.

Go to any computer web page and what is the most prominent os brand even being offered? Now, tell me how hard you have to dig on that same website to purchase an alternative os on your new computer. Yeah it's probably there somewhere... but you have to really dig to find what you want. How many newbies are gonna do that?!!! Or would even know to? That  is what keeps ms so prominant. And that prominance is what creates the illusions of compatability. If everyone buys and eats cow dung, then that is what your supplier will offer most readily... **Disclaimer**: That is just an example. I am not calling any particular company or product cow dung. One thing I truly admire and appreciate about the leading OS brands is this: They keep a great many people around the world employed and by that fact alone they are doing a lot of good in the world. So, I have a certain respect for all the retail OS companies.

I don't think that using Ubuntu is as much about quality or performance as it is about the mind set of the people who use it. A lot of people simply got tired of the games that other OS brands seem to still be playing. Ten years ago you had to be very tech savvy to even INSTALL Linux. Lack of drivers was a nightmare. You had to go out and buy Linux compatible hardware, then build your computer and hope that you didn't overlook something. Then... MAYBE it would work. What a hassle! Nay, Nay...** by far** is Ubuntu a dream come true.

BTW: Have you noticed the retail price drop for Win7 since Open Source OSes have improved? Now how is it that a superior OS upgrade costs less than the same companies last OS? Can anyone say "mark-up"...

  Sorry if my thoughts are a repeat. I confess, i didn't read through the other posts.

OooBuntuRox :guitar:

### Post by TimelessRogue on 2010-05-19
Aye, as others here have stated, stick with what works for you ... that's the bottom line of any argument of this sort.

However ... having been with Linux (dual booted with Win as backup if necessary) since the early days, I gotta say it just plain keeps getting better with each release.  Currently have Ubuntu on three newer HP laptops (two running Meercat), one ten year old HP laptop and an old Dell desktop of indeterminate age  (all my own personal computers) ... all were up and running flawlessly and full operational with the different hardware configurations.  Half-a-dozen installs on client's computers of various configurations went just a well.  Granted, a few tweakings were necessary in the early days to get stuff working totally right ... like wireless for example.  But I've got complete, fully functional systems in each case that are totally user-friendly and handle all the needs of the folks that I take care of.  Yes, not gamers ... that's another category.  And yes, there are other additional working programs to download/install in some cases ... if there's a need  ...

That's what works for me ...

The latest Win 7 Enterprise installation (just completed a few hours ago) was a quite lengthy process requiring much attention during the install and the various updates ... basically taking up last evening for the initial install and most of this morning (morning starts in the wee hours) for the various and sundry updates (security and otherwise), "outside" software re-installation and migration of data.  All of this from a pricey disc and more than a few hours on-line for the updates/upgrades/security fixes ... not a downloadable install, by the by.  Anything other than the programs that are included comes at yet additional cost, both in dollars and installation time.  Not knocking it ... that's just the way it is.

That's what _doesn't_ work for me ...

And free ... other than the principal of it ... is not the deciding factor, although it does matter.  Ease of usability and functionality for my clients is what really matters.

So ... and this will surely get me flamed ... for some out there commenting about programs or games that aren't totally supported in Linux:  jump into the ring ... join the fray ... give a hand and come up with some ideas to correct these "problems".  Lend a voice to the vendors to begin porting Linux versions of your favorite programs or games.  Let's hear some solutions, not just complaints.

Me?  Obviously I'm satisfied with Linux in general and Ubuntu in particular ...

If you don't mind a bad gui and using inferior alternative software that tries badly to emulate Windows and Apple software then Ubuntu is good for you. If you'd rather use with the real thing then stick with Windows or OSX.

### Post by MarcusW on 2010-05-19
> **Johnsie said:**
> I agree with the OP. There are alot of quality assurance issues with Ubuntu and not enough money being pumped into producing a beautiful interface. Ubuntu is what it is, a cheap (free) OS. When you buy cheap you're never going to get a high quality product with every detail quality assured.

If you don't mind a bad gui and using inferior alternative software that tries badly to emulate Windows and Apple software then Ubuntu is good for you. If you'd rather use with the real thing then stick with Windows or OSX.

For a power user, Linux is hard to beat. Sure, I could probably get my computers to have pretty much the same features they have now, in windows, but I don't want to have to sit for hours just to figure out basic functionality like SSH, RSA authentication, setting up the path, etc. (try starting firefox in windows/OS X from cmd/bash) 

I mean, windows doesn't even have a proper package manager. "Quality assured"? Having to download every single program I want through http? Compare to how you install programs in Ubuntu. Downloaded from their own servers, signed by their key. In other words, guaranteed to be safe, and a certain amount of quality assured. (compared to none)

About the GUI, windows doesn't even include multiple virtual desktops by default. That's something I'd have a hard time using my computer without. Sure, aero beats compiz in looks, but that isn't all that matters.

And I don't want to reboot after every upgrade also. Right now, I can keep my computer safe and up-do-date and it almost never has to rebooted.

### Post by Diluted on 2010-05-19
> **MarcusW said:**
> "Quality assured"? Having to download every single program I want through http? Compare to how you install programs in Ubuntu. Downloaded from their own servers, signed by their key. In other words, guaranteed to be safe, and a certain amount of quality assured. (compared to none)
Or you can check the digital signatures/hashes on the installation file before running it. You'd know it comes from the author directly, it isn't patched and it works just the way the author intended.

Although having a package manager is convenient and works for most of the time, it is still a layer where things can go wrong. Packagers may not [understand the code well enough](http://www.debian.org/security/2008/dsa-1571) or [release packages which perform worse](http://0pointer.de/blog/projects/pa-in-ubuntu.html).

---

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2010-05-19
Package managers may not be perfect, but ask users- they're the ones who think it's a wonderful improvement. It saves time and trouble, in real-world tests. It's one of the best things about Linux today, application management and security.

Johnsie, interface is largely subjective. I think the default Ubuntu Lucid looks much better than Windows 7. That's just because I prefer this kind of theme. The issue with including transparency and the such in Ubuntu is that Ubuntu is made for a large range of computers, some of which don't support compositing as of yet. So including those features would be dangerous and break systems for a lot of users.

However, rounded menus and a few more subtle improvements would help. However, the notifications system, indicators, and Gnome in general is quite nice from a design perspective- it follows Human Guidelines fairly closely.

Use KDE if you're really so upset over the default looks.

Also, would you call IE 8 'the real thing'? Or iTunes? If you've been using open source for a while, and you had to use the proprietary counterpart to your favorite applications, I think you would be disappointed just because it's different.

There are some niche programs that don't have viable equivalents, yes, but not many, and certainly not for an average user. The more into an OS you are, the more you want your 'free cheap OS' to be like that one. If you get so invested in your software, you should dual-boot, and help out. If you don't like Linux, you don't have to use it, you'll just be missing out on a lot of nice features. I value your opinion, and in some points I've brought up, you are very right.

But aside from that, much of this thread is purely opinion. You have to evaluate why you're using Linux, and adjust your perspective of what Linux is and how you can help if it's not the way you want in a certain aspect. No point in saying, "oh, it can't compete because it doesn't have this one program."

### Post by Guitar John on 2010-05-19
Music is a huge part of my life, hence my user name.  

First of all, I cannot imagine a situation that would bring me back to Windows.  Ever.

While I find some of the music and multi-media apps in Apple very tempting, I cannot bring myself to switch.  For one, the computers themselves are just so [expensive]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=9321095#post9321095").  Then there is the iPod.  I have never read the 79 page [EULA]("http://images.apple.com/legal/sla/docs/itunes.pdf") for iTunes but from what I have been told, you never actually own the music that you purchase.  In other words, you are only renting it.  On the other hand, I have all of my music (ogg) on my computer, my wife's, as well as both of our [music players]("http://www.newegg.com/Product/Product.aspx?Item=N82E16855125067&cm_re=sansa_fuze-_-55-125-067-_-Product").

Finally, a friend of mine switched to Mac a few years ago.  All I ever heard after that was:

"Get a Mac"
"If you had a Mac, you could....."
"If you can actually get your PC to start, you can...."

The almost cult-like zealotry became a bit of a turn-off, although that was what ultimately led me to find an alternative which was Ubuntu.

### Post by OooBuntuRox on 2010-05-19
[SIZE=2]> **Johnsie said:**
> ...If you don't mind a bad gui and using inferior alternative software that tries badly to emulate Windows and Apple software then Ubuntu is good for you. If you'd rather use with the real thing then stick with Windows or OSX.[/SIZE] [SIZE=2]

I am really starting to chuckle from some of the posts I am reading. I also find it a little humorous that hard-core windows users are even  members of a Ubuntu support forum. Just a little FYI history lesson:[/SIZE] [SIZE=2]

At&T basically created Unix (see the Wiki below). Since then, In general, GUI OSes are a knock off of Unix. Linux is based on Unix. Microsoft and IBM worked together to create OS/2, then Microsoft branched off from IBM and created Windows (rather poorly I might add). OS/2 was superior to Windows. If it were supported and compatible with windows apps, that would have been the OS to stick with... and worth paying for too. I forget where, but I believe that Xerox was involved with the development of Unix or it's GUI in the beginning too. And mac Os/x... well thats basically got unix under the hood. That is why it runs so solid.[/SIZE] [SIZE=2]

So, all (or at least most) Oses today are a knock off of Unix. ...and windows started as a poor knock off of OS/2 by IBM. The reason that The Unix/ Linux GUI isn't so polished is because Unix began as a command line os and was created pretty much for servers... so who cared how fancy the graphical interface was... or if it was a gaming machine. You got the system up and running and didn't look at it very often. Keep in mind, there was once a thing called terminals that did not boot and run independently.... they basically ran software off of the "mainframe" server.


Heres the wiki:[/SIZE]   [SIZE=2]

**Unix**[/SIZE]  [SIZE=2] (officially trademarked as **UNIX**, sometimes also  written as Unix with [small  caps]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Small_caps")) is a computer [operating system]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Operating_system") originally developed in 1969 by a group of [AT&T]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Telephone_%26_Telegraph") employees at [Bell  Labs]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bell_Labs"), including [Ken  Thompson]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ken_Thompson_%28computer_programmer%29"), [Dennis Ritchie]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dennis_Ritchie"), [Brian Kernighan]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brian_Kernighan"), [Douglas McIlroy]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Douglas_McIlroy"), and [Joe  Ossanna]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joe_Ossanna"). Today's Unix systems are split into various branches,  developed over time by AT&T as well as various commercial vendors  and [non-profit]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-profit") organizations.
[The Open Group]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Open_Group")[/SIZE]  [SIZE=2], an industry standards consortium, owns the  &#8220;Unix&#8221; trademark. Only systems fully compliant with and certified  according to the [Single UNIX Specification]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single_UNIX_Specification") are  qualified to use the trademark; others may be called "Unix system-like"  or "[Unix-like]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix-like")"  (though the Open Group disapproves of this term). However, the term  "Unix" is often used informally to denote any operating system that  closely resembles the trademarked system.
 During the late 1970s and early 1980s, the influence of Unix in  academic circles led to large-scale adoption of Unix (particularly of  the [BSD]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkeley_Software_Distribution") variant, originating from  the [University of California,  Berkeley]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley")) by commercial startups, the most notable of which are [Solaris]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solaris_Operating_System"), [HP-UX]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HP-UX") and [AIX]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AIX_operating_system"). Today, in addition to  certified Unix systems such as those already mentioned, [Unix-like]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix-like")  operating systems such as [Linux]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux") and BSD descendants ([FreeBSD]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FreeBSD"),  [NetBSD]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NetBSD"),  and [OpenBSD]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OpenBSD"))  are commonly encountered. The term "traditional Unix" may be used to  describe a Unix or an operating system that has the characteristics of  either [Version 7 Unix]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Version_7_Unix") or [UNIX  System V]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UNIX_System_V").

[/SIZE]**OS/2** is a computer [operating system]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Operating_system"), initially created by [Microsoft]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft")  and [IBM]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM"),  then later developed by IBM exclusively. The name stands for "Operating  System/2," because it was introduced as part of the same generation  change release as IBM's "[Personal System/2]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM_Personal_System/2") (PS/2)" line of  second-generation [personal computers]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Personal_computer"). OS/2 is no longer marketed by IBM, and  IBM standard support for OS/2 was discontinued on 31 December 2006.[[1]]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OS/2#cite_note-0")  Currently, [Serenity  Systems]("http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Serenity_Systems&action=edit&redlink=1") sells OS/2 under the brand name [eComStation]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EComStation").
 OS/2 was intended as a [protected mode]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protected_mode") successor of [PC-DOS]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PC-DOS"). Notably, basic [system  calls]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/System_call") were modeled after [MS-DOS]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MS-DOS")  calls; their names even started with "Dos" and it was possible to create  "Family Mode" applications: [text  mode]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Text_mode") applications that could work on both systems.[[2]]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OS/2#cite_note-1")  Because of this heritage, OS/2 shares similarities with [Unix]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix"), [Xenix]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xenix"), and [Windows]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft_Windows") in many ways.


[SIZE=2]
Whatever you prefer... Enjoy the debate. I'm off to try and learn more about Ubuntu in other parts of the forum.

OooBuntuRox :guitar:

[/SIZE]

Keep in mind, there was once a thing called terminals that did not boot and run independently.... they basically ran software off of the "mainframe" server.


Heres the wiki:[/SIZE]   [SIZE=2]

**Unix**[/SIZE]  [SIZE=2] (officially trademarked as **UNIX**, sometimes also  written as Unix with [small  caps]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Small_caps")) is a computer [operating system]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Operating_system") originally developed in 1969 by a group of [AT&T]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Telephone_%26_Telegraph") employees at [Bell  Labs]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bell_Labs"), including [Ken  Thompson]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ken_Thompson_%28computer_programmer%29"), [Dennis Ritchie]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dennis_Ritchie"), [Brian Kernighan]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brian_Kernighan"), [Douglas McIlroy]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Douglas_McIlroy"), and [Joe  Ossanna]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joe_Ossanna"). Today's Unix systems are split into various branches,  developed over time by AT&T as well as various commercial vendors  and [non-profit]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-profit") organizations.
[The Open Group]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Open_Group")[/SIZE]  [SIZE=2], an industry standards consortium, owns the  &#8220;Unix&#8221; trademark. Only systems fully compliant with and certified  according to the [Single UNIX Specification]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single_UNIX_Specification") are  qualified to use the trademark; others may be called "Unix system-like"  or "[Unix-like]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix-like")"  (though the Open Group disapproves of this term). However, the term  "Unix" is often used informally to denote any operating system that  closely resembles the trademarked system.
 During the late 1970s and early 1980s, the influence of Unix in  academic circles led to large-scale adoption of Unix (particularly of  the [BSD]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkeley_Software_Distribution") variant, originating from  the [University of California,  Berkeley]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley")) by commercial startups, the most notable of which are [Solaris]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solaris_Operating_System"), [HP-UX]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HP-UX") and [AIX]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AIX_operating_system"). Today, in addition to  certified Unix systems such as those already mentioned, [Unix-like]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix-like")  operating systems such as [Linux]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux") and BSD descendants ([FreeBSD]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FreeBSD"),  [NetBSD]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NetBSD"),  and [OpenBSD]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OpenBSD"))  are commonly encountered. The term "traditional Unix" may be used to  describe a Unix or an operating system that has the characteristics of  either [Version 7 Unix]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Version_7_Unix") or [UNIX  System V]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UNIX_System_V").

[/SIZE]**OS/2** is a computer [operating system]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Operating_system"), initially created by [Microsoft]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft")  and [IBM]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM"),  then later developed by IBM exclusively. The name stands for "Operating  System/2," because it was introduced as part of the same generation  change release as IBM's "[Personal System/2]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM_Personal_System/2") (PS/2)" line of  second-generation [personal computers]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Personal_computer"). OS/2 is no longer marketed by IBM, and  IBM standard support for OS/2 was discontinued on 31 December 2006.[[1]]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OS/2#cite_note-0")  Currently, [Serenity  Systems]("http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Serenity_Systems&action=edit&redlink=1") sells OS/2 under the brand name [eComStation]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EComStation").
 OS/2 was intended as a [protected mode]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protected_mode") successor of [PC-DOS]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PC-DOS"). Notably, basic [system  calls]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/System_call") were modeled after [MS-DOS]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MS-DOS")  calls; their names even started with "Dos" and it was possible to create  "Family Mode" applications: [text  mode]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Text_mode") applications that could work on both systems.[[2]]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OS/2#cite_note-1")  Because of this heritage, OS/2 shares similarities with [Unix]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix"), [Xenix]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xenix"), and [Windows]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft_Windows") in many ways.


[SIZE=2]
Whatever you prefer... Enjoy the debate. I'm off to try and learn more about Ubuntu in other parts of the forum.

OooBuntuRox :guitar:

[/SIZE]
This, my friends, is a case of "just because you quoted something doesn't mean your even close to accurate or correct." Have fun.

### Post by Axolotl9250 on 2010-05-20
I'm a Biology student and found that most of the programs I use for ecology or hypothesis testing are actually better and more 'at home' in Linux and feel a lot more natural, being developed by GNU. 

In my experience there are 3 stereotypes of student OS users. 1.) Users who are willing to use Linux once on recommendation of a few teachers on are department, find there's no windows tax that the open document format is less confusing than the new office, the science programs work well and can be customized, there's no viruses and nothing to pay (always a bonus to the student) for software. All it requires is a little bit of computer know how and the choice of a more friendly system to get started - and as Science students logic and working things out is part of the package. Then there are Windows users who don't really bother with stats or hypothesis testing, and aren't even science students, they bought a laptop before coming to uni to research and write up work on and just left it as is. Then there are the students who didn't need a loan and their parents pay for everything, all of those students have macs both the iMac and the laptop in their tiny student dorm, and insist on bugging the IT dept 24/7 saying their coursework got garbled up when putting it on Windows to print. That's how it is on my campus anyway.

So I know Windows and Mac have this student focus thing going on, but practically, for students that 'work' on their computers, like stats, science, IT students and do more than just document processing and research, Linux is cheaper and better.

### Post by antenna on 2010-05-20
I dunno.. I installed Windows 7 a few days ago on my girlfriends computer and although I was hoping to be impressed, on initial impressions I am actually rather disappointed.  

People talk about the polish of the interface and such, but I just don't think it comes close to being as refined, pretty, and consistent as my Gnome desktop.

In Win7 if I add a gadget to my desktop, the interface elements for controlling it (close, drag) bear little resemblance to the rest of the general interface.  Some dialogs will use progress bars, some will display images (file transfer), some will use both.  Many programs (witness Windows Live) will use their own looking window controls (why is this even allowed? Never seen this on Linux).  Earlier a disk needed to be checked before windows would start, and the UI looked completely different (XP era) from anything else I had seen previously.  Office of course looks like nothing else.  And these are MS apps... - use software from other vendors and it's likely to get even worse as the typical Windows app will completely disregard system icons, implement their own custom widgets everywhere and draw their own window controls (see Steam).  This stuff is just the beginning anyway, I could go on for hours about the UI issues (and this from a Linux user, ha).        

Also, configuration options are essentially all over the place.  The relative simplicity of my System menu in Gnome is a beautiful thing.

No virtual desktops yet either?  In 2010. 

I have to use Win7 for a fair while longer before I can get a complete understanding of it, but so far I just find it messy and unprofessional.

### Post by Objekt on 2010-05-20
Wow, this thread is still going?

It's news to me that Ubuntu is supposed to "compete" with commercial OSes.  I don't see it that way - for me, it's a replacement, a different and - usually - better way of doing things, at an unbeatable price.

It happened gradually, but I've moved to Ubuntu 9.10 as my "everyday" OS.  Ubuntu is where I do all my "serious" business.  I still browse the Web on occasion when running Windows XP, but only to save the trouble of rebooting.  Mostly, I run a few games in Windows XP because that's the only way they'll work.  

Linux compatibility now influences my hardware purchases, the way I used to select devices based partly on the quality of their Windows support software (or lack of).  It's one reason I bought a Brother MFC-7440N, for instance, rather than just any old laser multifunction.

Ubuntu is really damned good for the price, really good even not considering the price.  Without it, I would have no choice but to keep feeding the Microsoft machine.  Windows 7 doesn't do anything I can't live without, so live without it is exactly what I do.

### Post by GeoffKing on 2010-05-20
I started with DOS 2.1 and worked through to Vista.   I hated Vista and bought an Eee PC with Xandros.  It worked and is still working well, though it is limited.   Then tried lots of linux versions and ended up with Ubuntu 9.10.  I run this on 5 machines all internet connected.   I run 6 XP machines, none are connected to the internet, and 3 Win 2K machines not connected to the internet.  The Windows machines work with printers, scanners etc, and the Ubuntu machines act as a screen between the internet and Windows.  This works well, no virus problems so far, and no Windows patches to slow up the OS.   I have not yet tried 7 as I assumed it was a re-sprayed version of Vista.   
Printer, scanner etc drivers seem to be a problem with Linux.

### Post by Rususeruru on 2010-05-20
The OP's points are valid.  Linux is a work in progress and compatibility often results from reverse engineering where Windows especially has the ability to work with OEMs to design compatible components/specifications.  The Mac OS is a totally different ballgame since both hardware and the OS are designed by one company.

I see some people talking about Windows versions being similar to fragmentation in Linux and this is not the case.  Only 3 Windows versions are available at retail (Home Premium, Professional, and Ultimate) with another (Starter) reserved for corporations building low spec'd machines and the last of the non-server OS's (Enterprise) reserved for large organization's looking for volume licensing.

Compare these three versions to the multitude of Linux OS's.  In Windows and Mac OS there is always going to be a familiar interface and a standard method for acquiring new software, and the heritage for both Win/Mac are legacy desktop systems with large volumes of known software.

On Linux one may run not only a different distro but a different package manager and/or desktop environment among tons of other nuances and potential hardware incompatibilities and your average Joe Blow won't know where to turn for support.

Don't get me wrong choices are good but occasionally there need to be standards.  I don't know why Ubuntu/Debian use .deb files for installation and Fedora/RH/Suse use .rpm (this is just an example) or when I want to install something as simple as Google earth I have to either configure a new repo or execute a shell command to install from a binary.

Until standards emerge, no, Linux won't be a serious threat to the Win/Mac desktop platforms.  Initiatives like Android are promising and show that Linux can compete, but it will take a large distro (like Ubuntu) pairing with not only a large and well known computer manufacturer and then a massive advocacy/advertising campaign for Linux to truly get mainstream but no large pc manufacturer will be willing to break it's relationship with Microsoft.

... Why do I use Linux? I think the idea is good, and it runs better on my machines (All 1.6GHz or less) than Windows/Mac.  I'm also a CS student so this is good practice and learning material.
