### Post by Redache on 2010-05-22
> **OooBuntuRox said:**
> [SIZE=2][/SIZE] [SIZE=2]

I am really starting to chuckle from some of the posts I am reading. I also find it a little humorous that hard-core windows users are even  members of a Ubuntu support forum. Just a little FYI history lesson:[/SIZE] [SIZE=2]

At&T basically created Unix (see the Wiki below). Since then, In general, GUI OSes are a knock off of Unix. Linux is based on Unix. Microsoft and IBM worked together to create OS/2, then Microsoft branched off from IBM and created Windows (rather poorly I might add). OS/2 was superior to Windows. If it were supported and compatible with windows apps, that would have been the OS to stick with... and worth paying for too. I forget where, but I believe that Xerox was involved with the development of Unix or it's GUI in the beginning too. And mac Os/x... well thats basically got unix under the hood. That is why it runs so solid.[/SIZE] [SIZE=2]

So, all (or at least most) Oses today are a knock off of Unix. ...and windows started as a poor knock off of OS/2 by IBM. The reason that The Unix/ Linux GUI isn't so polished is because Unix began as a command line os and was created pretty much for servers... so who cared how fancy the graphical interface was... or if it was a gaming machine. You got the system up and running and didn't look at it very often. Keep in mind, there was once a thing called terminals that did not boot and run independently.... they basically ran software off of the "mainframe" server.


Heres the wiki:[/SIZE]   [SIZE=2]

**Unix**[/SIZE]  [SIZE=2] (officially trademarked as **UNIX**, sometimes also  written as Unix with [small  caps]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Small_caps")) is a computer [operating system]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Operating_system") originally developed in 1969 by a group of [AT&T]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Telephone_%26_Telegraph") employees at [Bell  Labs]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bell_Labs"), including [Ken  Thompson]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ken_Thompson_%28computer_programmer%29"), [Dennis Ritchie]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dennis_Ritchie"), [Brian Kernighan]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brian_Kernighan"), [Douglas McIlroy]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Douglas_McIlroy"), and [Joe  Ossanna]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joe_Ossanna"). Today's Unix systems are split into various branches,  developed over time by AT&T as well as various commercial vendors  and [non-profit]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-profit") organizations.
[The Open Group]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Open_Group")[/SIZE]  [SIZE=2], an industry standards consortium, owns the  “Unix” trademark. Only systems fully compliant with and certified  according to the [Single UNIX Specification]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single_UNIX_Specification") are  qualified to use the trademark; others may be called "Unix system-like"  or "[Unix-like]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix-like")"  (though the Open Group disapproves of this term). However, the term  "Unix" is often used informally to denote any operating system that  closely resembles the trademarked system.
 During the late 1970s and early 1980s, the influence of Unix in  academic circles led to large-scale adoption of Unix (particularly of  the [BSD]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Berkeley_Software_Distribution") variant, originating from  the [University of California,  Berkeley]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley")) by commercial startups, the most notable of which are [Solaris]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solaris_Operating_System"), [HP-UX]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HP-UX") and [AIX]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AIX_operating_system"). Today, in addition to  certified Unix systems such as those already mentioned, [Unix-like]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix-like")  operating systems such as [Linux]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux") and BSD descendants ([FreeBSD]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FreeBSD"),  [NetBSD]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NetBSD"),  and [OpenBSD]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OpenBSD"))  are commonly encountered. The term "traditional Unix" may be used to  describe a Unix or an operating system that has the characteristics of  either [Version 7 Unix]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Version_7_Unix") or [UNIX  System V]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UNIX_System_V").

[/SIZE]**OS/2** is a computer [operating system]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Operating_system"), initially created by [Microsoft]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft")  and [IBM]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM"),  then later developed by IBM exclusively. The name stands for "Operating  System/2," because it was introduced as part of the same generation  change release as IBM's "[Personal System/2]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IBM_Personal_System/2") (PS/2)" line of  second-generation [personal computers]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Personal_computer"). OS/2 is no longer marketed by IBM, and  IBM standard support for OS/2 was discontinued on 31 December 2006.[[1]]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OS/2#cite_note-0")  Currently, [Serenity  Systems]("http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Serenity_Systems&action=edit&redlink=1") sells OS/2 under the brand name [eComStation]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EComStation").
 OS/2 was intended as a [protected mode]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protected_mode") successor of [PC-DOS]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PC-DOS"). Notably, basic [system  calls]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/System_call") were modeled after [MS-DOS]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MS-DOS")  calls; their names even started with "Dos" and it was possible to create  "Family Mode" applications: [text  mode]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Text_mode") applications that could work on both systems.[[2]]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OS/2#cite_note-1")  Because of this heritage, OS/2 shares similarities with [Unix]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unix"), [Xenix]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xenix"), and [Windows]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft_Windows") in many ways.

Whatever you prefer... Enjoy the debate. I'm off to try and learn more about Ubuntu in other parts of the forum.

OooBuntuRox :guitar:

This made me laugh so hard, it's just. Oh my god.

To correct:

Xerox created the worlds first "GUI" of sorts in their PARC research centre. Apple paid money to go around the research centre and were allowed to take any ideas/projects from there and use it for their own needs. Apple then took the GUI concept and applied it to their computers(Lisa 2 if I'm not mistaken).

OS/2 Was an OS that was created jointly between IBM and Microsoft yes, But windows was NOT stolen from it, there is absolutely no evidence of this.

Unix was in no way the first OS to have a GUI. 

MS-DOS was based on Dr DOS, a company Microsoft bought. 

Windows itself is barely POSIX compliant, Microsoft stole very little from Unix as far as I know, and I'd support this theory with how bad Windows at its worse can really be.

You need to READ what is written before you start quoting sources and accusing everyone of stealing UNIX code.

Get your facts straight.

[SIZE=3]For the record: I do not feel or mean to imply that any companies have stolen or are stealing Unix (or other) code. Although I do believe that many companies in many industries borrow concepts from their competition. It seems to be the very nature of staying competitive.

It just seems that at this point in the game, multiple companies and sources are the reason that many OSes are where they are today. Not a single company. As time goes on, innovators sometimes switch teams as do the players.

### Post by gerowen on 2010-05-23
The problem Linux has, is that there are too many choices.  When a company looks for a desktop OS, they want them all to have the same general user interface, so they can train them once and not have to worry about it.  They want a solid, reliable, and consistent desktop experience.  That's why many of them choose Windows.  You can do some minor things to customize it, but in the end anybody can sit down at any Windows PC and find what they're looking for.  In Linux however, when integrating a Linux solution, people experience choice overload.  Do you want Ubuntu, Mandriva, Slackware, Debian, Fedora, Redhat, Mint, etc.?  Once you choose an OS what desktop environment do you want?  Do you want Gnome, KDE, Fluxbox, XFCE, etc.?  In Windows and MAC OSX, it's just Windows.  No matter what you do to it, no Windows box will look radically different than any other windows box.  You can deploy software to one windows box and it will run on all the other windows boxes in the same way.  In Linux, the platform you use to distribute your software depends on the OS, Redhat-ish(RPMs), Debian-ish(DEBs), or raw source?

That's why, as I've said, it's up to as as Linux devs/admins to suggest, implment, and support Linux options for companies.  We need to choose an operating system and desktop environment that we are experts in, implement those choices, and then support the customer afterward so they don't have to deal with the minor issues that come from deploying one operating system across different hardware platforms.

Personally, I love 10.04, it displays a level of polish that was unheard of in previous releases of Ubuntu.  The integrated icons in the system tray for chat/mail, etc. are nice, and I have yet to experience a crash of any kind.

---

### Post by aysiu on 2010-05-23
> **marcusdean.adams said:**
> The problem Linux has, is that there are too many choices.  When a company looks for a desktop OS, they want them all to have the same general user interface, so they can train them once and not have to worry about it.  They want a solid, reliable, and consistent desktop experience. That reasoning doesn't hold up. Sorry.

No company is going to say "We're switching to Linux. It's up to each employee to pick her own Linux distro in the next week. Go!" If a company were to switch to Linux, its IT department in consultation with upper management would decide what Linux distro and desktop environment to go with and then deploy it to all the employees. Then all the employees would have a consistent desktop experience.

If what you're describing as a barrier to entry were legitimate, no one would use Linux for servers (including the London stock exchange and Google) because they'd just be too confused about what distro to use. Ridiculous. You pick one and use it.

So no one eats at restaurants because there are too many choices? No one buys cars because there are too many choices? No one rents apartments because there are too many choices? No one goes to university because there are too many choices? Choices aren't bad. Uninformed choices are bad. Lack of useful information about choices is bad.

Using your reasoning, you could have said Linux can't compete on smartphones because no one could choose what distro to use. Most consumers do not pick an operating system. They pick a device. Google designed a new Linux-based operating system called Android and decided to sell it to handset makers, and those smartphones took off, because people didn't have to pick a Linux distribution. Google and HTC and the wireless carriers picked it for them.

If there are a lot of different types of engines, I wouldn't know. If I were to buy a car, I'd just buy a car, and the engine I'd end up using is the one that came with the car.

### Post by leeds_shrew on 2010-05-23

Indeed.  I want it to look and run how *I* want it to run - it may not be to everyone's taste. With Ubuntu this is easy. I haven't looked back - If only I'd have discovered it when I was 15 instead of 25!

I just had a fresh install of Lynx on my home PC a week ago. And all this week I spend all days long tuning it to my needs because it don't work. 
Plymouth confivcts with Nvidia. Gnome conflicts with floppy drive. Video player crashes without errors in log. CUPS has only ancient proprietary drivers for my popular printer, while new ones opensource are available. Mouse pointer jerks, and I can't switch its picture. Emerald don't edit themes. Firefox forgets its settings due to bug in Ubuntu extras. Alakarte can scramble your menu so much that you have to reinstall it all anew (a four-year -old bug). Default pulseaudio settings are lcausing jerks in sound, have to reconfigure. Official guide on turning off avahi don't turn it off. I'd like to remove it whole, but it is in dependencies of wine. Netbeans package in repository is three years old. Rhythmbox crashes on playing mp3. Nework manager has no way to start VPN on login, had to tinker with it's source directly. 
These are at most a half of bugs I had to tinker with on the last week. What sane person would call it user-friendly? It is breaking apart if you try to use it.
I am preparing an open petition to ubuntu developers. The text in short is "Please, make the next release of ubuntu without any one new feature. Fix the existing crap at last".

If you go to the Netbeans site you'll see that the latest available version is 6.8. Enter apt-cache show netbeans into a terminal in ubuntu 10.04 and what does it say? LO AND BEHOLD!:
```
thumbnail@waggaruffin:/var/www/thumbnail$ apt-cache show netbeans
Package: netbeans
Priority: optional
Section: universe/devel
Installed-Size: 3252
Maintainer: Ubuntu Developers <ubuntu-devel-discuss@lists.ubuntu.com>

```

Wanna turn avahi off? Try this:
```
sudo /etc/init.d/avahi-daemon stop
```

Using emerald to edit window decor themes? But compiz is compatible with metacity themes and uses metacity by default. Did you check to ensure that you'd configured compiz to actually use emerald for its window decoration?

Pulseaudio has worked beautifully for me out of the box so I couldn't really help you there.

Neither have had any issues with changing the mouse pointer image (although to be honest I really don't see the point.)

I also don't really use any VPN's either (I ssh tunnel into work and do everything via a screen session hosted on my desktop machine there).

Hope some of that helps!

### Post by hatebreed on 2010-06-06
I've been using linux again for the past month and have to say I'm very happy with how good it is now. It isn't perfec by any stretch, still have issues with some video cards and some sound issues with older systems. But over all I would have to say it's way better than when I started using it back in 98. The installers are far more streamlined, the x server works far better, the hardware detection is leaps and bounds better. Having said that, it has a long ways to go before it can compete on a level with windows. Why you ask? Well windows just works out of the box. Even if you have a really obscure piece of hardware, there will be a driver out there for it. There is alot of software for linux but I would have to say that there is more for windows. Not all windows software costs money either, I haven't paid for software on windows for years, download.com is a great resource. But the biggest gripe I have is gaming and that will never change because there are just to many windows users out there and it wouldn't be cost effective for a software company to make their game on linux. But I prefer Linux more and more every day, it's bringing me back to my roots of tinkering with computers and I love it. Ubuntu is the best and closest thing that linux has to windows, ease of use is what's going to bring more people to linux. There are way to many different distros out there that really aren't that much different than the one there based on. We need to start focusing on the main branches and perfect them to get linux on top. For the people by the people.

### Post by GSF1200S on 2010-06-09
> **lancest said:**
> Try doing a Windows install with no drivers and then having to hunt for them all over the internet, vs an Ubuntu install with all drivers included - which happens alot these days.  
(Been there done that)

Ubuntu has been FAR EASIER to install and configure for me since about 2006 than Windows. Linux has great hardware support these days.

Not to mention Windows comes with nothing and is full of security hassles.

+1

This is what really angers me reading these types of threads (no offense- allow me to explain): people do not consider the situation. Microsoft used an initially good idea (Windows WAS good back in the day) and created a nice chunk of capital (money). They then ended up with hegemonic control over the hardware companies, forcing them not only to ship Windows on their machines, but also to write drivers for the hardware. This hegemony is what results in the driver discs you get. The Linux folks have to build in support for all these devices by often times creating a driver from scratch and incorporating them into the kernel tree.

You have been told since you were born that as a customer you are to be served and catered to, and you expect it without realizing the shady crap that leads a company like Microsoft to the front of the pack. Linux has little capital, little advertising, and is largely maintained by passionate people in their spare time. Despite this, Linux has technologies that dont even exist in Windows, and it is certainly far more stable (if you think not, give it time). I dont blame you nor am I chastising you for saying such things, but understand you are using a product that is technically superior in many ways yet is struggling to compete in a market where $ is all that matters.

I also would like to make it known at this point that any new release of Ubuntu is essentially based off of Debian Unstable (Sid). For this reason, bugs are going to exist at times. Another poster early in this thread made a very good suggestion- utilize a distro that is more conservative and throw a nice theme in there. Some unstable distros even seem to be very bug free at least to me: Arch, Gentoo, and even Sidux.

Gentoo, the big PITA that weve all likely heard of, actually has a very unique and awesome method of handling packages. Gentoo stable is a little more conservative than Ubuntu (with the exception of kernel's where it is very close), and unstable packages are masked. If you want the latest Rhythmbox or the latest kernel (or whatever you want), you simply unmask that package and emerge (apt-get install for gentoo basically) it. In this sense, you have a rolling release stable distro with only programs you choose to be rolling on their unstable branch. Technically I think this is not recommended, but if you are willing to deal with issues (portage mainly) occassionally, than it isnt really a problem (hasnt been for me).

Ubuntu is a fine distro, and I will always keep a copy around if for no other reason than loyalty. But there are other approaches to Linux, from complete solutions (Fedora, OpenSUSE, Mepis, Sidux, Debian, etc) to solutions based on minimalism (Arch, Debian (yes its both), Gentoo, Slackware, etc).

### Post by BobSongs on 2010-06-09
Having re-read the original post, I wanted to say a couple things (this thread has many posts and mebbe this is just a repeat...)

1. My *neighbour* bought a new HP system complete with printer. I installed Karmic after convincing her that a** [COLOR=DarkSlateGray]dual boot[/COLOR] **would be wise to help her keep Vista bug-free longer (web surfing can have adverse effects on Windows). She attempted an Ubuntu upgrade that messed up basic functions (can't connect to the net). Her response? [COLOR=#333333]**"I want my Ubuntu back!!"**[/COLOR]

**Note:** She's a first-time user who, with **very little coaching**, was able to navigate effectively thru menus and applications. She liked how the Applications menu showed topics instead of Corporate Names (Symantec, Adobe, etc.). She loved the way the printer was installed in the system simply because we switched it on.

2. I once ran across a webpage that showed Vista's **inconsistent** error messages. Inconsistent in size, shape, text, embedded icons and so forth. Users just **love** to read how Windows as performed an **illegal** operation (will the FBI show up???)

**Try this** -- have a new PC user use Ubuntu for a year or so before introducing him/her to Windows. Vista's/Win7 new Start menu alone is frustrating enough to have them rush back to Linux.

**Conclusion:** The grass is always greener on the other side. **Windows** does some things [COLOR=Red]**well**[/COLOR]. Windows plays *Unreal Tournament* well. But after a few months the Windows **Registry** will degrade. But the Registry isn't "Interface". Yet such things make the Windows experience fail over time. **Ubuntu** does some things [COLOR=Red]**well**[/COLOR]. A steady and reliable 6-month upgrade schedule is truly unbeatable. Irritating inconsistencies in Ubuntu are regularly being squashed.

P.S. I've used Windows since 1991, starting with 3.0. I dabbled with a number of Linux Distributions since hearing about Linux in 1995. I dual-booted **XP/Breezy Badger** happily, upgrading regularly. For long stretches I have not used Windows (being very satisfied with Linux). May Linux continue doing what it does well.

### Post by OCEAN11 on 2010-06-13
Interesting thread!!

Ive been using Ubuntu for two years now, and I am mostly using Ubuntu 100 percent of the time at Home, but still forced to use Windows at work.

At work, I am forced to use Windows, for I use CADD / CAM systems that are only used and supported in Windows only, these programs are used by engineers, machinists, and designers across the globe, but minimal or no support unless you want free and totally basic software....

So many people have expressed their feeling on this, and software names as PTC Pro-Engineer Wildfire stopped any linux support two years ago, were talking about companies paying over 20 thousand dollars for some special cadd software and not supporting Linux, as well as CAM software titles like Mastercam and Surfcam, there is no support, and probably never will be.  

Another element that comes to mind is Gaming, I know too many know about this one, and if anyone was looking for Unreal Tournament 3 in Linux, they are never going to get it ported for Linux, same as so many other games people want to play in Ubuntu, unless you spend hours and hours and in support forums to look for help on custom installers and scripts, etc.

Another key part to my post here is that 6 months ago, a friend had received an e-mail from someone he did not know, he opened it and it was a .exe file that copied all of his contacts, and opened a window where it was an attack / virus / malware site, where 15 people had their computers totally screwed up, when I had received his e-mail, I opened up the e-mail and my screen went red, telling me this was a known attack site, and prevented any attack from happening, and was the only one in the group of e-mails that was not infected, thanks again to Ubuntu....
I don't know about everyone's Virus software they were using, you think this should have been prevented, but I'm not kidding, I was the only one that did not get infected, another big reason I like using Ubuntu.

I think this is exactly where the Original Poster was talking about, I for one, love using Ubuntu, I can do everything that a windows os can offer, BUT when this extends beyond the scope of Ubuntu, this is the part that's holding Ubuntu from totally being a dominate OS, I have had to learn so much from switching over to Ubuntu, and are still learning :lolflag:, but anyone who's been using Ubuntu for the past several years, you know what I'm talking about, the learning curve is way more complex than windows, but given that, as I stated, I am using Ubuntu 100 percent of the time at home, I think Linux & Ubuntu have succeeded in this with no doubt, it's getting all the software companies to include native support.

Sorry for the long post, appreciate everyones continued support and loyalty to Ubuntu!

---

### Post by needhelppeeps on 2010-06-15
Ubuntu annoyances.

I just reinstalled with 10.04 and here are my pros and cons:

Pros
Ubuntu is faster on my old machine than Windows 7 is on a newer machine lol

Most of the software I need is available in repositories which is presumably a good deal safer than downloading binaries from random websites. Usually it is available in source and I can compile it myself when I need extra software.

There are very, very few things I cannot do on my ubuntu machine. All the things I want to do are supported by some kind of software package.

Ubuntu requires a password for elevating privileges. I see a lot of windows 7 users who just click a continue button every time they are prompted and never bother to think about what it is.. having to type in a password makes someone think, "this must be a big security issue if I have to type in a password"

Cons

Wireless driver was not installed until I ran an update and sound needed custom tweak codes which were not easy to find. Of course, if I bought a machine that Ubuntu was known to work with this would not have been an issue. Overall, Ubuntu has great hardware support, though and the community provided answers for my problems, I just had to find them.

The package system doesn't always clean up after itself very well and leaves things installed that I do not need. Of course, some windows packages do the same thing with library files and such... but the log file format made it difficult to manually remove dependencies I know I wasn't using and didn't get cleaned up... basically I had to retype like 50 packages which took forever!

---

### Post by aviedw on 2010-06-16
Truthfully now that I'm a full Ubuntu/Linux user I'm hardly impressed with what MS or OSX come up with. They will always make things nicely packaged so you don't have to think, for the average user but when i go into a best buy or a micro center i sometimes laugh at how people are so set on the idea that what they see here is the only thing that's available. Like I've said before competition exist mainly because of the profit motive and not so much for the innovation of the user experience.

### Post by snowdrift on 2010-06-17
As a long-time Windows user, currently using 7 Ultimate x64, I consider one of Ubuntu's best offerings to be the onslaught of free and/or libre software it has heralded. Most everything I use on my various Windows machines is libre... I use OpenVPN, OpenSSH, OpenSSL, Thunderbird, Money Manager Ex (MMEX), RSSOwl, foobar2000, XnView, VLC, Pidgin, Notepad++, FileZilla, Firefox, TrueCrypt/Diskcryptor, Privoxy, UK's Kalender, 7Zip, etc.

Ubuntu 10 looks good out of the box but I seem always to fall back to Windows for certain other proprietary programs, MS Office, InDesign, Acrobat, The Master Genealogist, RoboForm, etc.

In the end, I don't care what people as long as they do it safely, which I do as a standard user with things like DEP and SEHOP enabled for all processes, a good firewall, a sound router/SPI firewall combo, and good common sense.

GNU/Linux has made me a better (more secure) Windows user, in short, and has saved me money.

### Post by waynemeat on 2010-06-19
I believe that the downside to Linux is it's main feature; its openness!
Support for each distribution is slowly diminishing because there are so many of them.
There are two options to build a better Linux OS and these are, (1) Pumping money into a project i.e. Google Chrome or, (2) Making things more unified i.e. less distro's and more standards.

### Post by nab on 2010-06-20
Like most people here I'm using both win and ubuntu for some years. When setting up a new system I have generally the same amount of problems to get some non-standard hardware working - yes, even in win 7 or XP where drivers exist and should be working out of the box. On both OS's I have once in a while problems with security updates, but mostly because of some tweaks that where necessary for some hardware to "behave". 
For me it's just a different set of advantages and disadvantages, so I use the system that in my opinion is best suited for the task at hand.

While windows has more "business related enduser software" I really like the ideas of repros in Linux (only one place to look for updates - very very nice feature :-)).
 
And another big plus for ubuntu is that in my experience the willingness to help a not so experienced user is greater in ubuntu than in windows forums, especially with non-standard problems.

I really don't understand this one or the other. I mean when I want to go with my kids to a restaurant I choose another than going with my wife to a candle light dinner :-)

---

### Post by Jammerton on 2010-06-21
Ubuntu runs better than anything I have ever used. It has bugs sure, but you can actually fix them unlike those in windows.  To say windows is better for gaming, or that osx is more polished may be true, however I think linux is by far the most reliable and affordable os out there. Linux teaches you, the others just hold you back.  As for video games, give it a couple of years, besides, I have a ps3, much less of a headache than windows gaming.

### Post by OooBuntuRox on 2010-06-21
> **waynemeat said:**
> I believe that the downside to Linux is it's main feature; its openness!
Support for each distribution is slowly diminishing because there are so many of them.
There are two options to build a better Linux OS and these are, (1) Pumping money into a project i.e. Google Chrome or, (2) Making things more unified i.e. less distro's and more standards.

You have something of a good point. It pays to pick an OS that has been around longer: Suse (Novell), OpenSolaris (SUN), FreeBSD (Based on Berkley's BSD), etc. Although I have found that bigger names seem to be more network oriented. Hence, fewer peripheral drivers. Ubuntu seems much more end-user/ internet oriented.

While I have used MS (and others) for decades... and I still use MS (mainly because it comes with new computers), I have come to love and appreciate Ubuntu. 

Depending on an individuals point of view and how anyone uses their particular os is what determines if the openness is a plus or a drawback. If you frequently reconfigure your hardware, etc You may forever be on the phone getting reinstall permission from MS. That is a huge waste of my time. I use MS as it is meant to be used and I use GNU/ Linux for the potential it has to offer.

As many have said earlier: Go with what YOU like best and don't worry so much about what others choose to use. They all have pro's and con's.

OooBuntuRox :guitar:

### Post by Phil Binner on 2010-06-22
I built a Ubuntu machine out of old bits and pieces about 2 years ago. I'm better with hardware than software, you understand. At the time I didn't think Ubuntu was ready, and I had a lot of trouble getting it working properly. I succeeded eventually because of the forums - this forum mainly.

I have just updated my laptop because of accidental damage, and because of the deals the insurance company make I had to buy from PC world or Currys. This means I had the choice of a very expensive Apple, or a good spec Windows 7 Machine. I got a Dell Windows 7 machine, bet because of compatibility issues - serious ones which wreck the machine to the point you have to take it back to factory settings - it has been re-built 4 times. It is now almost a month later, and I only finished the last re-build today. I have now left out ZoneAlarm to avoid getting the Toredo Tunnelling Pseudo Interface, and it seems to finally be right. It's goodby to my 2 year license for Zone Alarm though, and I had to pay Dell £129 to provide the software support that was necessarry to find out what was wrong.

The point of this tale of woe is this; it isn't only Ubuntu that has bugs, and when they do occur in Ubuntu you dont have to put your wallet on the internet to sort it out. Ubuntu, for all it's clunkyness is way more stable than Windows, and the new laptop with 4GB RAM and a P5 processor has only just caught up with my box of bits with an old pentium processor and about 1/2 gig RAM as far as speed is concerned.

I don't even agree that Ubuntu is all that far behind any more. Mozilla is doing some great apps that I use on Windows as well as Ubuntu, and they are very similar on each. My belief is that if Open Office continues to improve, then all that is required to tip the balance is for the "Places" windows to be made more like Windows Explorer (the XP version, not the new one which is cr*p) and a decent device manager to be provided, for me to abandon Windows altogether.

### Post by anoknusa on 2010-06-22
What the is all this?  I go to one blog or forum thread and find people complaining that Ubuntu's GNOME tweaks and KDE's Plasma interface are too much like Windows or Mac OS.  I go to another blog or forum, and people complain that it isn't enough like the latter two.  With regard to this thread: who cares about this "competition" business?  Yes, Windows 7 is a pretty slick improvement; I have a copy myself.  Yes, OS X is a pretty interface.  But in terms of functionality, my Kubuntu setup allows me to do absolutely anything I might want to do on Windows or Mac, and I'm sticking with it.  And polish? My Plasma setup looks and functions the way I want to, and offers more than a choice between blue and orange or a single window frame design.  The fact is, this is an mindless (and endless) debate fueled by people suckered by smooth marketing and branding, doomed before it ever began.  In the end, what you want is out there so long as you're willing to go get it.  You wan't functionality, security and the ability to customize your interface to the nines? As long as you're willing to go through the pain in the *** of playing around with it and learn a thing or two, go with Linux.  You want something that works right out of the box and has top-notch, professional customer support just a phone call and half a world away,? As long as you're willing to risk the time and expense of viruses and the like, go with Windows.  You want all of what Windows has, but wanna fight the corporate evil that is Microsoft? Give your $1500 to humble multi-billion dollar corporation Apple, and get a machine of comparable power and efficiency to a $500 Dell or HP.  You wanna play games?  Do what I did, and buy an Xbox 360 for $150--half the price of a Windows DVD.  You want what you want, and don't give a damn what these hipster assholes have to say about your computer?  Good on ya; good on ya.

Long rant short: I don't give a damn about competition. I've got what I want, it looks and functions exactly as I want, and it didn't cost me a penny. Screw the Big 2.

---

### Post by Lykopis on 2010-06-27
Though I haven't read all of this thread I have seen several posts like it.
First I would like to say I have used some flavor of Linux for about 5 years now.
Before that I used MS-Windows.
XP was the last MS-Windows OS I had used till I bought a new laptop with MS-Windows 7.
 It soon got Ubuntu installed, But to understand the pain I had trying to install Linux....Well
I ended up having to compile a custom Kernel because this machine was not supported under Linux. 
The point is this:
If it's broke in Linux you can get the source and fix it.
Try that in windows---- Sorry your out of luck, you have to depend on some hardware manufacture to fix it or M$ to do it for you.

So why would I throw out a "good" MS-Windows OEM install, compile a custom Linux kernel, test it, reinstall it  till it was stable for me?
Well first it took some hacker about 3 days from my purchase time to break into my MS-Windows 7 system.
(yes whoever did it left foot prints)
So much for MS-Windows 7 firewall :rolleyes: !! 
Security issues have always plagued Windows. I can't say I trust my personal data to MS-Windows with its Malware, Virus and Security problems.

For those of you who chose to use Window$ if it works for you go for it, 
but don't complain at us that use Linux.
Don't be jealous because we may "know" more than you.
Or because we don't run with the crowd. 
You made your choice just as we made ours, 
and each of us may have very different reasons.
For me Linux is a winner hands down.

---

### Post by lancest on 2010-06-27
> **Lykopis said:**
> 
So why would I throw out a "good" M$-Window$ OEM install, compile a custom Linux kernel, test it, reinstall it  till it was stable for me?
Well first it took some hacker about 3 days from my purchase time to break into my M$-Window$ 7 system.
(yes whoever did it left foot prints)
So much for M$-Window$ 7 firewall :rolleyes: !!

For those of you who chose to use Window$ if it works for you go for it, 
but don't complain at us that use Linux.
Don't be jealous because we may "know" more than you.
Or because we don't run with the crowd. 
You made your choice just as we made ours, 
and each of us may have very different reasons.
For me Linux is a winner hands down.

IMHO there is some connection to this: 
[What is Maker Culture? - DIY Roots]("http://www.associatedcontent.com/article/1528122/what_is_maker_culture_diy_roots.html")
By Todd McCall
Maker culture is a term that refers to a growing community of hobbyists and professionals dedicated to making their own functional devices, whether it be technological gadgets, open source hardware and software, fashion apparel, home decorating, or nearly any other aspect of physical life. The movement stems from a direct reaction to a consumer culture in which most products have become steadily homogenized and local industry has given into big box retailing of dull products made with cheap foreign labor. One of the results of the culture shift has been the fact that some of us have forgotten how to make hardly anything other than dinner and house plants, and a few of us have even forgotten how to do that much.

### Post by WinRiddance on 2010-06-28
> **arrow.69 said:**
>  **I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.** What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?


**Well, as far as I'm concerned the text in bold says it all.** You've obviously never even tried to fix all of the "bugs" that have been bothering you ... or finding ways to replace anything and everything Windows related with Ubuntu software. You talk about using Linux/Ubuntu off and on for four years which in my opinion gives you the experience of 2 to 3 years worth of usage ... and if you're not a professional user, or a professional technician, or someone who's commercially employed to actually work with computers for a living 40+ hours per week, then I can only give very little creedence to what you've had to say in your post.


When I made my switch to Ubuntu **after 18 years** of professional experience with every version of Windows, I promised myself that _this would not be a half-baked attempt_ by me but that I would make every effort for at least a few months to either do away with Windows once and for all, period, or to do the opposite by never trying Linux/Ubuntu ever again.


Well, with a few months of patience, searching the web for the perfect software apps (which can't all be found in the software center), and having the patience to become much more familiar with the inner workings of Linux/Ubuntu, I stand here today to say that as far as I'm concerned nothing compares with Ubuntu ... **absolutely nothing !!!**
[COLOR=DarkRed]**But why ... ???**[/COLOR]


Because no other operating system (for Joe general public) on this planet provides you **with this high level** of freedom, security, stability, help resources, commercially viable software, and low cost budget **all combined** ... FOR FREE. Matter of fact, just earlier today I had the devil of a time getting a high speed third party 10 Gbit wireless card (from Europe) to work on my dad's Windows7 machine which worked instantly as soon as I installed Ubuntu on another partition. So what does that say for your precious Windows7. Is eye candy (ever use Compiz) or games all that you're interested in ??? In my opinion most people, **perhaps yourself included**, have totally forgotten what a pain in the butt Windows used to be and at times can still be, even with Windows7. People have forgotten because they've gotten totally used to Windows ticks over several, sometimes even many years. Often people have just gotten used to the problems or viruses or crashes or hardware problems over time ... so that when Ubuntu doesn't work flawlessly right out of the box or after a little bit of fiddling in the first few weeks, they come up with comments such as yours. If you're gonna compare, you need to compare **the entire Operating System environment to be fair about it**, and not just your very own personal experience for the specific things that you want to accomplish.

Ever try Ubuntu with a Virtual Box and Windows installed in that ???

### Post by callowschoolboy on 2010-06-28
I haven't read all the responses so I hope I'm not repeating too bad here.  In another thread, "What do you hate about Ubuntu" everyone responded "Nothing, if I hated something I changed it to do what I want it to do."  Isn't that fundamental to Linux, that we could change it locally and if need be and we were that passionate we'd help develop a fix?!  A little time is well worth the outrageous cost of a more buggy, vulnerable and poorly designed OS!  And to add insult to injury is the sentiment that you just have to get the newest thing Microsoft or Apple comes up with.
This is like arguing whether to buy Chinese goods, in that everyone's doing so and the quality is low.  Except, imagine that the slipshod Chinese good cost a great deal of money and the one made in your home country cost nothing!  Just as Chinese exports can have health hazards, like toys, toothpaste and now drywall, running Windows is just begging to get your system crashed or worse; that's what gave me the motivation to finally switch and I wish I'd had the courage to do so years ago.

### Post by UUU3 on 2010-06-28
> **arrow.69 said:**
> I'm gonna get incredibly bashed for posting this, but here it goes.

I've been running various Linux distributions on a bunch of computers since I was 13.  Windows would **** me off, Vista got released, or sometimes I just felt like playing around.  I've been through MEPIS, OpenSuse, Kubuntu, Ubuntu,amongst others.  It eventually became clear to me that Ubuntu was the overall best desktop distribution out there, if only for the tremendous amount of support there is out there for it.

I love Ubuntu.  It's a very capable OS and I love the idea of running a completely free and open operating system.  I've probably run some variation of Ubuntu on and off for the past four years.  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

However, I have to admit that Ubuntu simply can't compete anymore.  Windows 7 is a huge leap for Windows, and OS X has always been user-friendly and functional.  I know that under the hood, Linux is more stable, quicker, more secure than Windows.  But the truth is, to the end-user on the desktop front, the experience you get running Ubuntu doesn't measure up.  The problem isn't a lack of applications of features.  Ubuntu just lacks polish and integration.  Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users.  I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability.  Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer.  They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system.  They want it to just work for them.  Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now.

What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

Windows always starts off great. Sort of like greased lightening on steroids. But I swear that its programmed to slow down  as time moves on. It degenerates and makes your PC go slower and slower... (I think it suffers from OS rust! Soon it doesn't shine anymore!) Then you get thinking well of course, I need more horse power and you go out and buy it. It satisfies windows for a while and then the whole cycle starts again!
Thats the ONLY thing that I like about windows is that it has pushed the PC hardware performance envelope.
And UBUNTU? It just keeps on performin screamin fast on your old PC!
Window??? Just say NO forever!!!

---

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2010-06-29
Something tells me a lot of people in this thread are more upset with Gnome than anything else. Try KDE 4 or look at Linux from a few more angles. I think you'll see that, outside of running certain niche Windows programs, Linux will meet your needs.

If there's one program you don't use too often that you still need, just dual boot. If you have the space, there's nothing to lose by having more than one OS. This is the main thing I don't understand- the people who love Ubuntu, and Linux in general, but since it can't play one Windows games or run one productivity application, they get rid of it entirely and force themselves to use just Windows. It seems a tad bit ludicrous when you could have the best of Linux and still keep the tiny necessities Windows offers you.

It all depends on what you primarily use your computer for. However, the fact is that most common computer users are totally satisfied, and even overwhelmed, with the capability of Ubuntu. Not everyone is a Creative Suite freak or gamer. Most people who use computers these days are casual browsers and people who use it to manage their mp3 players.

### Post by Lykopis on 2010-06-30
As I said MS-Word up at least to XP did install and run on Wine. I had someone bring a desktop needing MS-Word on a Mandriva box, (though I can't imagine why) I installed it and they used it under Wine.
MS-Office installer is listed under Wine's Top-10 Platinum List:
meaning -- Applications which install and run flawlessly on an out-of-the-box Wine  installation.

Photoshop I have myself run under Wine. (Gimp never really made the grade for me) Yes it has some issues but that wouldn't effect a typical user. CS4 AKA 11.0 is listed as silver:
Meaning--- Applications with minor issues that do not affect typical usage.
(The old Photoshop 7.XX line did run flawlessly on Wine.)

No Wine is not a perfect replacement for Windows but It can help someone making the transition from Windows to Linux.

> Most popular games have *not* been ported to Linux.I haven't gamed since the days of Quake 3. I know for a fact Solider of Fortune was ported to Linux, as was Quake up to at least Quake 3, for us old timers Doom also was ported to Linux. I have played Doom, Quake 2 and Quake 3 Linux ports. It would be my guess there are others by now, though I might be wrong as MS-Windows and X-Box are the biggest game machines right now. If your a PC gamer then yeah as I said earlier MS-Windows is understandable.

### Post by betrunkenaffe on 2010-07-08
> **aviedw said:**
> Should it have to compete? Does competition always drive innovation? Within capitalist USA (my country) New York (state) this has become a widely accepted idea. But what i have experienced in the few years that i have been with Linux is a OS that thrives, progresses and adapts despite its  stock quote, market share or target group and all the other jargon that's out there. Truthfully at this point i would rather pay the developers of Ubuntu than go back to Windows or Mac OS.

Necessity drives innovation, sometimes competition drives necessity, but not always. Much of what is called innovation occurs due to unfulfilled needs causing a "natural" adaptation and change to fulfill those needs.

I think the main reason why many early versions of products which are popular now failed was partly due to the available technology at the time and partly due to the users available at the time. Modern users have different needs than previous users and some "older" technologies are getting pulled out of the closet and seeing light of day because the demand is finally there on a large enough scale.

### Post by chudder on 2010-07-08
Let me tell you a testimonial of three people.  

All three had been using windoze for years on their machines and as we see with windoze, it makes the computer doze after X years of use.  They tried reinstalls, complete scans with multiple programs, etc.  Only marginal improvement was shown.  

They were desperate, so they installed Ubuntu and now their computers have new life and work like a charm.  No longer take an hour to boot up.  

I'll grant that these were XP machines but the problem and solution is the same.

Microsoft likes to put everything in one "small" (actually very big) package with a lot of stuff you don't need.  All Windows OS's have one thing in common, a lot of unnecessary fluff that ends up getting in the way of an efficient system.

Don't get me wrong, I think W7 is the best OS that MS has come up with, why?  Because they finally opened it up (somewhat) to the community.  It's far from open source but it was a big step forward.  They got the community's feedback instead of a whole bunch profit driven businessman and IT guys in a smoke-filled room with Bill Gates at the end of the table.

With that said, it's still got a lot of fluff, it's more stable than previous versions, but most of it is still fluff and will only slow down the cpu with time.

MacOS X, I'm not as familiar with it but here's my two cents.  It's unix based which is very good!  However, they totally negate and downplay the power of it by making it even more closed than Windoze.  

Both OS's use enormous amounts of system resources, as evidenced in my friends' computers.  Ubuntu has minimum system requirements but they are drastically less.

Some may say this is because it's an inferior and less capable system.  I disagree entirely, in the early days of Ubuntu, I could agree with you but now it's so developed that even some major computer manufacturers have made Ubuntu machines.  Many things are not compatible with Ubuntu but usually there is a linux replacement just as well.  

Okay I'm done ;)

---

### Post by betrunkenaffe on 2010-07-09
> **aviedw said:**
> Interesting response but I'm not sure if i agree fully. If innovation is the same as adapting then i guess i can agree. But if not i would say that out of necessity we adapt to our current situations. I don't know if its is correct to say that necessity drives invocation. I doubt business like Microsoft and Intel wait until it is completely necessary to further develop their technologies. If there are any necessities in this case it would be a company's willingness to stay ahead of their competitor. Intel wont rest because AMD is right behind them (maybe not right behind)

The point that I'm simply  trying to make is that Open Source take a different approach. Even though it seems alien to modern day business practices, Linux and other open source projects have down played the profit motive game that so many of the worlds software developers participate in.

I still find this whole open source concept completely amazing. The desire to innovate simply for its own sake.

Seems to me that we are probably thinking along very similar lines. When I say necessity, you have to think about what drives a business (to make money and grow). While a company could just "make money", the desire to grow and make more money makes it necessary to make alternative products that that can charge more for. Consumers being what they are won't buy the same product forever without some feature or benefit to provide incentive.

The act of adapting something doesn't mean innovation has occurred however the need to change something is required for innovation. I hope that made sense. All good inventions fulfill a need that wasn't previously there, all software projects are the same. While another project might be similar, there was something about the others that the initial developers felt needed improvement/changed or they disagreed on how it should be developped.

The point about Open Source not following the same path is definitely true and I agree with the view which is that software should be available to all and should be customizable as a user needs.

### Post by aysiu on 2010-07-18
> **aviedw said:**
> Why does every new user make such a big deal about the command line? Because it's intimidating. It's like being in a foreign country whose language you don't speak. It's a lot easier to walk into a bakery and point at the cake you like than to learn how to properly pronounce all the words for "I would like to buy this cake."

The terminal is very unforgiving, which also makes it even more intimidating for those who haven't used it before. It's case-sensitive and space-sensitive. Many new users make simple mistakes in the terminal that are frustrating but ones veterans would never make. ```
ls /home
``` somehow becomes ```
ls home
``` or ```
1s/home
``` Pointing and clicking is a lot easier for a beginner, even if it takes more time, because there is memorization involved. You have discoverability.

> Once you learn the command line you will realize that it's one of Unix's greatest assets. Once you refuse to learn the command line, you will realize that pretty much everything in Ubuntu can be accomplished via the GUI and having the option of both GUI and CLI for the same task is one of Linux's greatest assets.

Forcing CLI on someone who cares nothing for it does no one any good.

---

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2010-07-18
I haven't used the command line out of necessity since... Jaunty, I think. Things really have changed. However, I HAVE had to run disk check on a friend's Windows machine from their terminal, and it was quite daunting, seeing as how it wasn't bash. It's so unfamiliar... er, I mean "it's not user friendly!"

Ahem, you see the hypocrisy?

Oh, and for those who want a polished music player, may I suggest Amarok? It's quite a lot better than anything I've seen on Windows. ### Post by MattBD on 2010-07-18
> **viking1au said:**
> Two things will make me a permanent Linux Ubuntu user; 1; get rid of the Terminal, all things should be done with GUI, just as Microsoft basically got rid of the requirement for users to understand DOS. -- 2; Develop a truly DECENT Multi Media player that works, say at least as well as Win Media Player 11. --- Programs like Rythmbox & Banshee fall way short; of the 2 they should give Banshee a solid overhaul & get it up to par.
](*,)
Get rid of the terminal? That would be a terrible idea. First of all, the shell is an extremely efficient interface. It allows a far more fine-grained level of control over settings than any GUI will ever be able to.

Comparing it with the DOS prompt is also wrong. Look at the nightmare that is the Windows registry - you don't need to use the command line to edit that, but it's insanely complex for newcomers. At least a configuration file is generally well-commented and it's reasonably clear what does what. The main reasons Windows users can often get by without using the DOS prompt is because it is crap compared to the shell, and many Windows users don't know **** about how to use their computers.

Finally, need I remind you that Mac OS X, a system constantly praised for its graphical ease of use, is not only a form of Unix, but also has a terminal? No-one ever complains that OS X needs to get rid of the terminal? If anything I've had to use the terminal more on OS X than on Linux because OS X doesn't have a package manager, so where on Ubuntu I could have used Synaptic to install a few Python modules, on OS X I have to install them from the command line.

### Post by libssd on 2010-07-18
> **aysiu said:**
> Once you refuse to learn the command line, you will realize that pretty much everything in Ubuntu can be accomplished via the GUI and having the option of both GUI and CLI for the same task is one of Linux's greatest assets.

Forcing CLI on someone who cares nothing for it does no one any good.
Nobody is forcing the command line on anybody. You can install Ubuntu without ever opening a terminal session just as you can install Mac OS X or Windows 7 with nothing but the GUI. Some more advanced things in any of these operating systems can be done more easily from the command line. Experienced users often give people instructions to do things at the command line, which I think is a mistake for Linux newbies, but it's almost never a requirement. Old habits die hard.

My mother is 89, and if she ever started making serious noises about getting a computer, I would prefer to set up a cheap machine with Ubuntu or Mint for her, rather than leave her to the continuing nightmare of Windows security updates, or even Mac OS X. I can lock down Linux more easily, and it's easier to tailor it to the needs of an elderly user, who may not have top notch vision or dexterity with a mouse.

### Post by aviedw on 2010-07-19
> **aysiu said:**
> Because it's intimidating. It's like being in a foreign country whose language you don't speak. It's a lot easier to walk into a bakery and point at the cake you like than to learn how to properly pronounce all the words for "I would like to buy this cake."

The terminal is very unforgiving, which also makes it even more intimidating for those who haven't used it before. It's case-sensitive and space-sensitive. Many new users make simple mistakes in the terminal that are frustrating but ones veterans would never make. ```
ls /home
``` somehow becomes ```
ls home
``` or ```
1s/home
``` Pointing and clicking is a lot easier for a beginner, even if it takes more time, because there is memorization involved. You have discoverability.

 Once you refuse to learn the command line, you will realize that pretty much everything in Ubuntu can be accomplished via the GUI and having the option of both GUI and CLI for the same task is one of Linux's greatest assets.

Forcing CLI on someone who cares nothing for it does no one any good.

I agree with some of your points but i will use the bakery analogy to show where i disagree. I must point out that this comparison will always be slighted simply because we look at Linux as a deviation from windows. From something we seem to simply know to something that we seem to have to learn. 

But if a person was to visit a country and come upon a bakery in order to communicate it would at first be easier to just point to the object. But if a person decides to live in that country and become an active member of that society I'm sure they would strive to do more than just pointing at objects to convey what they wish they could save verbally. I think a person in that situation would find it beneficial to learn the language. 

The example is a little far reaching but the point is that the reason the command line seems threatening is because we weren't raised on it. I know i haven't. Since i can remember I've been pointing and clicking. 

I will agree that the GUI concept has made working with computers easier to grasp but from what i have experienced it doesn't always make them productive or make best use of the computer system. 

Windows and Mac tuck their command line away very nicely. 

But what i have also realized is that the constant innovations of the GUI is what drives the development of the surrounding systems. The more complex the GUI becomes the more graphics rendering it will require or processing power. But this is another issue altogether. 

Simply put if your going to visit a foreign country or culture have enough respect to learn the language without expecting it to change to your own.

### Post by aysiu on 2010-07-19
> **aviedw said:**
> I agree with some of your points but i will use the bakery analogy to show where i disagree. I must point out that this comparison will always be slighted simply because we look at Linux as a deviation from windows. From something we seem to simply know to something that we seem to have to learn. 

But if a person was to visit a country and come upon a bakery in order to communicate it would at first be easier to just point to the object. But if a person decides to live in that country and become an active member of that society I'm sure they would strive to do more than just pointing at objects to convey what they wish they could save verbally. I think a person in that situation would find it beneficial to learn the language. 

The example is a little far reaching but the point is that the reason the command line seems threatening is because we weren't raised on it. I know i haven't. Since i can remember I've been pointing and clicking. I fully agree with you to the extent that the analogy I presented holds up. Every analogy breaks down at some point. Yes, I think people should embrace the terminal. What I'm trying to present is *why* people are afraid of it in the first place. Yes, if someone has been "living" in Ubuntu country for a year and is still afraid of the terminal, I think that person needs to be a little more open-minded. But I'm trying to explain why newcomers are afraid of the terminal, and it makes sense. It's a language they don't know.

It is worth noting, though, where the analogy does break down.

First of all, even when you are learning a new language in a foreign country, people can generally understand you if you pronounce something a little wrong or use the wrong word in a sentence. The terminal will not understand anything you do if you type something even a little wrong. It is really unforgiving.

Secondly, even though a lot of Linux users like the terminal, Gnome and KDE are both very much part of Linux culture as well, and point-and-click approaches to tasks (things like Synaptic Package Manager or Boot-up Manager) are not any less Linux than the bash terminal is. So saying they have to adjust to Linux moving from Windows could just as easily mean they adjust to new ways of pointing and clicking than to typing instead of pointing and clicking.

### Post by hyper84 on 2010-07-20

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2010-07-21
I suggest a good portion of the people in this thread contribute to LUK (Linux Unified Kernel) or try [http://www.linux-xp.com/](http://www.linux-xp.com/). Perhaps this is what you're looking for.

The goal of Linux is efficiency, beauty, and innovation. If that means that it's not as familiar to Windows users and has a few better ways of doing things, fine. It's for the sake of producing the best software to be used, not being held back by false traditions and innane ways of doing things.

So, let's focus on the bugs, the gaps in functionality, the issues which are inhibiting our Linux experience- not the issues which are keeping it from being the next Windows. Because that's one thing I think we don't want.

I'm not assuming this is the idea of the majority of people here, but a lot of people sound a bit naive. Because of Gnomenu and emerald, they think everything in Linux can be made more Windows-like, just like GUI customization. However, it's foolish to try to make Linux into something it's not.

You can't just expect to have a free, open source, stable, secure, fast, easy, and simple Windows out of this. Not only is it disrespectful, but it's misguided. You can get REALLY FREAKING CLOSE, though. I think that's where most of the angst comes from- people just barely not being able to replicate the benefits of Linux with the exact same experience otherwise they had in Windows or OS X.

If you started out using Linux, rather than something else, you might have a different mindset of what's holding it back, and what can be improved. Migration assistance and format compatibility are good ideas, however.

You can have your opinion, but try to see what developers are working on and talk to them directly once you learn a thing or two. That's how things actually HAPPEN with open source. Developers see people whining on forums all the time, but that's not the kind of feedback they heed most attentively- that's just the paparazzi. They pay attention to people who research, investigate, and want to improve it.

To be quite blunt, if all you're going to do is complain, and not seek a solution, you're not helping yourself (no emotional satisfaction, just disappointment once you're done griping), you're not helping developers, since much of what you say is subjective and opinionated, and you're wasting everyone's time. If you want emotional support, go eat a fatboy, or watch a movie explosion. Write some poetry.

And for the rest of you, good work! I'm glad we're actually talking about some viable and relevant areas in which we could improve our OS.

---

### Post by Nick_Jinn on 2010-07-24

### Post by GaearGrimsrud on 2010-07-25
> **arrow.69 said:**
> I'm gonna get incredibly bashed for posting this, but here it goes.

I've been running various Linux distributions on a bunch of computers since I was 13.  Windows would **** me off, Vista got released, or sometimes I just felt like playing around.  I've been through MEPIS, OpenSuse, Kubuntu, Ubuntu,amongst others.  It eventually became clear to me that Ubuntu was the overall best desktop distribution out there, if only for the tremendous amount of support there is out there for it.

I love Ubuntu.  It's a very capable OS and I love the idea of running a completely free and open operating system.  I've probably run some variation of Ubuntu on and off for the past four years.  I usually run Ubuntu for six months to a year until something forces me to go back to Windows or I just get too annoyed at a few bugs.  Whenever a new release comes out I usually check it out, eager to see what improvements have come along.

However, I have to admit that Ubuntu simply can't compete anymore.  Windows 7 is a huge leap for Windows, and OS X has always been user-friendly and functional.  I know that under the hood, Linux is more stable, quicker, more secure than Windows.  But the truth is, to the end-user on the desktop front, the experience you get running Ubuntu doesn't measure up.  The problem isn't a lack of applications of features.  Ubuntu just lacks polish and integration.  Say what you will, the amount of bugs, small crashes that happen for seemingly no reason, lack of conformity across the OS, destroy the experience for most users.  I think Ubuntu will never be a viable option for the consumer because it trades off quality, functionality and intuitiveness for customizability.  Most users just want a nice OS with features designed to make their life easier on the computer.  They want all those features out of the box and they don't want to tinker with their operating system.  They want it to just work for them.  Anyways, no amount of customization or tweaking will bring Ubuntu up to the level of functionality Windows or OS X offer right now.

What do you guys think?  Am I missing something?  Why do you prefer Ubuntu?

I have to agree. My experience is similar to yours. I used Linux exclusively, no Windows at all, never used a Mac, for over four years between 2003 and 2008. I installed and used a lot of distros, Ubuntu, OpenSUSE, Fedora, Debian, Slackware, Arch among others. Plus I've used all Windows releases from 3.1 to 7, plus MS-DOS.
I tried Vista, to see how awful it was. I truthfully never had any problems with it. I may be the only person who didn't, but I doubt that. When Windows 7 was released I tried it and that was the end of my 'Linux Only' life. It was like a breath of fresh air. I'd got so used of having to use a terminal to configure this or that. Having to search for solutions to dependency hell. Having to manually install the necessary packages whatever so I could have multi-media capabilities, please I do understand why you have to do that with Linux. Having to use dreadful Open Office. All this had become just a normal way of doing things. I was no longer a computer user. Wasting time fixing or trying to fix so many little annoyances. I, like many other Linux regulars, just accepted that it was how things were. 
I removed all Linux and started to actually be productive most of the time instead of some of the time.
So I decided to install and dual boot Ubuntu 10.04. I wanted to see how things had improved in a couple of years. Not much, if at all, is the answer. The same dull Gnome desktop. At least KDE have tried to accept that this is now the 21st century. I still need to use a terminal. I can, a new user won't probably even know what a terminal is. Open Office doesn't seem to have been updated since I last used it in 2008! I un-installed it straight away. Even Firefox and Thunderbird work better in Windows. Because Mozilla know the majority of their users are Windows users.
Sorry all you Ubuntu zealots, it will **never** be a match for Windows for the average everyday user. Don't forget it's them who are the main users of computers not us. It's them that mainstream Linux distros need to target if they truly want to shake the foundations at Redmond and Cupertino. 
That isn't going to happen. People, wrongly I know, equate 'Free' with 'Cheap'. It's how society is. It teaches us that if you want something that does the job properly you have to pay for it. That may not be correct but it's how things are. 
Until distro providers grasp that nettle Linux will continue to be the 'cheap' alternative and that's a great shame.

---

Kind of odd that the poster above me joined and this is his first and only post.....Sometimes I might be a bit paranoid, but I do know that people join to post spam as well as promote products and propaganda.




So I got a web connect stick from T-mobile.....$40 for 1 month, high speeds to 5gb then dial up speeds unlimited.....Go deal.


Was it easier to get going in Windows? Nope. It worked in Windows 7. It took about 25 minutes for the program to download, install and then activate. It ran though a bunch of garbage then I FINALLY got online.


What was my linux experience like? Did I have to use the command line or compile software sources by hand? NOPE! I plugged the thing in and hit "connect" and I was online in 5 seconds....5 seconds against 25 minutes in Windows 7.

My benchmarks? All benchmarks faster in Mint than Windows 7.

Comes with DVD burning software and torrent software out of the box....comes with open office....comes with tools to reformat my disks....comes wtih tools to salvage my virus ridden windows partition.....Everything just worked out of the box. 



Its only when you have the odd piece of hardware or you want to get creative and go above and beyond the default limitations that you have to start compiling drivers and use commands.





I have been replacing peoples WIndows systems with Linux when they bring their computers to me loaded with viruses....and you know what? When they get back their LINUX operating system, after I do all the work of installing it for them, they are THRILLED. They love it and then they tell their friends about me....And I am no linux guru. I just picked it up in my spare time and am still a noob to many around here, but apparently Im good enough to get a linux machine off the ground functioning better than WIndows. 


Windows doesnt give you problems off the bat....it slowly slows down and breaks as its riddled with viruses....the opposite is true with Linux. Getting going is the hard part. Once you have it installed nicely (its what I do) you get it back and viruses are a non-issue....not so in windows.

---

### Post by Reallyold on 2010-07-25
I really like what Nick (the most recent response) says here, and I sense accuracy in it even with my very limited Ubuntu experience. But I know what the original poster is saying.....Ubuntu looks like it was built by geeks for geeks, and all the silly naming conventions were cute in the early days I suppose but now they are just irritating to a new user, especially someone who has become used to Windows terminology. 

Example....I just spent a good half-day trying to find where my USB Western Digital drive went. (It's where I installed Ubuntu). I skimmed through the nice user manual and searched forums to find out why my drive was (apparently) not recognized. Finally I found it in the file manager under the name "HOST". My old "C" drive was named "80GB drive" or something like that. (I found that quickly because it sort of made sense).  But I had to do a search for a file name I knew was out there (should have thought of that earlier) in order to see the name of the drive in the file structure. Why wasn't the WD drive named something close, like "120GB drive" or "Western Digital Drive"? 

Then there were all the other folders that had names I simply could not relate to.  This is what makes Ubuntu difficult to get into. Yes I am sick of Windows. As the man said, after a few years it was getting really slow (it felt like 1995 all over again). It had a new malware/spyware/pest whatever you call 'em, every couple of days. I was really really ready to dump Windows.  But I also do not want to spend valuable weeks and months learning what all these dumb names mean. Why don't they just call the thing "File Manager" instead of "Alfred" (or whatever..I still haven't memorized it)?

Yes in my early days it was fun and a challenge but I've had enough of that. Now I want "Easy". I'll probably stay with this OS and I'll learn what I have to because I do like it already, but gosh darn it why don't the developers start making it more intuitive? I think that would make it much more popular and gather in a whole lot more users. 

(Puff puff wheeze...I'm done now)

---

### Post by Nick_Jinn on 2010-07-26
Mint is probably better than Ubuntu for a very beginner....less work getting it to do the basics.  It plays DVDs instantly, and the DVD version already has flash to watch youtube. You can get there in Ubuntu, but its one less step in Mint.


Yeah, for geeks there isnt much advantage to mint, but for noobs OR people who just dont like wasting time even if they COULD customize it easily, Mint is really the way to go.....I sort of think Ubuntu should have been what Mint is since it wanted to reach out to everyone.....ironically Mint doesnt, or doesnt claim to....and yet the changes Mint made are the changes that is helping get Ubuntu onto the PCs that previously had windows. 


If anyone is frustrated with Ubuntu, maybe try Mint or PCLinux or even GOS....Mint is my first choice because its the closest to Ubuntu and yet has tools that help encourage you to access the customization features easily. Before the Mint menu I was intimidated to go crawing through the system menus.  Now its easy (always was, but its a less intimidating setup).

I still use regular Ubuntu for Ubuntu Studio and maybe for MythTV next.....better and cheaper than a DVR.



Anyway. I live in the Bay Area. There are people here way more experienced than me, but I can help get peoples systems going taking the most frustrating parts out of peoples experience....People like it better than if they tried to do it themselves.

---

### Post by aviedw on 2010-07-26
What windows suffers from is bloat. There are just so many lines of code and not enough developers going through it before its released so when we get the new shiny OS, we also get a service pack like the next month to fix issues that they should have dealt with before it was released. Xp in my opinion didn't become a truly solid OS until service pack 3. And it will probably take the same if not more for windows 7. 

But from going from Windows to Linux, should expect a learning curve. Now if the questions is, should there be a learning curve? I think its fair to say that yes there should be only so that linux maintains its individuality. And just take your time. The forum is here for any and all questions. Most issues that have popped up already have solutions for them. 

It took me a while to fully grasp linux. The way in which programs were installed drove me crazy. I just was so use to downloading a program and double clicking it and watching all these windows pop up telling the app is being installed. But now i cant see myself having to do that unless its something very specific and i have to compile it because it hasn't been packaged yet. 

Just learn little by little start with what you use to do and venture out. Linux offers so much out of the box.

---

### Post by Nick_Jinn on 2010-07-26
I am somebody who desires to learn more and go deeper, even though I am a noob.

However, I cant help but notice that there is sometimes animosity towards people who want something as easy to use as Windows and want it for free.....first of all, some things about linux ALREADY ARE EASIER than windows, while others things harder.....Its setting up and getting linux to work that is hard...but unless you keep tinkering it should be a REALLY solid and long lasting system. Windows is slow and tedious yet idiot proof, but you dont get a stable system. You get viruses and problems, even with Norton installed.....in fact right now I have a computer that is struck with a virus that Norton couldnt catch or fix, and I am giving them back a dual boot with Mint.

What noobs need to get that easy to use free OS is grunts like me who are willing to spend their time doing the basics for them, elevating their system from default to a comfortable base level that most people will want....after I ask them their habits and needs and evaluate their hardware.


People seem to love the systems I set up for them better than windows....that is why I keep getting phone calls. That and because i work so cheap.
